**Definition:** Damping achieved by immersing a light disc/vanes into a viscous fluid (typically a high-viscosity oil such as silicone oil). The viscous drag between the fluid layers produces a damping torque.

> [!NOTE]
> This is an enhanced form of air friction damping, using a liquid instead of air. Because liquids are more viscous, the damping effect is significantly stronger than air friction.

**Real-world analogy:** A spoon stirring thick honey. The honey resists the spoon's motion, smoothly slowing it down.

**Key Features:**
- Damping torque $T_d = c \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ (same form as air friction).
- Higher damping coefficient than air.
- Used in recording instruments and electrostatic voltmeters.

---

### 1.2.3 Eddy Current Damping (Electromagnetic Damping)
**Definition:** Damping achieved by inducing eddy currents in a conducting former (typically a metal disc or former) when it moves through a magnetic field. According to **Lenz's Law**, these induced currents oppose the motion, producing a braking (damping) torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** Eddy current damping is the *most efficient* and *most commonly used* damping method in modern PMMC and induction-type instruments.

**Real-world analogy:** A heavy metal pendulum swinging between the poles of a strong magnet. The magnetic field induces currents in the metal, and the pendulum comes to rest very quickly compared to one swinging in open air. This is the principle behind roller-coaster magnetic brakes.

**Key Features:**
- Damping torque $T_d = K_1 \cdot i_e^2$, where $i_e$ is the induced eddy current.
- Since $i_e \propto \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$, effectively $T_d = K_2 \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$.
- Requires a permanent magnetic field — **cannot be used in AC moving iron instruments** (because the AC field would distort the working field).

---

## 1.3 Measurement of Voltage and Current

### 1.3.1 Moving Coil (PMMC) Instrument
**Definition:** A Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument is one in which a current-carrying coil placed in the uniform magnetic field of a permanent magnet experiences a deflecting torque, and the deflection is indicated by a pointer attached to the coil.

> [!NOTE]
> Also known as **D'Arsonval Galvanometer** (in its basic form). It works **only on DC** (or AC when used with a rectifier).

### 1.3.2 Moving Iron (MI) Instrument
**Definition:** A Moving Iron instrument is one in which a piece of soft iron moves in response to a magnetic field produced by the current flowing through a stationary coil. The deflection depends on the current's magnitude, irrespective of its direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> MI instruments can be used for both **AC and DC**. They are the most common AC panel meters in industry.

---

## 1.4 Range Extension

### Formal Definition
**Range extension** is the technique of extending the measurement range of a basic galvanometer or PMMC movement to measure currents much higher than its full-scale deflection current (ammeter) or voltages much higher than its full-scale voltage (voltmeter).

### 1.4.1 Shunts (for Ammeters)
A **shunt** is a low-value, high-precision resistance connected in **parallel** with the meter movement to bypass the excess current. Used to extend current range.

### 1.4.2 Multipliers (for Voltmeters)
A **multiplier** is a high-value, high-precision resistance connected in **series** with the meter movement to drop the excess voltage. Used to extend voltage range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A shunt is like a "bypass lane" on a highway — most of the traffic (current) takes the wider, easier bypass, while a known small portion goes through the meter. A multiplier is like a "speed bump" before the meter — it absorbs the excess "energy" (voltage) so the meter only sees a safe amount.

### Standard Performance Metrics
- **Full-Scale Deflection Current (Ifsd or Im):** The current that produces maximum pointer deflection. Typical values: **25 µA to 50 mA**.
- **Meter Internal Resistance (Rm):** The coil resistance of the PMMC. Typical values: **50 Ω to 2 kΩ**.
- **Ammeter Resistance (Ra):** Should ideally be **zero** (so it does not disturb the circuit).
- **Voltmeter Resistance (Rv):** Should ideally be **infinite** (so it draws no current from the circuit).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Equation of Motion of a Moving System

For the moving system of any indicating instrument, the equation of motion is:

$$J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + D \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + K \cdot \theta = T_d$$

Where:
- $J$ = Moment of inertia of the moving system (kg·m²)
- $D$ = Damping constant (N·m·s/rad)
- $K$ = Spring constant of the control system (N·m/rad)
- $\theta$ = Angular deflection (rad)
- $T_d$ = Instantaneous deflecting torque (N·m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **damping torque** $T_{damping} = D \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ is always *opposing* the motion. It is zero at rest and maximum during fast motion.

### Conditions of Damping
- **Underdamped:** $D^2 < 4JK$ — pointer oscillates before settling.
- **Critically Damped:** $D^2 = 4JK$ — pointer reaches the true value in minimum time without oscillation. **Ideal condition.**
- **Overdamped:** $D^2 > 4JK$ — pointer reaches the value slowly without oscillation.

---

## 2.2 PMMC — Theory of Operation

### Construction (Bullet Logic)
- **Magnet System:** High-grade Alnico or modern rare-earth (Samarium-Cobalt, NdFeB) permanent magnet producing strong, uniform flux in the air gap.
- **Moving Coil:** A rectangular coil of fine insulated copper wire wound on a lightweight aluminum former.
- **Poles Pieces:** Soft iron pole pieces shaped to produce **radial uniform magnetic flux** in the air gap (so $B$ is always perpendicular to the coil sides regardless of position).
- **Control System:** Helical phosphor-bronze springs that provide controlling torque and act as current leads.
- **Pointer & Scale:** Attached to the spindle; scale is uniformly divided.
- **Damping:** **Eddy current damping** — induced in the aluminum former as it moves in the magnetic field.

### Working Principle — The Lorentz Force
When current $I$ flows through the coil, the coil sides (length $l$) experience a force $F = BIl$ in the magnetic field $B$. The two forces are equal, opposite, and perpendicular to the coil sides, creating a torque:

$$T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$$

Where:
- $N$ = Number of turns of the coil
- $B$ = Flux density in the air gap (Tesla)
- $I$ = Current through the coil (Amperes)
- $A$ = Effective area of the coil = $l \times d$ (m²)

At equilibrium: $T_d = T_c \implies N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A = K \cdot \theta$

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{NBA}{K} \cdot I}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $NBA/K$ is a constant for a given instrument, the **deflection is directly proportional to current**. This gives a **uniform scale** — a key advantage of PMMC.

### Advantages of PMMC
1. High accuracy and sensitivity.
2. Uniform scale (linear response).
3. Low power consumption.
4. Effective eddy current damping.

### Disadvantages of PMMC
1. **Works only on DC** — AC current would produce alternating torque and the pointer would not deflect.
2. More expensive than MI.
3. Can be damaged by overload (no robust iron parts to absorb the kick).

---

## 2.3 Moving Iron (MI) Instruments — Two Types

### Type 1: Attraction (or "Plunger") Type
**Construction:** A fixed coil carries the current to be measured. A soft iron plunger (or vane) is attached to the spindle and is *attracted* into the coil when current flows.

**Working:** The coil's current magnetizes the iron plunger. The induced (opposite) poles on the iron are attracted by the coil's field, producing an inward force and a deflecting torque:

$$T_d = K_1 \cdot I^2 - K_2 \cdot \theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The torque is proportional to **$I^2$**, making it suitable for both AC and DC. The **scale is non-uniform** (compressed at the low end, expanded at the high end) because of the $I^2$ relationship.

### Type 2: Repulsion (or "Disc") Type
**Construction:** Two soft iron vanes/plates are placed inside the coil. One is **fixed** to the coil, the other is **movable** (attached to the spindle).

**Working:** The coil's current magnetizes BOTH iron vanes with the **same polarity** (both North or both South). Like poles repel, and the movable vane is pushed away from the fixed vane, producing a deflection.

$$T_d = K \cdot I^2 \quad \text{(approximately, near equilibrium)}$$

**Repulsion-type MI is more common** because the repulsion force is stronger and more uniform, giving a longer, more usable scale than the attraction type.

### Advantages of MI
1. Works on both AC and DC.
2. Robust construction; can withstand overloads.
3. Cheaper than PMMC.
4. Simple in construction.

### Disadvantages of MI
1. Non-uniform scale.
2. Hysteresis errors in iron (especially on DC).
3. Affected by stray magnetic fields (less of an issue with repulsion type).
4. Higher power consumption than PMMC.

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is essential for solving **range extension numerical problems** in KTU exams. Memorize these!

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Deflecting Torque (PMMC) | $T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$ | Lorentz force torque |
| 2 | Deflection (PMMC) | $\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K} \cdot I$ | Linear relationship |
| 3 | Deflecting Torque (MI) | $T_d \propto I^2$ | Both types |
| 4 | Shunt Resistance | $R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}$ | $m = I / I_m$ |
| 5 | Multiplier Resistance | $R_s = R_m \cdot (m - 1)$ | $m = V / V_m$ |
| 6 | Multiplication Factor (Current) | $m = \dfrac{I}{I_m} = 1 + \dfrac{R_m}{R_{sh}}$ | Ammeter |
| 7 | Multiplication Factor (Voltage) | $m = \dfrac{V}{V_m} = 1 + \dfrac{R_s}{R_m}$ | Voltmeter |
| 8 | Meter Current | $I_m = I - I_{sh}$ | KCL at junction |
| 9 | Meter Voltage | $V_m = V - I_m R_s$ | KVL around loop |
| 10 | Effective Resistance (Ammeter) | $R_{eff} = \dfrac{R_m \cdot R_{sh}}{R_m + R_{sh}}$ | Parallel combination |
| 11 | Total Resistance (Voltmeter) | $R_T = R_m + R_s$ | Series combination |
| 12 | Air Friction Damping Torque | $T_d = c \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Viscous drag |
| 13 | Eddy Current Damping Torque | $T_d \propto i_e^2 \propto \left(\dfrac{d\theta}{dt}\right)^2$ | Lenz's law |

**Symbols Used:**
- $I$ = Total current to be measured (A)
- $I_m$ or $I_{fsd}$ = Full-scale current of basic meter (A)
- $V$ = Total voltage to be measured (V)
- $V_m$ = Full-scale voltage of basic meter (V) = $I_m \cdot R_m$
- $R_m$ = Internal resistance of basic meter (Ω)
- $R_{sh}$ = Shunt resistance (Ω)
- $R_s$ = Series multiplier resistance (Ω)
- $m$ = Multiplying power / multiplication factor (dimensionless)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Instrument Type | Primary Industrial Use |
|----------------|------------------------|
| PMMC Ammeter | DC current measurement in **battery banks, solar PV systems, electroplating, traction systems** |
| PMMC Voltmeter | DC voltage measurement in **DC motors, generators, control panels** |
| MI Ammeter/Voltmeter | AC measurement in **power distribution panels, switchboards, industrial AC drives** |
| Shunt | Battery monitoring, welding current measurement, electric vehicle BMS |
| Multiplier | High-voltage DC transmission monitoring, cathode-ray tube circuits |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Shunt Resistance for Ammeter Range Extension

### Given
- A basic PMMC meter with internal resistance $R_m$ and full-scale current $I_m$
- We want to measure a total current $I$ (where $I \gg I_m$)

### Circuit Analysis
The shunt $R_{sh}$ is connected in **parallel** with the meter. Total current $I$ splits at the junction:
- $I_m$ flows through the meter (coil)
- $I_{sh}$ flows through the shunt

By **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):**
$$I = I_m + I_{sh}$$

By **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):** The voltage across the meter equals the voltage across the shunt (parallel connection):
$$V_m = V_{sh}$$
$$I_m \cdot R_m = I_{sh} \cdot R_{sh}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** From the KCL equation:
$$I_{sh} = I - I_m$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the voltage equation:
$$I_m \cdot R_m = (I - I_m) \cdot R_{sh}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $R_{sh}$:
$$R_{sh} = \frac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I - I_m}$$

**Step 4:** Define the **multiplying power** $m$ as the ratio of the total current to the meter current:
$$m = \frac{I}{I_m} \implies I = m \cdot I_m$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $I = m \cdot I_m$ into the $R_{sh}$ expression:

$$R_{sh} = \frac{I_m \cdot R_m}{m \cdot I_m - I_m} = \frac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I_m(m - 1)}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $I_m$:

$$\boxed{R_{sh} = \frac{R_m}{m - 1}}$$

### Alternative Form (Useful for Multi-Range Meters)
We can also write:
$$R_{sh} = \frac{R_m}{m - 1} = \frac{R_m}{\frac{I}{I_m} - 1} = \frac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I - I_m}$$

### Ayrton (Universal) Shunt for Multi-Range Ammeters
For a multi-range ammeter, instead of separate shunts (which interact), we use a **series combination of resistors** where the connection point is switched. This avoids the "switch contact resistance" error.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Multiplier Resistance for Voltmeter Range Extension

### Given
- A basic PMMC meter with internal resistance $R_m$ and full-scale current $I_m$
- We want to measure a total voltage $V$ (where $V \gg V_m = I_m R_m$)

### Circuit Analysis
The multiplier $R_s$ is connected in **series** with the meter. The same current $I_m$ flows through both (series connection).

By **KVL** around the loop:
$$V = V_m + V_{R_s}$$
$$V = I_m R_m + I_m R_s$$
$$V = I_m (R_m + R_s)$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The meter current $I_m$ at full-scale deflection:
$$I_m = \frac{V}{R_m + R_s}$$

**Step 2:** Define the multiplying power $m$ for voltmeter as:
$$m = \frac{V}{V_m} = \frac{V}{I_m R_m} \implies V = m \cdot I_m \cdot R_m$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $V = m \cdot I_m \cdot R_m$ into the KVL equation:
$$m \cdot I_m \cdot R_m = I_m (R_m + R_s)$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $I_m$ and solve:
$$m \cdot R_m = R_m + R_s$$

$$\boxed{R_s = R_m (m - 1)}$$

---

## 3.3 SOLVED NUMERICAL PROBLEMS (KTU Exam Pattern)

### Problem 1: Basic Shunt Calculation [7 Marks Type]
**Question:** A PMMC ammeter has a coil resistance of $100 \, \Omega$ and a full-scale deflection current of $50 \, \mu A$. Calculate the value of shunt resistance required to extend the range to $0–5 \, A$. Also calculate the multiplying power.

**Given Data:**
- $R_m = 100 \, \Omega$
- $I_m = 50 \, \mu A = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, A$
- $I = 5 \, A$

**Step 1: Multiplying power**
$$m = \frac{I}{I_m} = \frac{5}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 100{,}000 = 10^5$$

**Step 2: Shunt resistance**
$$R_{sh} = \frac{R_m}{m - 1} = \frac{100}{100{,}000 - 1} = \frac{100}{99{,}999}$$

$$R_{sh} \approx 1.00001 \times 10^{-3} \, \Omega \approx 1 \, m\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The shunt resistance is *extremely* small (about 1 milliohm) because it must carry almost all of the 5 A current. This is why shunts are made of thick, low-resistance materials like **manganin** (an alloy with near-zero temperature coefficient) and are often mounted externally for high-current measurements.

---

### Problem 2: Basic Multiplier Calculation [7 Marks Type]
**Question:** A basic PMMC movement with internal resistance $50 \, \Omega$ and full-scale current $1 \, mA$ is to be used as a voltmeter with range $0–10 \, V$. Find the required multiplier resistance.

**Given Data:**
- $R_m = 50 \, \Omega$
- $I_m = 1 \, mA = 10^{-3} \, A$
- $V = 10 \, V$

**Step 1: Full-scale voltage of the basic meter**
$$V_m = I_m \cdot R_m = 10^{-3} \times 50 = 0.05 \, V = 50 \, mV$$

**Step 2: Multiplying power**
$$m = \frac{V}{V_m} = \frac{10}{0.05} = 200$$

**Step 3: Multiplier resistance**
$$R_s = R_m (m - 1) = 50 \times (200 - 1) = 50 \times 199 = 9950 \, \Omega$$

$$R_s = 9.95 \, k\Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The total voltmeter resistance is $R_T = R_m + R_s = 50 + 9950 = 10{,}000 \, \Omega = 10 \, k\Omega$, which gives a sensitivity of $R_T / V = 10{,}000 / 10 = 1000 \, \Omega/V$. This is the **sensitivity rating** of the voltmeter.

---

### Problem 3: Multi-Range Ammeter (Ayrton Shunt) [Challenge]
**Question:** Design a multi-range ammeter with ranges $1 \, A$, $5 \, A$, and $10 \, A$ using a basic movement of $R_m = 1000 \, \Omega$ and $I_m = 100 \, \mu A$ (using Ayrton shunt with three resistors in series).

**Step 1: For the 1 A range**
$$m_1 = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 10{,}000$$
$$R_{sh1} = \frac{1000}{10{,}000 - 1} \approx 0.10001 \, \Omega$$
The total shunt resistance $R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 0.10001 \, \Omega$.

**Step 2: For the 5 A range**
$$m_2 = \frac{5}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 50{,}000$$
The effective shunt is $R_2 + R_3 = \dfrac{1000}{49{,}999} \approx 0.020 \, \Omega$
So $R_1 = 0.10001 - 0.020 = 0.08001 \, \Omega$.

**Step 3: For the 10 A range**
$$m_3 = \frac{10}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 100{,}000$$
The effective shunt is $R_3 = \dfrac{1000}{99{,}999} \approx 0.010 \, \Omega$
So $R_2 = 0.020 - 0.010 = 0.010 \, \Omega$.

> [!NOTE]
> This problem illustrates the **Ayrton shunt** design where the switch moves a tap point through a chain of resistors, ensuring only one shunt is in use at a time and avoiding contact resistance errors.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 3: Effective Resistance of an Extended Ammeter

After connecting a shunt, the equivalent resistance seen by the circuit is:

$$R_{eff} = \frac{R_m \cdot R_{sh}}{R_m + R_{sh}}$$

Since $R_{sh} \ll R_m$ (shunt is always much smaller than the meter), we get:

$$R_{eff} \approx R_{sh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This confirms the engineering requirement: an ideal ammeter should have **near-zero resistance** to avoid disturbing the circuit under test.

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 4: Sensitivity of a Voltmeter

**Voltmeter sensitivity** is defined as the total resistance per volt of full-scale range:
$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{R_T}{V} = \frac{R_m + R_s}{V} = \frac{1}{I_m} \, \Omega/V$$

For example, a $100 \, \mu A$ movement gives a sensitivity of $10{,}000 \, \Omega/V$. This is a standard industry rating.

A voltmeter with higher sensitivity draws less current from the circuit, giving more accurate readings — this is why **$20{,}000 \, \Omega/V$** is considered high quality.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a PMMC Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1[PMMC INSTRUMENT ARCHITECTURE]
        A[Permanent Magnet Alnico or Rare Earth] --> B[Soft Iron Pole Pieces Radial Field]
        B --> C[Uniform Magnetic Flux Density B in Air Gap]
        D[PMMC Moving Coil N turns on Aluminum Former] --> E[Current I from External Circuit]
        E --> F[Lorentz Force F equals BIl on Coil Sides]
        F --> G[Deflecting Torque Td equals NBA times I]
        H[Control Springs Phosphor Bronze] --> I[Controlling Torque Tc equals K theta]
        G --> J[Spindle and Pointer]
        I --> J
        K[Aluminum Former] --> L[Eddy Currents Induced]
        L --> M[Damping Torque Opposes Motion]
        M --> J
        J --> N[Equilibrium Point Deflection theta]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above flowchart shows how the **three torques (deflecting, controlling, damping)** interact at the spindle, eventually bringing the pointer to a stable deflected position $\theta$.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture of a Moving Iron (Repulsion Type) Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P2[MOVING IRON REPULSION TYPE]
        A[External AC or DC Current] --> B[Stationary Coil]
        B --> C[Magnetic Field Produced Inside Coil]
        C --> D[Fixed Iron Vane Magnetized Pole N]
        C --> E[Movable Iron Vane Magnetized Pole N]
        D --> F[Repulsion Force Between Like Poles]
        E --> F
        F --> G[Deflecting Torque Td proportional to I squared]
        G --> H[Spindle and Pointer]
        I[Gravity Control or Spring] --> J[Controlling Torque Tc]
        J --> H
        K[Air Friction Damper Vane in Chamber] --> H
        H --> L[Non Uniform Scale Deflection]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Circuit Schematic — Ammeter with Shunt (Range Extension)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CIRCUIT[Ammeter Circuit with Shunt]
        SRC[(Source with Current I)] --> BUS1{Junction Node}
        BUS1 --> M[Basic PMMC Meter Rm and Im]
        BUS1 --> SH[Shunt Resistance Rsh]
        M --> BUS2{Junction Node}
        SH --> BUS2
        BUS2 --> LOAD[(Load)]
        LOAD --> SRC
    end
    M -.->|"Im through meter"| NOTE1["Im is small fraction of I"]
    SH -.->|"Ish equals I minus Im"| NOTE2["Ish is large fraction bypasses meter"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the schematic above, the meter and shunt are in **parallel**. The total current $I$ from the source splits at the first junction: a tiny portion $I_m$ goes through the meter, and the bulk $I_{sh} = I - I_m$ flows through the low-resistance shunt.

---

## 4.4 Circuit Schematic — Voltmeter with Multiplier (Range Extension)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VCIR[Voltmeter Circuit with Multiplier]
        VSRC[(Source with Voltage V)] --> POS[Positive Terminal]
        POS --> MS[Multiplier Resistance Rs]
        MS --> MM[Basic PMMC Meter Rm and Im]
        MM --> NEG[Negative Terminal]
        NEG --> VSRC
    end
    MM -.->|"Im through meter equals V divided by Rm plus Rs"| NOTEV["Same current flows in series"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike the ammeter, here the meter and multiplier are in **series**. The same current $I_m$ flows through both, and the multiplier drops the excess voltage $V - V_m$ so that the meter only sees its rated full-scale voltage.

---

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram — Damping Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DAMP[THREE DAMPING METHODS]
        D1[Air Friction Damping] --> D1A[Light piston or vane]
        D1A --> D1B[Air resistance in chamber]
        D1B --> D1C[Damping torque proportional to angular velocity]
        D2[Fluid Friction Damping] --> D2A[Disc in viscous oil]
        D2A --> D2B[Viscosity drag]
        D2B --> D2C[Damping torque proportional to angular velocity]
        D3[Eddy Current Damping] --> D3A[Conducting former in magnetic field]
        D3A --> D3B[Induced eddy currents Lenz law]
        D3B --> D3C[Damping torque proportional to square of velocity]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define damping and briefly explain the three main types of damping used in electrical measuring instruments.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Damping** is the process of bringing the moving system of a measuring instrument to rest at its equilibrium position quickly and smoothly after a deflection.

The three main types are:
1. **Air Friction Damping:** A light piston/vane moves in an air chamber; air resistance provides damping torque proportional to angular velocity. Used in electrostatic voltmeters and instruments working in DC magnetic fields.
2. **Fluid Friction Damping:** A disc moves in a viscous oil (e.g., silicone oil). Provides stronger damping than air; used in recording instruments.
3. **Eddy Current Damping:** A conducting former moves in a magnetic field. Eddy currents are induced and, by Lenz's law, oppose the motion. Most efficient; used in PMMC instruments. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the three types)*

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between PMMC and Moving Iron instruments (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | PMMC | Moving Iron |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| Operating principle | Current in coil in permanent magnet field | Magnetization of soft iron by current in stationary coil |
| Quantity measured | DC only | Both AC and DC |
| Scale type | Uniform (linear) | Non-uniform (square law) |
| Damping | Eddy current (in aluminum former) | Air friction (in chamber) |
| Accuracy | High (0.1% to 2%) | Moderate (2% to 5%) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

*(3 marks — 1 mark per valid comparison point)*

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

---

### CHOICE QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q A (a): [7 Marks] With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a PMMC instrument. Derive the equation for the deflecting torque.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The PMMC instrument consists of:
- **Magnet:** A permanent magnet made of Alnico or rare-earth material provides a strong, uniform magnetic field in the air gap.
- **Pole pieces:** Laminated soft-iron pole pieces shaped to produce a **radial** magnetic field in the air gap, so that the flux lines are always perpendicular to the coil sides.
- **Moving coil:** A rectangular coil of fine insulated copper wire wound on a lightweight aluminum former. The aluminum former also provides eddy current damping.
- **Control system:** Two helical phosphor-bronze hairsprings provide the controlling torque and also serve as current leads.
- **Pointer and scale:** A lightweight pointer attached to the spindle moves over a uniformly graduated scale.
- **Damping:** Eddy current damping is achieved automatically by the aluminum former moving in the magnetic field.

**Working (2 marks):**
When the current $I$ to be measured flows through the moving coil, the coil sides (length $l$) experience forces $F = BIl$ in opposite directions. These forces produce a deflecting torque on the spindle. The pointer moves until the controlling torque from the springs balances the deflecting torque.

**Derivation of Deflecting Torque (2 marks):**
For a coil with $N$ turns, area $A$, in a magnetic field of density $B$, carrying current $I$:
$$T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$$
At equilibrium: $T_d = T_c \implies N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A = K \cdot \theta$
$$\theta = \frac{NBA}{K} \cdot I$$
This shows the deflection is linearly proportional to current, giving a uniform scale.

---

**Q A (b): [7 Marks] A PMMC ammeter has a coil resistance of $200 \, \Omega$ and requires $5 \, mA$ for full-scale deflection. Calculate: (i) the shunt resistance required to extend its range to $0–10 \, A$, and (ii) the value of the multiplier resistance needed to convert the same movement into a voltmeter of range $0–50 \, V$.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $R_m = 200 \, \Omega$
- $I_m = 5 \, mA = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, A$
- For ammeter: $I = 10 \, A$
- For voltmeter: $V = 50 \, V$

**Part (i) — Shunt Resistance (4 marks):**

Multiplying power for ammeter:
$$m = \frac{I}{I_m} = \frac{10}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2000$$

Shunt resistance:
$$R_{sh} = \frac{R_m}{m - 1} = \frac{200}{2000 - 1} = \frac{200}{1999} \approx 0.10005 \, \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{sh} \approx 0.1 \, \Omega}$$

**Part (ii) — Multiplier Resistance (3 marks):**

Full-scale voltage of the basic movement:
$$V_m = I_m \cdot R_m = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 = 1 \, V$$

Multiplying power for voltmeter:
$$m = \frac{V}{V_m} = \frac{50}{1} = 50$$

Multiplier resistance:
$$R_s = R_m (m - 1) = 200 \times (50 - 1) = 200 \times 49 = 9800 \, \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_s = 9.8 \, k\Omega}$$

---

### CHOICE QUESTION B: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q B (a): [7 Marks] Explain the construction and working of a repulsion-type moving iron instrument. State its advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A repulsion-type moving iron instrument consists of:
- **Stationary coil:** A hollow cylindrical coil carrying the current to be measured. The coil is wound with insulated copper wire on a non-magnetic former.
- **Two iron vanes inside the coil:**
  - A **fixed vane** attached rigidly to the coil's inner wall.
  - A **movable vane** attached to the spindle, free to rotate.
- **Control system:** A spring (or gravity control) provides the controlling torque.
- **Pointer and scale:** A pointer attached to the spindle moves over a non-uniform scale.
- **Damping:** Air friction damping is used (a vane in a chamber), since eddy current damping would interfere with the magnetic field.

**Working (2 marks):**
When current $I$ flows through the stationary coil, it produces a magnetic field. This field magnetizes BOTH iron vanes in the **same direction** (both North or both South at their facing ends). Like poles repel each other, and the movable vane is pushed away from the fixed vane, rotating the spindle and moving the pointer. The deflection is proportional to $I^2$, making the instrument suitable for both AC and DC.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Works on both AC and DC.
- Robust construction; tolerates overloads.
- Cheaper than PMMC.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Non-uniform scale (compressed at low end).
- Hysteresis errors when used on DC.
- Higher power consumption than PMMC.

---

**Q B (b): [7 Marks] A basic D'Arsonval movement has a resistance of $100 \, \Omega$ and a full-scale current of $1 \, mA$. It is to be converted into a multi-range ammeter with ranges $0–100 \, mA$, $0–1 \, A$, and $0–10 \, A$ using individual shunts. Calculate the shunt resistance for each range.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $R_m = 100 \, \Omega$
- $I_m = 1 \, mA = 10^{-3} \, A$

**General formula:** $R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}$, where $m = \dfrac{I}{I_m}$.

**Range 1: $I = 100 \, mA = 0.1 \, A$** (2 marks)
$$m_1 = \frac{0.1}{10^{-3}} = 100$$
$$R_{sh1} = \frac{100}{100 - 1} = \frac{100}{99} \approx 1.0101 \, \Omega$$

**Range 2: $I = 1 \, A$** (2 marks)
$$m_2 = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000$$
$$R_{sh2} = \frac{100}{1000 - 1} = \frac{100}{999} \approx 0.1001 \, \Omega$$

**Range 3: $I = 10 \, A$** (2 marks)
$$m_3 = \frac{10}{10^{-3}} = 10{,}000$$
$$R_{sh3} = \frac{100}{10{,}000 - 1} = \frac{100}{9999} \approx 0.0100 \, \Omega$$

**Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Range | Multiplying Power $m$ | Shunt Resistance $R_{sh}$ |
|-------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| $100 \, mA$ | 100 | $1.0101 \, \Omega$ |
| $1 \, A$ | 1000 | $0.1001 \, \Omega$ |
| $10 \, A$ | 10,000 | $0.0100 \, \Omega$ |

---

## 5.4 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting to convert $mA$ to $A$ or $k\Omega$ to $\Omega$. Always work in base SI units (Amperes, Volts, Ohms).
> 2. **Sign of $m-1$:** Students often write $\frac{R_m}{m+1}$ instead of $\frac{R_m}{m-1}$. Memorize: it is **$m-1$**, because the meter current $I_m$ is *subtracted* from the total $I$ in the shunt ($I_{sh} = I - I_m$).
> 3. **Confusing $V_m$ and $I_m$:** For a voltmeter, $V_m = I_m \cdot R_m$ — students often use $V_m = R_m$ by mistake.
> 4. **Spring constant missing:** When asked to derive the deflection equation, many students stop at $T_d = NBAI$ and forget the equilibrium condition $T_d = T_c = K\theta$ to get the final form.
> 5. **Choosing the wrong instrument type for the question:** A question asking for AC measurement expects **Moving Iron** (PMMC doesn't work on AC), and vice versa.
> 6. **Not stating the type of damping explicitly:** If a question asks for advantages/disadvantages, always name the damping method and the reason (e.g., "Eddy current damping is used because the aluminum former moves in a strong magnetic field").

---

## 5.5 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Damping** = dissipation of kinetic energy of the moving system. Three types: **Air Friction**, **Fluid Friction**, **Eddy Current**.
- **Eddy current damping** is the most efficient; uses Lenz's law; requires a magnetic field.
- **PMMC** = **P**ermanent **M**agnet **M**oving **C**oil = D'Arsonval movement. DC only. Linear scale. $T_d = NBAI$.
- **Moving Iron** = works on both AC and DC. Two types: **Attraction** (plunger) and **Repulsion** (disc). $T_d \propto I^2$. Non-uniform scale.
- **Shunt** for ammeter = **parallel** resistor, low value, carries most of the current.
- **Multiplier** for voltmeter = **series** resistor, high value, drops the excess voltage.
- **Key formula for shunt:** $R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}$, with $m = \dfrac{I}{I_m}$.
- **Key formula for multiplier:** $R_s = R_m(m - 1)$, with $m = \dfrac{V}{V_m}$.
- **Ayrton shunt** is used for multi-range ammeters to avoid switch contact resistance errors.
- **Voltmeter sensitivity** = $\dfrac{1}{I_m}$ in $\Omega/V$. A $50 \, \mu A$ meter = $20{,}000 \, \Omega/V$ (high quality).
- **Material for shunts:** **Manganin** (low temperature coefficient of resistance).
- **Material for multipliers:** **Constantan** or **Manganin** (high resistivity, stable).
- **Ideal ammeter** resistance = **0**; **Ideal voltmeter** resistance = **infinity**.
- The three torques: **Deflecting ($T_d$)**, **Controlling ($T_c$)**, **Damping ($T_{damp}$)**. At equilibrium, $T_d = T_c$; damping torque = 0 at rest.
- **Equation of motion:** $J\theta'' + D\theta' + K\theta = T_d$. For critical damping: $D^2 = 4JK$.
- **PMMC uses Eddy current damping**; **MI uses Air friction damping** (because eddy currents would distort the weak working field).
- **Always state units** in final answers for KTU exams: $R_{sh}$ in $\Omega$, $R_s$ in $\Omega$ or $k\Omega$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Measurement - Flux Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT411 - ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENT AND MEASURING INSTRUMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Measurement - Flux Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Flux Meter** is a precision-grade permanent magnet moving-coil (PMMC) type integrating instrument used to measure the **total change in magnetic flux linkage (in Weber-turns)** of a search coil. It is the standard laboratory instrument for direct measurement of magnetic flux, flux density, and the B-H characteristics of ferromagnetic specimens.

The classical and most widely accepted form is the **Grassot Flux Meter**, which uses a moving coil suspended in the field of a **permanent magnet** with extremely small controlling torque (achieved by eliminating control springs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> A flux meter is essentially a permanent-magnet moving-coil instrument in which the controlling torque is very small and the instrument is provided with a suitable coil of known area-turns. The deflection of the pointer is proportional to the change in flux linkages of the search coil.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain-English Analogy — "The Memory Bucket":**

Imagine you are standing at the mouth of a river holding an empty bucket. The river carries water (magnetic flux) for a short duration and then stops. Now imagine a *magical* bucket that, after the river stops, *remembers* exactly how much water passed by — its level stays at that final value until you manually empty it.

A **flux meter** behaves exactly like this magical bucket:
- The **river** = changing magnetic flux linking a search coil
- The **water** = the induced electrical impulse (`e = -N dΦ/dt`)
- The **magical bucket** = the moving coil of the PMMC meter with almost zero restoring force
- The **pointer position** = a permanent "memory" of the total flux change

Because the controlling springs are removed (or made extremely weak), the pointer **does not snap back to zero** — instead it stays where the flux change left it, like the water level in the magical bucket. This is why it is called an **integrating** instrument.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flux change | ΔΦ | **Weber (Wb)** | Primary measured quantity |
| Flux linkage change | NΔΦ | **Weber-turns (Wb·turns)** | Output reading basis |
| Coil resistance | R | **few ohms (≈ 20–100 Ω)** | Critical to integrator behaviour |
| Deflection | θ | **radians** (or scale divisions) | Stays at final value |
| Spring constant | K | **≈ 0** (negligible) | Distinguishes flux meter from galvanometer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Point to Remember:** A flux meter is essentially a **galvanometer with zero controlling torque** — this single phrase, if written in your exam, is worth high credit because it is the conceptual foundation of the entire instrument.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation rests on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** combined with the **PMMC torque equation**. The logic unfolds as follows:

1. **Search coil (of N turns and area A) is placed in the unknown magnetic field.**
2. The flux through this coil changes from Φ₁ to Φ₂ over a short interval — either by withdrawing the coil from the field, by reversing it, or by switching the magnetising current ON/OFF.
3. The induced emf in the search coil is, by Faraday's law:
   `e = -N (dΦ/dt)`
4. This emf drives a transient current through the search coil and the flux-meter moving coil, which are connected in series (total circuit resistance = **R**):
   `i = e / R = -(N/R)(dΦ/dt)`
5. The PMMC coil experiences a deflecting torque proportional to current:
   `Td = G · i = -(G N / R)(dΦ/dt)` where **G** is the galvanometer constant.
6. Because the controlling torque is essentially zero (no spring), the coil accelerates under this impulse and integrates it.
7. Integrating the equation of motion over the time interval of flux change gives a **deflection θ proportional to the total change in flux linkage**:
   `θ ∝ N · ΔΦ`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The beauty of this instrument is that the deflection is **independent of the time taken** to change the flux. Whether the flux changes in 0.1 s or 10 s, the pointer moves by the same amount. This is a direct consequence of integration.

## 2.2 The Three "Why" Layers

- **Why no springs?** — Springs would produce a restoring torque proportional to angle. If present, the deflection would depend on instantaneous current, not the integral of current. Removing springs makes the meter *integrate* by default.
- **Why extremely low friction?** — Any friction (mechanical or air damping) drains the impulse and produces errors. Hence the moving system is suspended on jewelled bearings in low-friction geometry.
- **Why connect a known search coil in series?** — The number of turns N is fixed and known, so the deflection scale can be directly calibrated in **Weber-turns** or **Webers** (if N is incorporated in the scale factor).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `e = -N · dΦ/dt` | Induced emf in search coil (Faraday's Law) | V |
| 2 | `i = e / R` | Induced current (R = search-coil + flux-meter resistance) | A |
| 3 | `Td = G · i` | Deflecting torque on PMMC coil (G = galvanometer constant) | N·m |
| 4 | `Tc = K · θ ≈ 0` | Controlling torque (K ≈ 0 for flux meter) | N·m |
| 5 | `θ = (N · ΔΦ) / (R · Kf)` | Final steady deflection of flux meter | rad |
| 6 | `ΔΦ = (Kf · R · θ) / N` | Change in flux (used for calibration) | Wb |
| 7 | `B = ΔΦ / A` | Flux density from search coil area A | T (Tesla) |
| 8 | `Kf = G / (K + D₀)` | Flux-meter constant (D₀ = friction damping) | — |

> **CRITICAL EXAM RULE:** In the table above, the notation `Kf · R · θ` and `Kf / (K + D₀)` are written with the LaTeX vertical bar **only when displayed as a math expression**, never as a raw `|` character — this preserves markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Testing of permanent magnets** — residual flux and B-H loop characterisation.
- **Calibration of electromagnets** used in MRI machines, particle accelerators, and DC machines.
- **Quality control of transformer core material** — sheet-steel B-H curve tracing.
- **Ballistic comparison standard** — historically used to calibrate ballistic galvanometers.
- **Modern replacement:** Electronic flux meters (op-amp based integrators) have largely replaced Grassot meters, but the principle taught is identical and frequently appears in KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deflection is Proportional to Change in Flux Linkage

We will now derive the **governing deflection equation** of a Grassot flux meter from first principles. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

### Step 1: Faraday's Law for the Search Coil

Consider a search coil of N turns placed in a changing magnetic field of flux Φ.

$$e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

The negative sign indicates Lenz's law (opposition to the cause). The magnitude is what produces the current.

### Step 2: Instantaneous Current in the Circuit

The search coil and the moving coil of the flux meter are connected in series, forming a closed loop of total ohmic resistance **R** (neglecting the small inductance of the moving coil since the change is slow).

$$i = \frac{e}{R} = -\frac{N}{R} \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Torque Equation of the PMMC Moving Coil

A PMMC coil of area a_c carrying current i in a uniform radial field B_c experiences a deflecting torque.

$$T_d = B_c \cdot a_c \cdot N_c \cdot i = G \cdot i$$

where **G = B_c · a_c · N_c** is the *galvanometer constant* of the flux-meter movement. Substituting current:

$$T_d = -\frac{G \cdot N}{R} \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

### Step 4: Equation of Motion of the Moving System

Applying Newton's second law for rotation (J = moment of inertia of moving coil):

$$J \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = T_d - T_c - T_f$$

Since the controlling torque is **T_c = Kθ ≈ 0** (no springs) and friction damping is negligible:

$$J \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -\frac{G \cdot N}{R} \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

### Step 5: Integrate Once with Respect to Time

Integrating both sides from t = 0 to t = t₁ (the duration over which flux changes):

$$J \frac{d\theta}{dt} \Big|_{0}^{t_1} = -\frac{G \cdot N}{R} \int_{0}^{t_1} \frac{d\Phi}{dt} dt$$

$$J \left[ \frac{d\theta}{dt}\bigg|_{t_1} - \frac{d\theta}{dt}\bigg|_{0} \right] = -\frac{G \cdot N}{R} [\Phi_2 - \Phi_1]$$

### Step 6: Boundary Condition — Final Velocity is Zero

After the flux change is complete (current = 0), there is no torque, so the moving system comes to rest due to the small friction damping D₀. Thus:

$$\left. \frac{d\theta}{dt} \right|_{t_1} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left. \frac{d\theta}{dt} \right|_{0} = 0$$

This gives us:

$$0 = -\frac{G \cdot N}{R} (\Phi_2 - \Phi_1)$$

This appears to give 0 = 0, which is not useful. The resolution is that the moving system was in fact **not at rest at t = 0** — it was at a steady previous deflection θ₁. The proper form comes from integrating the *deflecting impulse* against the *damping impulse*:

$$D_0 \frac{d\theta}{dt} + K \theta = -\frac{G \cdot N}{R} \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

With K ≈ 0 and integrating over the short change interval:

$$D_0 \int_{0}^{t_1} \frac{d\theta}{dt} dt = -\frac{G \cdot N}{R} \int_{\Phi_1}^{\Phi_2} d\Phi$$

$$D_0 (\theta_2 - \theta_1) = -\frac{G \cdot N}{R} (\Phi_2 - \Phi_1)$$

### Step 7: Final Governing Equation

Defining the flux-meter constant **K_f = G / D_0** and taking magnitudes:

$$\boxed{\;\theta_2 - \theta_1 = \frac{N \cdot \Delta\Phi}{R} \cdot K_f\;}$$

Or, expressed as the change in flux:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta\Phi = \frac{R}{K_f \cdot N} (\theta_2 - \theta_1)\;}$$

> **Interpretation:** The **deflection is directly proportional to the change in flux linkage N·ΔΦ**, and is *independent of the rate* at which the flux changes. This is the celebrated property of the flux meter.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expression for Flux Density B

If the search coil has a known cross-sectional area **A**, then the flux density is simply:

$$B = \frac{\Delta\Phi}{A} = \frac{R \cdot (\theta_2 - \theta_1)}{K_f \cdot N \cdot A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the form examiners expect when the problem statement says: *"A search coil of 50 turns and area 2 cm² is connected to a flux meter. The flux meter reads 100 scale divisions. The flux-meter constant is 10⁻⁴ Wb/div and the total resistance is 80 Ω. Find the flux density."*

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Given:** N = 50 turns, A = 2 cm² = 2 × 10⁻⁴ m², R = 80 Ω, K_f = 10⁻⁴ Wb/div, θ = 100 divisions, ΔΦ corresponds to removing the coil from field B to zero.

**Step 1 — Compute flux change:**

$$\Delta\Phi = \frac{R \cdot \theta}{K_f \cdot N} = \frac{80 \times 100}{10^{-4} \times 50} = \frac{8000}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ Wb}$$

Wait — that scale is clearly wrong for a meter reading. The correct interpretation is that the flux-meter constant must be re-examined. Let us redo it correctly.

**Corrected step:** The flux-meter constant in SI units is normally given in **Wb/division × Ω** form. The proper engineering formula is:

$$\Delta\Phi = \frac{C \cdot \theta}{N}$$

where C is the **flux-meter constant in Wb-turns/division**. For our problem, C = 10⁻⁴ Wb·turns/div. Then:

$$\Delta\Phi = \frac{10^{-4} \times 100}{50} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb}$$

$$B = \frac{\Delta\Phi}{A} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.0 \text{ T}$$

> **Final Answer: B = 1.0 Tesla.** This is a typical laboratory value for a strong permanent magnet.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

Because the Grassot flux meter is a *physical instrument* with a moving coil, permanent magnet, and external search coil, a true 1:1 pictorial drawing is impractical in Mermaid. We therefore present a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that maps every signal, energy, and mechanical interaction — this is the KTU-accepted schematic for the topic.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Grassot Flux Meter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    magField[/"Unknown Magnetic Field B"/]
    searchCoil["Search Coil: N turns, Area A"]
    pmcMovement["PMMC Movement: Coil in Radial Field Bc"]
    permMagnet["Permanent Magnet Assembly"]
    jewelledBearing["Jewelled Bearing (Low Friction)"]
    pointerDial["Pointer and Calibrated Scale"]
    externalRheostat["External Series Resistor Rext"]
    controlSpring["No Control Spring K approx 0"]

    magField -- "Flux Phi links coil" --> searchCoil
    searchCoil -- "emf e = -N dPhi/dt" --> pmcMovement
    externalRheostat -- "limits circuit resistance R" --> pmcMovement
    permMagnet -- "provides steady field Bc" --> pmcMovement
    pmcMovement -- "torque Td = G i" --> jewelledBearing
    jewelledBearing -- "rotates freely" --> pointerDial
    pointerDial -- "deflection theta proportional to N Delta Phi" --> controlSpring
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Operating Sequence (Coil Withdrawal)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: Search coil inside field B"]
    fluxA["Flux Phi1 links N turns"]
    startA --> fluxA
    withdraw["Operator withdraws coil rapidly from field"]
    fluxA --> withdraw
    emfA["emf induced: e = -N dPhi/dt"]
    withdraw --> emfA
    curA["Current i flows in moving coil: i = e/R"]
    emfA --> curA
    torqueA["Deflecting torque Td = G i"]
    curA --> torqueA
    integA["Coil integrates impulse over time"]
    torqueA --> integA
    restA["Coil comes to rest at theta2"]
    integA --> restA
    fluxB["Phi2 = 0, Delta Phi = Phi1"]
    restA --> fluxB
    readA["Reading: theta2 - theta1 proportional to Delta Phi"]
    fluxB --> readA
    calcB["Compute B = Delta Phi / A"]
    readA --> calcB
    endA["End: B displayed in Tesla"]
    calcB --> endA
```

## 4.3 Construction Cross-Section Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    polePiece1["North Pole Piece"]
    polePiece2["South Pole Piece"]
    coreCyl["Soft Iron Core Cylinder"]
    movingCoil["Moving Coil Winding"]
    spindle["Spindle / Pointer Shaft"]
    jewel["Jewelled Pivot Bearing"]
    scalePlate["Calibrated Scale Plate"]
    searchCoilExt["External Search Coil (User Side)"]
    terminals["External Terminals T1 and T2"]
    damping["Air Damping Chamber (Small Vane)"]

    polePiece1 --- coreCyl
    coreCyl --- polePiece2
    movingCoil --- spindle
    spindle --- jewel
    jewel --- scalePlate
    movingCoil --- damping
    searchCoilExt --- terminals
    terminals --- movingCoil
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** If asked to draw the flux meter in your answer script, draw the PMMC core-and-coil system with the search coil externally connected through the two terminals, and explicitly **strike out** or label as *"omitted"* the control springs. This is the single highest-credit visual cue.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP, Set B]**
**State the principle of operation of a Grassot flux meter. Why is the controlling torque kept negligibly small?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Grassot flux meter operates on the principle of **electromagnetic induction combined with electromagnetic torque**. When the flux linking a search coil of N turns changes, an emf `e = -N dΦ/dt` is induced. This emf drives a current through the moving coil of the PMMC meter, producing a torque `Td = Gi`. The deflecting impulse is integrated by the low-friction moving system.

The controlling torque is kept **negligibly small (K ≈ 0)** by removing the control springs so that the deflection becomes proportional to the **integral of the induced current**, which in turn equals the **change in flux linkage N·ΔΦ**. This makes the deflection independent of the rate of change of flux. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of a flux meter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. The deflection is independent of the time taken to change the flux. (1 mark)
2. It can directly measure total flux linkage in Weber-turns. (1 mark)
3. It gives accurate results even for very rapid changes in flux. (½ mark)

**Disadvantages:**
1. The pointer does not return to zero automatically — it drifts and requires manual reset. (½ mark)
2. The instrument is highly sensitive to stray magnetic fields and external disturbances. (½ mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Grassot flux meter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the deflection of a flux meter in terms of the change in flux linkage. Show clearly why the deflection is independent of the time taken for the flux change. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Construction (3 marks):**
The Grassot flux meter is essentially a PMMC instrument with the following special features:
1. **No control springs** — the pointer is held in position purely by friction of the jewelled bearing. A small phosphor-bronze hair-spring is used *only* to lead current in and out.
2. **Permanent magnet and moving coil** — same as PMMC, providing a uniform radial field Bc.
3. **Low-friction suspension** — jewelled bearings minimise mechanical friction.
4. **External terminals** — provided to connect the search coil in series with the moving coil.
5. **Pointer and calibrated scale** — directly readable in Weber-turns (if N is fixed) or Weber (with the search-coil area-turns known).

**Working (4 marks):**
1. The search coil of N turns and area A is connected across the flux-meter terminals and placed in the unknown field B.
2. The flux through the search coil is changed either by withdrawing the coil out of the field or by reversing it.
3. The induced emf `e = -N dΦ/dt` drives current `i = e/R` through the moving coil of the flux meter.
4. The moving coil experiences a torque `Td = G·i` which, in the absence of any controlling spring, produces a deflection θ proportional to the integral of the current — i.e., proportional to N·ΔΦ.
5. The pointer remains at its final position, indicating the total change in flux linkage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> Students frequently write *"a flux meter works like a voltmeter"* or *"it is a moving-iron instrument."* This is **wrong** and costs 1–2 marks immediately. A flux meter is **a PMMC instrument with negligible controlling torque**. Use the phrase *"zero or near-zero restoring torque"* explicitly.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1 of these notes** (Steps 1 to 7). The student should reproduce:

- Faraday's law: `e = -N dΦ/dt` **(1 mark)**
- Induced current: `i = -(N/R)(dΦ/dt)` **(1 mark)**
- Deflecting torque: `Td = G·i` **(1 mark)**
- Equation of motion with K ≈ 0: `D₀ dθ/dt = -(G·N/R)(dΦ/dt)` **(1 mark)**
- Integration yields: `D₀(θ₂ - θ₁) = -(G·N/R)(Φ₂ - Φ₁)` **(1 mark)**
- Final form: `θ₂ - θ₁ = (G/D₀) · (N/R) · ΔΦ = K_f · (N·ΔΦ/R)` **(1 mark)**
- Reasoning for time-independence: The integration over time removes the `dt`, so the result depends only on the *total* flux change ΔΦ, not on the rate dΦ/dt. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how a flux meter is used to plot the B-H curve of a ferromagnetic specimen in a ring method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flux meter is connected to a search coil of 200 turns and area 5 cm². The flux-meter constant is 100 µWb-turns/division and the total circuit resistance is 50 Ω. The pointer deflects by 80 divisions when the search coil is removed from a magnetic field. Calculate: (i) the change in flux, (ii) the original flux density, and (iii) the galvanometer constant G if the damping constant D₀ = 5 × 10⁻⁶ N·m·s/rad. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Ring Method Setup (3 marks):**
1. A **ring-shaped (toroidal) specimen** of the ferromagnetic material is uniformly wound with a **magnetising winding** (primary) carrying current I.
2. A **search coil (secondary)** of N₂ turns is wound uniformly over the primary and connected to the flux meter.
3. The magnetising field H = (NI)/(2πr), where r is the mean radius of the ring.
4. The flux density B = Φ/A, where A is the cross-sectional area of the ring.

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Start with the specimen in a demagnetised state (H = 0, B = 0).
2. Increase I in small steps; at each step, the flux through the search coil changes, and the flux meter reading ΔΦ is recorded.
3. The corresponding B is computed: B = ΔΦ / A.
4. The (H, B) points are plotted to give the **initial magnetisation curve**.
5. Reverse I, reduce to zero, then increase in the opposite direction — this traces the **hysteresis loop** of the material.
6. The coercivity H_c, retentivity B_r, and area of the loop (hysteresis loss per cycle) can all be read from the plotted curve.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> Many students forget to mention that the **ring method avoids end-effects and demagnetising fields**, which is precisely why it is the laboratory-standard method. Including this sentence in your answer can earn you a full extra concept mark.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** N = 200 turns, A = 5 cm² = 5 × 10⁻⁴ m², C = 100 µWb-turns/div = 100 × 10⁻⁶ Wb·turns/div, R = 50 Ω, θ = 80 divisions, D₀ = 5 × 10⁻⁶ N·m·s/rad.

**(i) Change in flux (2 marks):**

$$\Delta\Phi = \frac{C \cdot \theta}{N} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 80}{200} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{200} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Wb}$$

**(ii) Original flux density (2 marks):**

$$B = \frac{\Delta\Phi}{A} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.08 \text{ T}$$

**(iii) Galvanometer constant G (3 marks):**

From the flux-meter governing equation:

$$\theta = \frac{G \cdot N \cdot \Delta\Phi}{R \cdot D_0}$$

$$G = \frac{\theta \cdot R \cdot D_0}{N \cdot \Delta\Phi} = \frac{80 \times 50 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 4 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$G = \frac{80 \times 250 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.5 \text{ N·m/A}$$

**Answers: (i) ΔΦ = 40 µWb, (ii) B = 0.08 T, (iii) G = 2.5 N·m/A**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss Alert:**
> - Students frequently forget to convert µWb to Wb and cm² to m² — always do unit conversions FIRST. (Costs ~1 mark per mistake)
> - In part (iii), do not confuse the **galvanometer constant G** with the **flux-meter constant K_f**. They are different (G has units N·m/A, K_f is dimensionless per division).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Flux meter = PMMC + zero controlling torque.** Memorise this single line.
- **Deflection equation:** `θ = (N · ΔΦ / R) · K_f` where `K_f = G / D₀`.
- **It is an integrating instrument** — measures total flux change, not rate of change.
- **Deflection is independent of the time** taken to change the flux — direct consequence of integration.
- **No control springs** are used; only a thin pigtail or hair-spring for current lead-in.
- **Calibration constant** must be rechecked before each measurement because of pointer drift.
- **Applications:** Permanent-magnet testing, B-H curve plotting (ring method), residual-flux measurement in DC machines and electromagnets.
- **Key search-coil formula:** `B = ΔΦ / A`; `H = NI / l` for the magnetising winding.
- **Avoid confusion with ballistic galvanometer** — a ballistic galvanometer has a *finite* but small K, while a flux meter has K ≈ 0.
- **Electronic flux meters** (op-amp Miller integrator) are the modern replacement and use the same governing equation.
- **Examiners love three things in this topic:** (1) the derivation, (2) the explanation of why K ≈ 0, and (3) a numerical on ΔΦ, B, or G. Prepare all three.
- **One-sentence board-exam answer for full marks in a 1-mark question:** *"A flux meter is a permanent-magnet moving-coil instrument with negligible controlling torque, whose deflection is proportional to the change in flux linkage of a connected search coil."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of BH Curve - Hysteresis Loop (Method of Reversal). Measurement of Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT411 - ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENT AND MEASURING INSTRUMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of BH Curve - Hysteresis Loop (Method of Reversal). Measurement of Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The B–H Curve and Magnetic Hysteresis

### 1.1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **B–H Curve** (or Magnetization Curve) of a ferromagnetic material is the graphical locus of the relationship between the magnetic flux density **B** (in Tesla, T) and the magnetizing field intensity **H** (in Ampere-turns per metre, A/m) inside the material. When a specimen of ferromagnetic material is subjected to a cyclic, uni-directional magnetizing force **H** that alternates between equal positive and negative values, the flux density **B** traces a closed loop known as the **Hysteresis Loop**.

The loop represents the **irreversible** and **non-linear** behaviour of magnetic domains inside the material, where the value of **B** at any instant depends not only on the instantaneous value of **H** but also on the magnetic history of the specimen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box — Key Terms**
> - **Retentivity (Br):** The residual flux density retained by the material when the magnetizing field is reduced to zero.
> - **Coercivity (Hc):** The reverse magnetizing field intensity required to reduce the residual flux density to zero.
> - **Saturation Flux Density (Bs):** The maximum flux density achieved when further increase in H produces negligible change in B.

### 1.1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Memory of a Sponge"

Imagine dipping a dry kitchen sponge (representing a magnetic domain) into water (**H** increasing) — it soaks up water (**B** rising). When you take the sponge out (**H = 0**), it is still wet — it *remembers* it was dipped. You have to squeeze it hard in the opposite direction (**reverse H** = coercivity Hc) to wring out the remaining water. The total path of the sponge getting soaked, retaining water, and being wrung dry forms a **closed hysteresis loop**. The sponge never returns to its original state instantly — its behaviour depends on its **history**.

Similarly, a ferromagnetic material "remembers" its magnetic past. The path of magnetization is not retraced on demagnetization; this lag is called **hysteresis** (Greek *hysteros* = "to lag behind").

### 1.1.3 Why the Hysteresis Loop Matters in Engineering

- **Transformer Core Design:** A narrow loop (low retentivity, low coercivity) like **CRGO steel** or **silicon steel** is chosen to minimize hysteresis loss, which is the wasted electrical energy converted into heat during every AC cycle.
- **Permanent Magnets:** A wide, "square" loop with high **Br** and **Hc** (e.g., **Alnico, NdFeB, SmCo**) is desired to make strong, permanent magnets.
- **Switching/Memory Devices:** The rectangular loop of materials like **ferrite cores** is exploited in magnetic memory and digital logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The *Method of Reversal* using a **Ballistic Galvanometer** is the classical experimental technique mandated in the syllabus for experimentally tracing the B–H curve. It is preferred over AC methods (like the **Cathode-Ray Oscilloscope** method) because it offers greater precision in absolute flux measurement.

---

## 1.2 Measurement of Resistance — Overview

### 1.2.1 Formal Definition

**Electrical resistance (R)** is the opposition offered by a component to the flow of direct current, given by **Ohm's Law** as **R = V / I** (in Ohms, Ω). The accurate measurement of resistance is a fundamental requirement in electrical engineering, as it determines conductor sizing, insulation integrity, contact quality, and earth-fault safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classification of Resistance (Mandatory for Exam):**
> 
> | Range (Ω) | Classification | Typical Examples | Preferred Methods |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | < **1 Ω** | **Low Resistance** | Shunts, ammeter coils, bus-bar joints, armature windings | Kelvin Double Bridge, Ammeter–Voltmeter (with proper lead correction), Potentiometer |
> | **1 Ω to 100 kΩ** | **Medium Resistance** | Field coils, carbon resistors, heater elements | Wheatstone Bridge, Ammeter–Voltmeter, Substitution Method, Carey-Foster Bridge |
> | > **100 kΩ** | **High Resistance** | Insulation of cables, capacitors, transformer oil, mica | Loss-of-Charge Method, Megger (Direct-Deflection), Galvanometer-Sensitive Method |
> | Insulators | **Insulation / Leakage Resistance** | Cable insulation, machine windings, switchgear | Megger, Loss-of-Charge |
> | Soil / Earth | **Earth Resistance** | Earth electrodes, lightning protection | Fall-of-Potential Method, Wenner 4-Probe |

### 1.2.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Water in a Pipe"

Think of electrical resistance like friction inside a water pipe. **Low resistance** is like a wide, smooth pipe (water flows easily). **Medium resistance** is a pipe with moderate friction. **High resistance** is like a narrow, blocked pipe (water trickles). Just as a plumber chooses different tools (pressure gauge, flow meter) depending on the pipe size, an electrical engineer must choose different bridge circuits and instruments depending on the magnitude of the resistance being measured — you cannot use a plumber's wrench for a tiny precision valve!

### 1.2.3 Why Measurement of Resistance is Critical in Engineering

- **Quality Control:** Verifying wire-wound resistor tolerances.
- **Insulation Testing:** Ensuring safety of electrical installations.
- **Earth Testing:** Verifying protective earthing for human safety.
- **Temperature Compensation:** Resistance thermometers (**RTDs**).
- **Battery / Cell Testing:** Internal resistance indicates health.
- **Contact Maintenance:** Identifying loose, corroded, or oxidized joints.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Method of Reversal — Concept and Theory

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

The **Method of Reversal** is a ballistic (impulse) method for experimentally determining the B–H curve. It exploits the fact that when the current through a **magnetizing coil** wound uniformly on a toroidal specimen is **reversed suddenly**, the corresponding change of flux through a closely-coupled **search coil** induces a brief pulse of charge in the search-coil circuit. This charge is measured by a **ballistic galvanometer**, whose maximum deflection is proportional to the total charge that flowed.

### 2.1.2 Logic Steps (Why and How)

1. **Toroidal Specimen Preparation:** A ring of the material under test is wound with two coils:
   - **Magnetizing (Primary) coil** with N₁ turns carrying current I.
   - **Search (Secondary) coil** with N₂ turns connected to the ballistic galvanometer.
   - This toroidal arrangement is chosen to avoid end-effects and ensure a uniform H field.

2. **Cyclic Magnetization:** The current is reversed several times at a fixed value to bring the specimen to a **steady cyclic state** (the B–H curve closes on itself and is reproducible).

3. **Flux Measurement via Ballistic Deflection:** On the final reversal, the sudden change in flux Δφ induces a short-duration current pulse. The **ballistic galvanometer** integrates this pulse to a maximum deflection θ_max.

4. **Calculating H:** H is computed from Ampere's law applied to the toroid (because the field is essentially confined to the ring).
   $$H = \frac{N_1 \, I}{l_m} \quad [\text{A/m}]$$
   where $l_m$ is the **mean magnetic path length** = mean circumference of the ring.

5. **Calculating B:** B is found from the flux change measured by the galvanometer.
   $$B = \frac{\phi}{A} \quad [\text{Tesla}]$$
   where A is the cross-sectional area of the ring.

6. **Plotting the Loop:** Repeating the procedure at various values of I and plotting B vs. H traces the complete hysteresis loop.

### 2.1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Material Selection:** Choosing core materials for transformers, motors, generators.
- **Quality Control of Steel:** Acceptance testing of electrical sheets.
- **Research:** Developing new soft/hard magnetic materials.
- **Calibration of Fluxmeters** and standard references.

---

## 2.2 Wheatstone Bridge (Medium Resistance)

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

The **Wheatstone Bridge** is a four-arm null-deflection DC bridge. Four resistances P, Q, R, and the unknown X form a diamond, energized by a DC source. A sensitive **galvanometer** is connected between the two opposite junctions. At balance (no current through galvanometer), the ratio of resistances in adjacent arms is equal, allowing X to be calculated.

### 2.2.2 Balance Condition Derivation (Logic Steps)

1. Let current $I_1$ flow through arm **P** and **R**; current $I_2$ flow through arm **Q** and **X**.
2. At balance, galvanometer current is zero, so potential at both galvanometer terminals is equal.
3. Therefore: Voltage drop P = Voltage drop Q  AND  Voltage drop R = Voltage drop X.
4. So $I_1 P = I_2 Q$ and $I_1 R = I_2 X$.
5. Dividing: $\frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{X}$, which gives the classical balance equation.

---

## 2.3 Kelvin Double Bridge (Low Resistance)

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

The **Kelvin Double Bridge** is a refined bridge circuit that overcomes the principal limitation of the Wheatstone bridge — the error introduced by **contact resistance** and **lead resistance**, which becomes very significant when measuring resistances below **1 Ω**.

It introduces a **second pair of ratio arms (R₃, R₄)** in addition to the standard ratio arms (R₁, R₂), and a **standard low resistance Rs** as a reference. When both ratio pairs are equal, the lead-resistance term mathematically vanishes.

---

## 2.4 Loss-of-Charge Method (High Resistance)

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle

In the **Loss-of-Charge Method**, a capacitor (of known capacitance C) is charged to a known voltage V₁, and then allowed to discharge *through the unknown high resistance R*. The voltage across the capacitor decays exponentially. By measuring the time **t** taken for the voltage to fall from V₁ to V₂, the resistance R can be computed.

The decay follows:
$$V(t) = V_1 \cdot e^{-t / RC}$$

Because the unknown R is very large, the discharge is slow and can be timed accurately.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Formula Bank for KTU Board Exams (Module 2 Topic). Memorize this table.**

| Sl. | Concept / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Magnetizing field (toroid) | $H = N_1 I \, / \, l_m$ | A/m | $l_m$ = mean circumference |
| 2 | Flux density (specimen) | $B = \phi \, / \, A$ | T (Tesla) | A = cross-sectional area |
| 3 | Ballistic galvanometer | $Q = K_b \cdot \theta_{\max}$ | C | $K_b$ = ballistic constant |
| 4 | Charge–flux linkage | $Q = N_2 \cdot \Delta\phi \, / \, R_{\text{tot}}$ | C | $R_{\text{tot}}$ = search circuit resistance |
| 5 | Flux change on reversal | $\Delta\phi = 2 \phi_{\max}$ | Wb | From +φ to –φ |
| 6 | Combined (ballistic) | $\phi = K_b \theta R_{\text{tot}} \, / \, (2 N_2)$ | Wb | **Master equation** |
| 7 | Wheatstone balance | $X = R \cdot Q \, / \, P$ | Ω | P, Q = ratio arms; R = known |
| 8 | Kelvin Double Bridge | $R_x = R_s \cdot (R_1 / R_2)$ (with $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_4$) | Ω | Second-term vanishes if ratios are equal |
| 9 | Loss-of-Charge (R) | $R = t \, / \, (C \cdot \ln(V_1 / V_2)) = 0.4343 \, t \, / \, (C \cdot \log_{10}(V_1 / V_2))$ | Ω | For high R, t in seconds, C in Farads |
| 10 | Sensitivity of Wheatstone | $S = \theta / \Delta R$ — max when all arms ≈ $R_x$ | — | Bridge most sensitive near balance |
| 11 | Hysteresis loss (Steinmetz) | $W_h = \eta \cdot B_{\max}^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V$ | J/m³/cycle | $\eta$ = Steinmetz coefficient |
| 12 | Insulation resistance | $R_{\text{ins}} = V_{\text{test}} \, / \, I_{\text{leak}}$ | Ω | Test by **Megger** at 500/1000 V DC |
| 13 | Earth resistance (Fall-of-Pot.) | $R_E = V \, / \, I$ (after scaling) | Ω | Should be < 1 Ω for HV substations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL Anti-Table-Break Rule (Internal):** In your notebook, write the absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ (with curly braces) — but in the answer sheet's printed formula, always use the form shown above to avoid the markdown table-breaking pipe `|` conflict.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications — A Summary

- **Power Industry:** Routine testing of transformer cores (B–H curve) and field windings (Wheatstone), and bus-bar joints (Kelvin bridge).
- **Switchgear Manufacturing:** Insulation resistance (Megger) at 5 kV for 11 kV equipment.
- **Substations:** Earth-pit resistance tested annually (must be < 1 Ω for HV).
- **Cable Industry:** Insulation resistance of XLPE cables, loss-of-charge on cable reels.
- **Electronic Industry:** RTD calibration, resistor tolerance sorting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation — The Ballistic-Galvanometer Equation for Method of Reversal

### 3.1.1 Setup and Definitions

A toroidal specimen of ferromagnetic material has:
- **Mean magnetic path length** = $l_m$ [metres]
- **Cross-sectional area** = $A$ [$m^2$]
- **Primary (magnetizing) winding** = $N_1$ turns
- **Secondary (search) winding** = $N_2$ turns
- Total resistance of the search circuit (galvanometer + search coil) = $R_{tot}$ [Ω]
- **Ballistic constant** of the galvanometer = $K_b$ [Coulomb per radian]
- Deflection observed = $\theta_{max}$ [radians or scale divisions]

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Defining H in the Specimen (Ampere's Circuital Law)

For a toroid, by Ampere's Law, the magnetomotive force (MMF) around the mean path equals the enclosed current:

$$\oint H \cdot dl = N_1 I$$

Because H is approximately uniform along the mean path:

$$H \cdot l_m = N_1 I$$

Therefore, the magnetizing field intensity:

$$H = \frac{N_1 I}{l_m} \quad [\text{A/m}]$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Flux Linkage with Search Coil

The total flux linking the search coil is:

$$\Psi = N_2 \phi = N_2 \cdot B \cdot A$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — EMF Induced in Search Coil (Faraday's Law)

When the flux changes with time, the induced EMF is:

$$e(t) = -\frac{d\Psi}{dt} = -N_2 A \frac{dB}{dt}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Instantaneous Current in Search Circuit

By Ohm's law, the instantaneous current is:

$$i(t) = \frac{e(t)}{R_{tot}} = -\frac{N_2 A}{R_{tot}} \cdot \frac{dB}{dt}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Total Charge That Flows (Integration)

Integrate the current over the entire transient:

$$Q = \int i(t) \, dt = -\frac{N_2 A}{R_{tot}} \int_{B_1}^{B_2} dB = \frac{N_2 A (B_1 - B_2)}{R_{tot}}$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Specialize to a Full Reversal

In a reversal, $B$ changes from $+B$ to $-B$, so $B_1 - B_2 = 2B$. Thus:

$$Q = \frac{2 N_2 A B}{R_{tot}}$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Relate Charge to Galvanometer Deflection

The ballistic galvanometer integrates the charge pulse and the maximum deflection $\theta_{max}$ is:

$$Q = K_b \cdot \theta_{max}$$

### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Solve for B (Master Equation)

Equating the two expressions for Q:

$$K_b \cdot \theta_{max} = \frac{2 N_2 A B}{R_{tot}}$$

Solving for B:

$$B = \frac{K_b \cdot \theta_{max} \cdot R_{tot}}{2 N_2 A} \quad [\text{Tesla}]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Equation for the Method of Reversal:**
> $$B = \frac{K_b \cdot \theta \cdot R_{tot}}{2 N_2 \cdot A}, \qquad H = \frac{N_1 I}{l_m}$$
> These two equations give one point $(H, B)$ on the B–H curve. Repeating for several currents gives the complete loop.

### 3.1.10 Step 9 — Determining the Ballistic Constant K_b (Calibration)

The galvanometer's ballistic constant is determined using a **standard mutual inductor** of mutual inductance **M** (Henry). A known current change $\Delta I$ in the primary induces a known charge:

$$Q_{cal} = \frac{M \cdot \Delta I}{R_{cal}}$$

The corresponding deflection is $\theta_{cal}$, so:

$$K_b = \frac{M \cdot \Delta I}{R_{cal} \cdot \theta_{cal}}$$

This calibration is done once before the experiment.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Wheatstone Bridge Balance Condition

### 3.2.1 Circuit Definition

The bridge has four arms: P, Q, R, and the unknown X, forming a closed loop ABCDA. A DC source E with a small series resistor is connected between A and C. A galvanometer is connected between B and D.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $I_1$ = current through arms P and R. Let $I_2$ = current through arms Q and X. Let $I_g$ = galvanometer current (zero at balance).

**Kirchhoff's Current Law at node B:**
$$I_1 = I_2 + I_g$$

**At balance, $I_g = 0$, so:**
$$I_1 = I_2$$

**KVL in loop ABDA:**
$$I_1 P - I_2 Q = 0 \implies I_1 P = I_2 Q$$

**KVL in loop BCDB:**
$$I_1 R - I_2 X = 0 \implies I_1 R = I_2 X$$

**Dividing the two equations:**

$$\frac{I_1 P}{I_1 R} = \frac{I_2 Q}{I_2 X}$$

$$\frac{P}{R} = \frac{Q}{X}$$

**Therefore the balance condition:**

$$X = \frac{Q \cdot R}{P} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{X}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The arms P and Q together form the **ratio arms**, and R is the **standard (variable) arm**. The unknown is calculated from the balance condition.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Kelvin Double Bridge Balance Equation

### 3.3.1 Circuit Description

The Kelvin double bridge has:
- **Unknown resistance** $R_x$ and **standard low resistance** $R_s$ in series, carrying current I.
- **First ratio arms**: $R_1$ and $R_2$.
- **Second ratio arms**: $R_3$ and $R_4$.
- A shorting link (yoke) of resistance $r$ between $R_x$ and $R_s$.
- A galvanometer between the junction of $R_1, R_3$ and the junction of $R_2, R_4$.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $I$ = current through $R_x$ and $R_s$ in series.
Let $i$ = current through the ratio-arm network.
Let $i_g$ = galvanometer current (= 0 at balance).

**EMF drop equation across the upper arms (galvanometer path):**
$$R_x \cdot I + \text{(potential drop in yoke r split between R3 and R4)} = R_1 \cdot i_1$$

At balance, applying KVL around the two loops and using star-delta transformation for the yoke $r$, $R_1$, $R_2$, one can show:

**General balance equation of Kelvin double bridge:**

$$R_x = R_s \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_2} + \frac{r \cdot R_4}{R_3 + R_4 + r} \cdot \left( \frac{R_1}{R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_4} \right)$$

**Special simplified case (the engineering case):**
If we choose $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$ (i.e., both ratio arms equal), then $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_4$ and the second term **vanishes identically**, leaving:

$$\boxed{R_x = R_s \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** By making the ratio arms equal, the troublesome term (which contains the yoke resistance $r$ and contact resistances) cancels out completely. This is the genius of Lord Kelvin's design.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation — Loss-of-Charge Method

### 3.4.1 Circuit and Initial Conditions

A capacitor of capacitance **C** (Farads) is charged to an initial voltage **V₁** by briefly connecting to a DC source. The capacitor is then disconnected and allowed to discharge through the unknown resistance **R**.

### 3.4.2 Differential Equation of Discharge

The voltage across the capacitor and resistor are equal (KVL). The discharge current equals the rate of decrease of charge:

$$i = -\frac{dq}{dt} = \frac{V}{R}$$

But $V = q/C$, so:

$$-\frac{dq}{dt} = \frac{q}{RC}$$

### 3.4.3 Solving the ODE

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dq}{q} = -\frac{dt}{RC}$$

Integrating from $q_1$ to $q_2$ (i.e., $V_1$ to $V_2$):

$$\ln\left(\frac{q_2}{q_1}\right) = -\frac{t}{RC}$$

Since $q = CV$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = -\frac{t}{RC}$$

### 3.4.4 Final Formula for R

$$\boxed{R = \frac{t}{C \cdot \ln\left(V_1/V_2\right)} = \frac{0.4343 \, t}{C \cdot \log_{10}\left(V_1/V_2\right)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This method is most accurate for resistances from **100 kΩ to several TΩ** (Tera-ohms), such as the leakage resistance of cable insulation, transformer oil, or the surface resistance of insulating materials.

### 3.4.5 Numerical Example (Worked Out)

Suppose C = 1 µF, V₁ = 200 V, V₂ = 100 V, t = 5 seconds.

$$R = \frac{0.4343 \times 5}{10^{-6} \times \log_{10}(200/100)} = \frac{2.1715}{10^{-6} \times 0.3010}$$

$$R = \frac{2.1715}{0.301 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.21 \times 10^{6} \, \Omega = 7.21 \, \text{MΩ}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation Summary — Hysteresis Loss (Steinmetz Law)

Although tangential, this frequently appears in KTU exams as a 3-mark sub-question.

**Steinmetz's empirical law** gives the hysteresis loss per cubic metre per cycle:

$$w_h = \eta \cdot B_{\max}^{1.6} \cdot f \quad [\text{J/m}^3/\text{cycle}]$$

For a volume V of material, the total hysteresis loss per second (i.e., power loss) is:

$$P_h = \eta \cdot B_{\max}^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

where:
- $\eta$ = **Steinmetz hysteresis coefficient** (material-dependent, e.g., 500 for silicon steel)
- $B_{\max}$ = maximum flux density [T]
- $f$ = frequency of magnetization reversal [Hz]
- $V$ = volume of the core [$m^3$]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exponent 1.6 is empirical — it may vary between 1.5 and 2.5 depending on the material, but 1.6 is the standard KTU textbook value.

---
