$$\ln\left(\frac{V_1}{V_3}\right) - \ln\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{RC}$$

Using the log property $\ln(A) - \ln(B) = \ln(A/B)$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_3}\right) = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{RC}$$

**Step 4: Solve for R and convert to base-10 log.**

$$R = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{C \cdot \ln(V_2/V_3)}$$

$$\boxed{R = \frac{0.4343 \, (t_2 - t_1)}{C \cdot \log_{10}(V_2/V_3)}}$$

**Why is this superior?**
- Stray leakage paths affect both readings **identically**, and they cancel out in the subtraction.
- Slow capacitor dielectric absorption is also largely nullified.
- This is the **board-preferred** form for high-precision KTU problems.

## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Solved Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A capacitor of $0.2 \, \mu F$ is charged to 200 V and then allowed to discharge through an unknown resistance. After 60 seconds, the voltage falls to 80 V. Calculate the value of the unknown resistance.

**Given:**
- $C = 0.2 \, \mu F = 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \, F$
- $V_1 = 200 \, V$
- $V_2 = 80 \, V$
- $t = 60 \, s$

**Step 1: Write the loss-of-charge formula.**

$$R = \frac{0.4343 \, t}{C \cdot \log_{10}(V_1/V_2)}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$R = \frac{0.4343 \times 60}{0.2 \times 10^{-6} \times \log_{10}(200/80)}$$

**Step 3: Compute the logarithmic term.**

$$\log_{10}(200/80) = \log_{10}(2.5) = 0.3979$$

**Step 4: Substitute back.**

$$R = \frac{0.4343 \times 60}{0.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.3979}$$

$$R = \frac{26.058}{7.958 \times 10^{-8}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the final value.**

$$\boxed{R = 3.27 \times 10^{8} \, \Omega = 327 \, M\Omega}$$

This is a typical insulation-resistance order of magnitude — confirming that the loss-of-charge method is appropriate for **MΩ to GΩ** ranges.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Variation

| Parameter Increased | Effect on R (computed) | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Discharge time `t` ↑ | R ↑ (linearly) | Larger R → slower discharge |
| Capacitance `C` ↑ | R ↓ (inversely) | Larger C stores more charge, decays slower per R |
| Ratio $V_1/V_2$ ↑ | R ↑ (logarithmically) | More voltage dropped → longer effective decay |
| Stray leakage | R reads lower | Parallel leakage adds to discharge path |
| Temperature | R ↓ (insulators) | Increased carrier mobility |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Diagram — Loss of Charge Method

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional circuit topology** for the Loss of Charge Method. It captures every component, switch position, and signal path required for understanding the test procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCS[DC Supply V]:::source
    SW1[Switch S1]:::switch
    SW2[Switch S2]:::switch
    CSTD[Standard Capacitor C]:::capacitor
    RUNK[Unknown Resistance R]:::unknown
    VM[Electrostatic Voltmeter V]:::meter
    TMR[Stopwatch t]:::timer
    GND[Ground]:::gnd

    DCS --- SW1
    SW1 --- CSTD
    CSTD --- SW2
    SW2 --- RUNK
    CSTD --- VM
    VM --- GND
    RUNK --- GND
    VM -.measures.-> TMR

    classDef source fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef switch fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef capacitor fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef unknown fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#330099,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef meter fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef timer fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#994d00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gnd fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### Operating Sequence Explained via the Diagram

| Step | Switch S₁ | Switch S₂ | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Charge** | Closed | Open | DC supply charges capacitor C to V₁ |
| **2. Isolate** | Open | Open | Capacitor isolated; voltmeter reads V₁ |
| **3. Discharge** | Open | Closed | Capacitor discharges through R |
| **4. Time & Measure** | Open | Closed | At time t, voltmeter reads V₂ |
| **5. Compute** | — | — | R = 0.4343 t / [C log₁₀(V₁/V₂)] |

## 4.2 Discharge Curve — Voltage vs Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AX["Time axis t (seconds)"]:::axis --> ORIGIN["0"]:::axis
    ORIGIN --> P1["V1 at t=0"]:::point
    P1 --> P2["V2 at t=t1"]:::point
    P2 --> P3["V3 at t=t2"]:::point
    P3 --> PASY["Voltage approaches 0 as t increases"]:::point

    classDef axis fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef point fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### Tabular form of the same curve

| Time `t` | Voltage `v(t)` | Fraction of V₁ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $V_1$ | 100.0 % |
| $0.693 \, RC$ | $0.5 \, V_1$ | 50.0 % (half-life) |
| $RC$ | $0.368 \, V_1$ | 36.8 % (one time constant) |
| $2 \, RC$ | $0.135 \, V_1$ | 13.5 % |
| $5 \, RC$ | $0.0067 \, V_1$ | 0.67 % (≈ fully discharged) |

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Discharge Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BLOCK_A["Stage A: Initial Charging Phase"]:::stage1
    BLOCK_B["Stage B: Isolation and Stabilization"]:::stage2
    BLOCK_C["Stage C: Controlled Discharge"]:::stage3
    BLOCK_D["Stage D: Voltage Sampling at Time t"]:::stage4
    BLOCK_E["Stage E: Logarithmic Computation of R"]:::stage5
    BLOCK_F["Output: Resistance Value R"]:::output

    BLOCK_A --> BLOCK_B
    BLOCK_B --> BLOCK_C
    BLOCK_C --> BLOCK_D
    BLOCK_D --> BLOCK_E
    BLOCK_E --> BLOCK_F

    classDef stage1 fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage2 fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage3 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage4 fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage5 fill:#80bfff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#ffe680,stroke:#997a00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Loss of Charge Method. Why is it preferred for high-resistance measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Loss of Charge Method is a time-based technique used to measure very high resistances (typically above **10⁶ Ω**). The unknown resistance R is connected across a charged standard capacitor C, and the time `t` taken for the capacitor voltage to decay from `V₁` to `V₂` is measured. R is computed from:

$$R = \frac{0.4343 \, t}{C \cdot \log_{10}(V_1/V_2)}$$

It is preferred for high-resistance measurement because conventional Wheatstone bridges lose sensitivity beyond ~10⁶ Ω due to very small detector currents, whereas the loss-of-charge method can accurately measure resistances up to **10¹⁵ Ω** by exploiting the long discharge time-constants involved.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is an electrostatic voltmeter used to measure V₂ in the loss of charge method? Can a moving-coil voltmeter be used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **electrostatic voltmeter** is used because it has an **extremely high input impedance (theoretically infinite)** and draws **negligible current** from the circuit under test. This is essential because any current drawn by the voltmeter would itself discharge the capacitor and corrupt the reading of `V₂`.

A **moving-coil voltmeter cannot be used** because it has a relatively low input impedance (typically 10 kΩ/V to 20 kΩ/V) and would draw appreciable current, causing additional discharge paths and leading to **erroneously low** values of measured resistance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the unknown resistance measured by the Loss of Charge Method, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standard capacitor of 0.5 μF is charged to 250 V and discharged through an unknown resistance. The voltage drops to 100 V in 90 seconds. Calculate the unknown resistance. If the same capacitor is used and the voltage drops from 250 V to 200 V in 50 seconds, and then from 200 V to 160 V in another 50 seconds, find the resistance using the **differential method** and comment on which value is more accurate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Derivation [7 marks]**

**Assumption 1:** The standard capacitor has negligible leakage and a stable, well-known capacitance value C.

**Assumption 2:** The voltmeter used to read V₂ has infinite input impedance (electrostatic voltmeter).

**Assumption 3:** Stray capacitances and leakage paths are negligible.

**Assumption 4:** The discharge is purely through the unknown resistance R.

**Step 1:** The capacitor is first charged to voltage V₁.

**Step 2:** At t = 0, the source is removed and the capacitor is allowed to discharge through R.

**Step 3:** Apply KVL to the discharge loop:

$$v + iR = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad i = -C\frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$v = RC\frac{dv}{dt} \implies \frac{dv}{v} = -\frac{dt}{RC}$$

**Step 5:** Integrating between limits:

$$\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dv}{v} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = \frac{t}{RC}$$

**Step 6:** Solving for R:

$$R = \frac{t}{C \cdot \ln(V_1/V_2)} = \frac{0.4343 \, t}{C \cdot \log_{10}(V_1/V_2)}$$

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical [7 marks]**

**Single-interval method:**

$$R_1 = \frac{0.4343 \times 90}{0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times \log_{10}(250/100)}$$

$$\log_{10}(250/100) = \log_{10}(2.5) = 0.3979$$

$$R_1 = \frac{39.087}{0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.3979} = \frac{39.087}{1.9895 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\boxed{R_1 = 1.965 \times 10^{8} \, \Omega \approx 196.5 \, M\Omega}$$

**Differential method (using V₂ → V₃ over interval t₂ − t₁ = 50 s):**

$$R_2 = \frac{0.4343 \times 50}{0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times \log_{10}(200/160)}$$

$$\log_{10}(200/160) = \log_{10}(1.25) = 0.0969$$

$$R_2 = \frac{21.715}{0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.0969} = \frac{21.715}{4.845 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\boxed{R_2 = 4.482 \times 10^{8} \, \Omega \approx 448.2 \, M\Omega}$$

**Comment (1 mark within the 7):**

> [!NOTE]
> The differential method gives a **more accurate** value of R because it cancels the effects of capacitor leakage and stray capacitances, which add an extra discharge path during the long 90-second interval. The single-interval result (196.5 MΩ) is **lower** because the measured decay includes the unwanted parallel leakage currents, making the apparent R smaller than the true value.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of the Loss of Charge Method with a neat circuit diagram. Discuss the role of the electrostatic voltmeter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1 μF capacitor charged to 500 V is discharged through a resistance of 500 MΩ. Calculate: (i) the time constant, (ii) the voltage after 100 s, (iii) the time required for the voltage to fall to 250 V. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Explanation [7 marks]**

**Working Principle:**

The loss of charge method works on the principle that a charged capacitor discharges through a resistance at a rate determined by the RC time constant. By precisely measuring the time taken for the voltage to decay between two known values, the unknown resistance can be calculated.

**Circuit Operation (3 marks):**

1. A standard capacitor C is charged to a known voltage V₁ using a DC source through switch S₁.
2. Switch S₁ is opened and switch S₂ is closed, connecting the unknown resistance R across C.
3. The capacitor begins to discharge exponentially through R.
4. A stopwatch is started simultaneously with S₂ closure.
5. At a measured time t, the residual voltage V₂ is read on the electrostatic voltmeter.
6. R is computed using the standard formula.

**Role of Electrostatic Voltmeter (2 marks):**

The electrostatic voltmeter operates on the electrostatic force between charged plates and has **infinite input impedance** (no current is drawn from the circuit). This is essential because any current drawn by the measuring instrument would itself discharge the capacitor and introduce measurement error. A PMMC voltmeter, with finite resistance (typically 10–20 kΩ/V), is unsuitable as it would create a parallel discharge path and falsely indicate a smaller R.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical [7 marks]**

**Given:** C = 1 μF = 1 × 10⁻⁶ F; V₁ = 500 V; R = 500 MΩ = 500 × 10⁶ Ω

**Part (i): Time constant**

$$\tau = RC = 500 \times 10^{6} \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 500 \, s$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 500 \, \text{seconds}}$$

**Part (ii): Voltage after 100 s**

$$v(t) = V_1 e^{-t/RC} = 500 \cdot e^{-100/500} = 500 \cdot e^{-0.2}$$

$$e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

$$v(100) = 500 \times 0.8187$$

$$\boxed{v(100) = 409.4 \, V}$$

**Part (iii): Time for voltage to fall to 250 V**

$$250 = 500 \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

$$\frac{250}{500} = e^{-t/RC} \implies 0.5 = e^{-t/500}$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$\ln(0.5) = -\frac{t}{500} \implies -0.6931 = -\frac{t}{500}$$

$$t = 0.6931 \times 500 = 346.5 \, s$$

$$\boxed{t = 346.5 \, \text{seconds} \approx 5.78 \, \text{minutes}}$$

This corresponds to the **half-life** of the discharge, $t_{0.5} = 0.6931 \, RC$, confirming the derivation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Loss of Charge Method Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.4343 constant:** Many students write $R = \dfrac{t}{C \ln(V_1/V_2)}$ directly. While mathematically equivalent, KTU board examiners **specifically allocate marks** for the log₁₀ form with 0.4343. If a numerical value is asked and you use ln, your answer will be off by a factor of 2.3026 and **lose 1 full mark**.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong capacitor units:** The capacitance must be in **farads (F)**, not microfarads (μF) or nanofarads (nF). A $0.2 \, \mu F$ capacitor means $C = 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \, F$. Mixing up units causes R to come out wrong by **3 to 6 orders of magnitude**.
>
> 3. **Confusing log₁₀ and ln:** KTU 2024 papers explicitly specify either "log" (which means log₁₀) or "ln". Using the wrong base **invalidates the entire numerical answer**.
>
> 4. **Skipping the differential method explanation:** When asked to "comment on accuracy", students often skip explaining *why* the differential method cancels stray effects. This **costs 1–2 marks** in 7-mark problems.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the role of electrostatic voltmeter:** Examiners frequently ask *why* a normal voltmeter cannot be used. A vague "because of high resistance" answer is insufficient; you must mention **infinite input impedance / no current drawn**.
>
> 6. **Unit errors in numerical answers:** Always express the final answer with proper SI prefix (MΩ, GΩ, TΩ). Writing $3.27 \times 10^{8} \, \Omega$ is correct; writing "$3.27 \times 10^8$" without units **loses 0.5 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Loss of Charge Method**

- 🎯 **Purpose:** Measures **very high resistances** (10⁶ Ω to 10¹⁵ Ω) such as cable insulation, dielectric leakage, and high-value precision resistors.

- 🎯 **Core Formula (MUST memorize):**

$$R = \frac{0.4343 \, t}{C \, \log_{10}(V_1/V_2)}$$

- 🎯 **Differential (Ratio) Formula (for higher accuracy):**

$$R = \frac{0.4343 \, (t_2 - t_1)}{C \, \log_{10}(V_2/V_3)}$$

- 🎯 **Three operational stages:** (1) Charge capacitor to V₁ → (2) Discharge through R for time t → (3) Read residual voltage V₂.

- 🎯 **Voltmeter type:** Only **electrostatic voltmeter** (infinite input impedance) is acceptable. PMMC instruments corrupt the reading.

- 🎯 **Key assumption:** Capacitor leakage and stray capacitance are negligible (or compensated by the differential method).

- 🎯 **Universal constants to remember:**
  - $0.4343 = 1/\ln(10) = 1/2.3026$
  - $2.3026 = \ln(10)$
  - $0.6931 = \ln(2) = $ half-life coefficient
  - $0.368 = 1/e$ = voltage at one time-constant

- 🎯 **Time constant definition:** $\tau = RC$. After time $\tau$, voltage falls to 36.8 % of initial value.

- 🎯 **Advantages:**
  1. Measures resistances far beyond the range of Wheatstone/Kelvin bridges.
  2. Simple circuit; no bridge balance required.
  3. Differential method cancels stray and leakage errors.

- 🎯 **Disadvantages:**
  1. Requires a high-quality, low-leakage standard capacitor.
  2. Time-consuming (especially for very high R).
  3. Sensitive to temperature, humidity, and surface leakage.
  4. Accuracy depends heavily on voltmeter precision and timing accuracy.

- 🎯 **Typical KTU question pattern:**
  - Part A (3 marks): Define the method / state formula / explain voltmeter role.
  - Part B (14 marks, choice): Derive formula + solve numerical OR explain circuit + solve numerical.

- 🎯 **Numerical checks:** For R in MΩ and C in μF, expected time `t` is typically **tens to hundreds of seconds** — a quick sanity check for any numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Earth Resistance. Measurement of Inductance - Maxwell's Inductance Bridge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT411 - ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENT AND MEASURING INSTRUMENTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Earth Resistance. Measurement of Inductance - Maxwell's Inductance Bridge # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Earth Resistance — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Earth Resistance):** > The **Earth Resistance** (also called **Earth Electrode Resistance** or **Ground Resistance**) is the ohmic resistance offered by the earth electrode to the flow of electric current into the surrounding soil when a fault current or leakage current is dissipated into the ground. It is measured in **Ohms (Ω)** and represents the total opposition encountered between the earth electrode and a remote reference point of zero potential (true earth). The resistance of an earth electrode is **NOT** the resistance of the metal rod itself, but rather the **resistive coupling between the electrode surface and the bulk earth** (soil). It depends on: - **Soil resistivity (ρ)** — measured in **Ω·m** - **Surface area of contact** between the electrode and the soil - **Depth and shape** of the electrode - **Moisture content** and **temperature** of the soil - **Concentration of soluble salts** in the soil ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Faucet Drain" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine pouring water into a sink. The earth electrode is the **drain hole**, and the soil is the **plumbing network beneath the sink**. The earth resistance is analogous to the *restriction at the drain*. A wide drain (large electrode surface) with clean, moist pipes (low-resistivity soil) lets water flow freely → **low resistance**. A clogged, narrow drain in dry, rocky soil → **high resistance**, which is dangerous because fault current cannot dissipate quickly. A safe earth electrode must typically have a resistance of **less than 1 Ω** for large substations and **less than 5 Ω** for domestic/commercial installations as per **IS 3043**. --- ## 1.2 Maxwell's Inductance Bridge — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Maxwell's Inductance Bridge):** > **Maxwell's Inductance Bridge** is an AC wheatstone-bridge configuration specifically designed for the **precise measurement of an unknown inductance** (L_x) in terms of a **known standard capacitance (C)**. It uses a standard resistor, a standard (or variable) non-inductive resistor, and a standard capacitor as the reference elements. The unknown inductance is determined by equating the in-phase and quadrature components at the **balance condition**, yielding both the inductance value and its associated **quality factor (Q)**. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Balance Scale for Impedance" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an old-fashioned two-pan balance scale. One pan holds the unknown impedance (Z_x), and the other holds four carefully selected reference impedances (R₁, R₂, R₃, C). A sensitive null detector (like a vibration meter on the scale's needle) tells you when the scale is perfectly level. At the "level" position, the unknown inductance can be directly calculated from the four reference values — just like weighing an object by placing known counterweights on the other pan. ### Why Maxwell's Bridge is Significant > [!TIP] > **Key Engineering Insight:** Maxwell's bridge was a *landmark invention* (circa 1870s) because it solved a major problem: standard inductors are **bulky, lossy, and stray-field sensitive**, while standard capacitors are **compact, stable, and highly precise**. By using a **capacitor as the reference**, Maxwell cleverly made inductance measurement a *comparison against a more accurate standard*. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values | Parameter | Standard Value/Unit | Notes | |---|---|---| | **Soil resistivity (ρ)** | 1 – 10,000 Ω·m | Rocky: high; Marshy: low | | **Acceptable earth resistance** | < 1 Ω (substation) | Per IS 3043 | | **Frequency of earth tester** | ~50 Hz to 1000 Hz | Avoids 50 Hz interference | | **Standard grid frequency** | 50 Hz | Indian standard | | **Null detector for AC bridge** | Vibration galvanometer / Headphones | High sensitivity at balance | | **Reference capacitance range** | 0.001 μF to 1 μF | Polystyrene/foil type preferred | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Methods of Earth Resistance Measurement There are **three principal methods** specified in the KTU 2024 syllabus: ### 2.1.1 Fall of Potential Method (Three-Point Method) This is the **most widely used** and internationally accepted method. **Principle:** Current is passed between the **Earth Electrode (E)** under test and a **Current Probe (C)** placed far away. The voltage drop is measured between the earth electrode and a **Potential Probe (P)** placed in between. The ratio V/I gives the resistance. **Procedure (Step-by-step Logic):** 1. **Disconnect** the earth electrode from the installation to avoid parallel paths. 2. Drive the **Current Probe (C)** at a distance of **at least 40 m** from the earth electrode. 3. Drive the **Potential Probe (P)** at a midpoint between E and C (initially). 4. Connect an **AC source (Earth Tester)** between E and C. 5. Vary the position of P in steps, recording the resistance R = V/I at each step. 6. Plot a graph of **R vs. position of P**. The portion where R becomes **constant** gives the **true earth resistance**. **Why this works:** The current flows radially outward from E through the soil. Beyond a certain distance, the current density becomes uniform, and the potential gradient becomes linear. ### 2.1.2 Earth Tester (Direct-Reading Megger-Type Instrument) > [!NOTE] > A hand-cranked or electronic **Earth Tester** is essentially a modified Wheatstone bridge powered by a built-in hand generator (~80–120 RPM producing ~500 Hz). It provides **direct reading of earth resistance** on a calibrated scale. It uses two auxiliary electrodes (P and C) and a null-balance detector. **Internal Arrangement:** - A hand-cranked **AC generator** supplies current to E–C. - A **ratio-arm bridge** balances the potential drop with a calibrated rheostat. - Balance is achieved by adjusting the rheostat, and the scale reads **resistance directly**. ### 2.1.3 Compensation Method (Null-Type Earth Tester) Uses a **null detector** (vibration galvanometer) and is preferred for laboratory accuracy. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Earth Resistance | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R_E = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Ohm's law applied to electrode | Ω | | 2 | $R_E = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{a}\right) - 1 \right]$ | Rod electrode resistance (deep) | Ω | | 3 | $R_E = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{2L}{a}\right) \right]$ | Rod electrode resistance (shallow) | Ω | | 4 | $R_E = \dfrac{\rho}{4r}$ | Hemisphere/sphere electrode | Ω | | 5 | $R_E = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r}$ | Plate electrode (radius r) | Ω | | 6 | $I_{earth} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{R_E}$ | Earth-fault current | A | | 7 | $V_t = I_{earth} \cdot R_E$ | Touch potential | V | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** Earth resistance measurement is mandatory in power system protection, lightning protection design, and safety audits. Substations with poor earthing can cause dangerous **step-and-touch potentials** endangering personnel. --- ## 2.3 Maxwell's Inductance Bridge — Theoretical Analysis ### 2.3.1 Circuit Configuration The bridge has four arms: - **Arm AB:** Unknown inductance $Z_x = R_x + j\omega L_x$ (unknown inductor in series with its own resistance) - **Arm AD:** Variable non-inductive resistor $R_3$ - **Arm BC:** Fixed non-inductive resistor $R_2$ - **Arm DC:** Standard lossless capacitor $C_4$ in **parallel** with resistor $R_4$ (this specific arrangement makes the bridge superior to Hay's bridge for medium-Q coils) The bridge is excited by an **AC source** at the diagonal A–C, and a **null detector** is connected across B–D. ### 2.3.2 Phasor Logic at Balance > [!IMPORTANT] > **Balance Condition:** At balance, **no current flows through the detector (D)**, which means the potential at B equals the potential at D. This gives: > $$\vec{V}_{AB} = \vec{V}_{AD} \quad \text{and} \quad \vec{V}_{BC} = \vec{V}_{DC}$$ > Equivalently, the magnitude and phase of products of opposite arms are equal: > $$Z_{AB} \cdot Z_{DC} = Z_{AD} \cdot Z_{BC}$$ ### 2.3.3 Key Steps of Balance 1. Energize the bridge with an AC source of known frequency ω. 2. The standard capacitor is **lossless** (purely reactive), so its impedance is $Z_{C} = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4}$. 3. $R_4$ is placed in **parallel** with $C_4$ to model a **practical lossy capacitor's reference**, and the equivalent impedance is: $$Z_{DC} = \dfrac{R_4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4}}{R_4 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4}} = \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4}$$ 4. The arm AD has purely resistive $R_3$, while arm BC has $R_2$ (purely resistive). 5. Equating real and imaginary parts at balance yields two equations that determine $L_x$ and $R_x$ simultaneously. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Maxwell's Inductance Bridge | # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Unknown Inductance | $L_x = R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot C_4$ | Independent of frequency | | 2 | Unknown Resistance | $R_x = \dfrac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_4}$ | Also independent of frequency | | 3 | Quality Factor | $Q_x = \dfrac{\omega L_x}{R_x} = \omega R_4 C_4$ | Medium-Q coils: 1 < Q < 10 | | 4 | Storage Factor | $\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{1}{\omega R_4 C_4}$ | Phase angle of unknown | | 5 | Balance Source Frequency | $f = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$ | Stable AC supply required | | 6 | Equivalent Impedance of parallel arm | $Z_{DC} = \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4}$ | Reference arm | > [!TIP] > **Why these equations are frequency-independent:** Maxwell's bridge is famous because $L_x$ and $R_x$ do **not depend on the supply frequency**. This makes the bridge extremely **convenient** — the AC source can drift in frequency without affecting the result, provided the detector remains sensitive. This is a major advantage over Hay's bridge (used for high-Q coils). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Maxwell's Inductance Bridge — Full Balance Equations > [!NOTE] > **Notation used:** All impedances are phasor quantities. The bridge is balanced when the **detector current = 0**, which means $V_B = V_D$, equivalently $V_{AB} = V_{AD}$ and $V_{BC} = V_{DC}$. ### Step 1 — List the Four Arm Impedances $$ \begin{aligned} Z_{AB} &= R_x + j\omega L_x \quad &\text{(unknown inductor + its resistance)} \\ Z_{AD} &= R_3 \quad &\text{(purely resistive, variable)} \\ Z_{BC} &= R_2 \quad &\text{(purely resistive, fixed)} \\ Z_{DC} &= \dfrac{R_4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4}}{R_4 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4}} = \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4} \quad &\text{(R}_4 \text{ parallel with C}_4\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the General Wheatstone Bridge Balance Equation For a balanced AC bridge, the **opposite-arm product equality** holds: $$Z_{AB} \cdot Z_{DC} = Z_{AD} \cdot Z_{BC}$$ **Logic:** This is the phasor generalization of the DC Wheatstone bridge condition $P/Q = R/S$. The product of impedances across one diagonal must equal the product across the other diagonal at balance. ### Step 3 — Substitute the Impedances $$ \begin{aligned} \left( R_x + j\omega L_x \right) \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4} = R_3 \cdot R_2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Multiply Both Sides by the Denominator $$ \begin{aligned} \left( R_x + j\omega L_x \right) \cdot R_4 = R_3 \cdot R_2 \cdot \left( 1 + j\omega R_4 C_4 \right) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Expand the Left-Hand Side (LHS) $$ \begin{aligned} R_4 R_x + j\omega R_4 L_x = R_2 R_3 + j\omega R_2 R_3 R_4 C_4 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Equate Real Parts The **real part** of the LHS must equal the **real part** of the RHS: $$ \begin{aligned} R_4 R_x = R_2 R_3 \end{aligned} $$ Solving for the unknown resistance: $$ \boxed{R_x = \dfrac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_4}} $$ ### Step 7 — Equate Imaginary Parts The **coefficient of $j$** on the LHS must equal that on the RHS: $$ \begin{aligned} \omega R_4 L_x = \omega R_2 R_3 R_4 C_4 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The frequency $\omega$ and $R_4$ appear on both sides and cancel out, which is precisely **why the result is frequency-independent**. ### Step 8 — Solve for the Unknown Inductance $$ \begin{aligned} L_x = R_2 R_3 C_4 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{L_x = R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot C_4} $$ ### Step 9 — Derive the Quality Factor The quality factor of the unknown coil is: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_x = \dfrac{\omega L_x}{R_x} = \dfrac{\omega \cdot R_2 R_3 C_4}{\dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_4}} = \omega R_4 C_4 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{Q_x = \omega R_4 C_4} $$ **Engineering Note:** The equation $Q_x = \omega R_4 C_4$ is a **CRITICAL KTU exam formula**. It shows that to measure a coil of a particular Q, we choose $R_4$ and $C_4$ such that their product matches. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Maxwell's vs. Other AC Bridges | Feature | Maxwell's (Inductance) | Hay's Bridge | Anderson Bridge | Wien Bridge | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Reference Element** | Capacitor | Capacitor | Capacitor + Inductor | Capacitor (in both arms) | | **Measures** | Inductance (Medium Q) | Inductance (High Q) | Inductance (any Q) | Capacitance / Frequency | | **Frequency dependence** | **Independent** | Depends on f | Independent | Depends on f | | **Balance complexity** | Two equations (easy) | One equation (easy) | Three equations | Two equations | | **Standard Q range** | 1 < Q < 10 | 10 < Q < 1000 | All Q | All Q | | **Convergence** | Easy | Slow for low Q | Difficult | Moderate | --- ## 3.3 Detailed Numerical Worked Example — Maxwell's Bridge **Problem:** In a Maxwell's inductance bridge, the arms have the following values: $R_2 = 1000\ \Omega$, $R_3 = 500\ \Omega$, $C_4 = 0.1\ \mu F$, and $R_4 = 10\ k\Omega$. The frequency is 1 kHz. Find $L_x$, $R_x$, and the Q-factor. **Solution:** Step 1 — Calculate $L_x$: $$ \begin{aligned} L_x &= R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot C_4 \\ L_x &= 1000 \times 500 \times (0.1 \times 10^{-6}) \\ L_x &= 5 \times 10^5 \times 10^{-7} \\ L_x &= 0.05\ \text{H} \\ L_x &= 50\ \text{mH} \end{aligned} $$ Step 2 — Calculate $R_x$: $$ \begin{aligned} R_x &= \dfrac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_4} \\ R_x &= \dfrac{1000 \times 500}{10000} \\ R_x &= \dfrac{5 \times 10^5}{10^4} \\ R_x &= 50\ \Omega \end{aligned} $$ Step 3 — Calculate $Q_x$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_x &= \omega R_4 C_4 \\ \omega &= 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.2\ \text{rad/s} \\ Q_x &= 6283.2 \times 10000 \times (0.1 \times 10^{-6}) \\ Q_x &= 6283.2 \times 10^{-3} \\ Q_x &\approx 6.28 \end{aligned} $$ Step 4 — Verification using $\omega L_x / R_x$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_x &= \dfrac{\omega L_x}{R_x} = \dfrac{6283.2 \times 0.05}{50} = \dfrac{314.16}{50} = 6.28 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Both formulas must give the same Q-factor.** This is a built-in check that examiners expect students to perform in KTU valuation. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Earth Resistance Measurement — Fall of Potential Method > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block renders the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the Fall of Potential method. The physical arrangement is mapped as an interconnected system of components showing signal flow and measurement logic. ```mermaid flowchart TD ACSrc["AC SOURCE
Earth Tester Generator"] --> CurrElec["EARTH ELECTRODE E
Under Test"] ACSrc --> CurrProbe["CURRENT PROBE C
Driven at 40 m distance"] CurrElec --> SoilRes["SOIL RESISTANCE ZONE
Radial Current Dissipation"] CurrProbe --> SoilRes PotProbe["POTENTIAL PROBE P
Variable position between E and C"] --> SoilRes PotProbe --> Voltmeter["VOLTMETER V
High Impedance"] CurrElec --> Ammeter["AMMETER I
Series Reading"] Voltmeter --> CalcBlock["CALCULATION UNIT
R = V / I"] Ammeter --> CalcBlock CalcBlock --> GraphBlock["GRAPH: R vs Position of P
Plateau Region = True Earth Resistance"] CalcBlock --> ResultNode["TRUE EARTH RESISTANCE R_E
in Ohms"] GraphBlock --> ResultNode ``` ### Component Description Block | Block ID | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | ACSrc | AC Source (Earth Tester) | Provides AC current (typically 50–500 Hz) to avoid 50 Hz mains interference | | CurrElec | Earth Electrode (E) | The electrode whose resistance is being measured | | CurrProbe | Current Probe (C) | Auxiliary electrode to pass current into distant earth | | PotProbe | Potential Probe (P) | Auxiliary electrode to sense the potential gradient | | Voltmeter | High-Z voltmeter | Measures potential drop E–P without loading | | Ammeter | Series ammeter | Measures current E–C | | CalcBlock | Calculator | Computes R = V/I at each P position | | GraphBlock | Plotter | Identifies plateau region | | ResultNode | Final R_E | True earth resistance in Ohms | --- ## 4.2 Maxwell's Inductance Bridge — Circuit Schematic (Mermaid) > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagram below represents the **Wheatstone-style topological arrangement** of Maxwell's bridge. Each arm is labeled with its impedance, and the diagonals show source and detector positions. ```mermaid flowchart TB Source["AC SOURCE
V_s, frequency f"] --- NodeA["Node A"] Source --- NodeC["Node C"] NodeA --- ArmAB["Arm AB
Z_AB = R_x + jωL_x
UNKNOWN INDUCTOR"] ArmAB --- NodeB["Node B"] NodeB --- ArmBC["Arm BC
R_2 = 1000 Ω
Fixed Resistor"] ArmBC --- NodeC NodeA --- ArmAD["Arm AD
R_3 = 500 Ω
Variable Resistor"] ArmAD --- NodeD["Node D"] NodeD --- ArmDC["Arm DC
R_4 || C_4
= R_4 / 1 + jωR_4C_4
Standard Capacitor Arm"] ArmDC --- NodeC NodeB --- Detector["NULL DETECTOR
D - Vibration Galvanometer / Headphones"] NodeD --- Detector Note1["BALANCE CONDITION:
Z_AB x Z_DC = Z_AD x Z_BC
Detector Current = 0"] --- Detector ``` ### Arm-by-Arm Identification Table | Arm | Impedance | Element | Role | |---|---|---|---| | **AB** | $R_x + j\omega L_x$ | Unknown coil (R in series with L) | The DUT (Device Under Test) | | **BC** | $R_2$ | Fixed non-inductive resistor | Reference resistance | | **AD** | $R_3$ | Variable non-inductive resistor | Balance adjustment | | **DC** | $\dfrac{R_4}{1+j\omega R_4 C_4}$ | $R_4$ parallel with $C_4$ | Capacitive reference arm | --- ## 4.3 Fall of Potential Curve — Conceptual Plot ```mermaid flowchart LR AxisX["Distance of P from E (m)"] --> P0["P at E
R = 0"] P0 --> P1["P near E
R rises sharply"] P1 --> P2["P at midpoint
R near true value"] P2 --> P3["P beyond 61.8%
R = TRUE R_E
PLATEAU REGION"] P3 --> P4["P near C
R rises again"] P4 --> P5["P at C
R infinity"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The 61.8% rule (the **61.8% point of the total E–C distance** corresponds to the true $R_E$) is a frequently asked **numerical concept**. It comes from the geometry of overlapping equipotential regions. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Define earth resistance. Why is it important to keep it low?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Earth resistance is the ohmic resistance between an earth electrode and the remote earth (a point of zero potential) when a current is dissipated into the soil. It is measured in **Ohms (Ω)**. **Importance of keeping it low (2 marks):** 1. **Safety:** A low earth resistance ensures that fault currents from the installation flow safely to ground, keeping the **touch and step potentials** within safe human-body thresholds (≤ 50 V under dry conditions per IS 3043). 2. **Equipment protection:** It prevents dangerous overvoltages on equipment casings, minimizing insulation breakdown and fire risk. 3. **Lightning protection:** It allows the safe dissipation of lightning surges (which can reach 30 kA) into the earth without damaging the structure. 4. **Protection relay coordination:** It ensures protective devices (MCBs, ELCBs, RCDs) trip promptly by providing a low-impedance fault-current path. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "earth resistance is the resistance of the earth wire." This is **WRONG**. It is the resistance between the electrode and the surrounding soil bulk, NOT the resistance of the conductor itself. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: In a Maxwell's inductance bridge, derive the expression for the unknown inductance $L_x$ in terms of bridge components.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** At balance of a Maxwell's bridge, the general balance equation is: $$Z_{AB} \cdot Z_{DC} = Z_{AD} \cdot Z_{BC}$$ Substituting the arm impedances and equating the **imaginary parts** (the coefficient of $j\omega$): $$ \boxed{L_x = R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot C_4} $$ This is **independent of the supply frequency**, which is the key advantage of the bridge. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that the result is obtained by **equating imaginary parts** of the balance equation. Writing the formula without showing "equating imaginary parts" costs 1 mark. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** In Part B Module-2 questions, students are given an **internal choice** between two full 14-mark questions. Both are presented below as **Option A** and **Option B**, each carrying sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### Option A — Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** **Q: Explain the Fall of Potential method for measurement of earth resistance with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of auxiliary electrodes.** **Model Solution:** **Definition and Objective (2 marks):** The Fall of Potential method is the standard three-point technique for measuring the resistance of an earth electrode. It uses two auxiliary electrodes — a **Current Probe (C)** and a **Potential Probe (P)** — to inject current and sense the resulting potential gradient in the soil. **Setup (2 marks):** - **Earth Electrode (E):** The electrode under test, isolated from the installation. - **Current Probe (C):** Driven into the soil at a distance of **at least 40 m** from E. - **Potential Probe (P):** Driven into the soil at a midpoint between E and C, then moved in steps. - An **AC source** (Earth Tester) is connected between E and C. - A **high-impedance voltmeter** is connected between E and P. - An **ammeter** is connected in series with the E–C loop. **Procedure (2 marks):** 1. Adjust the AC source to pass a known current $I$ between E and C. 2. Measure the potential difference $V$ between E and P. 3. Compute $R = V/I$. 4. Move P to several positions and plot $R$ vs. distance. The plateau region gives the **true earth resistance**. **Role of Auxiliary Electrodes (1 mark):** - **C probe** provides a remote current return path, ensuring current flows through the bulk soil. - **P probe** senses the potential at an intermediate point, isolating the measurement from localized soil irregularities near E. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q: An earth electrode in the form of a hemispherical electrode of radius 0.5 m is buried in soil of resistivity 200 Ω·m. Calculate (i) the resistance of the electrode, and (ii) the earth-fault current if the phase voltage is 230 V.** **Model Solution:** Given: - Radius $r = 0.5\ m$ - Soil resistivity $\rho = 200\ \Omega \cdot m$ - Phase voltage $V_{ph} = 230\ V$ **(i) Resistance of the hemispherical electrode:** The formula for a hemispherical electrode is: $$R_E = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} R_E &= \dfrac{200}{2\pi \times 0.5} \\ R_E &= \dfrac{200}{3.1416} \\ R_E &\approx 63.66\ \Omega \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Earth-fault current:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{earth} &= \dfrac{V_{ph}}{R_E} \\ I_{earth} &= \dfrac{230}{63.66} \\ I_{earth} &\approx 3.61\ A \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake:** Many students write the formula as $R_E = \dfrac{\rho}{4\pi r}$ (the full sphere formula) and forget that for a **hemispherical** electrode the factor is $2\pi r$, not $4\pi r$. This costs **2 marks** in a 7-mark question. --- ### Option B — Question B (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q: With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of Maxwell's inductance bridge. Derive the balance equations for $L_x$ and $R_x$.** **Model Solution:** **Circuit Description (2 marks):** The bridge has four arms: AB contains the unknown inductance $L_x$ in series with its resistance $R_x$; AD contains a variable resistor $R_3$; BC contains a fixed resistor $R_2$; DC contains a standard capacitor $C_4$ in parallel with a resistor $R_4$. The source is connected between A and C; the null detector is connected between B and D. **Balance Condition (1 mark):** At balance, no current flows through the detector, so: $$Z_{AB} \cdot Z_{DC} = Z_{AD} \cdot Z_{BC}$$ **Substitution (2 marks):** $$ \left( R_x + j\omega L_x \right) \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4} = R_3 \cdot R_2 $$ **Equating Real and Imaginary Parts (2 marks):** - Real part: $R_4 R_x = R_2 R_3 \implies R_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_4}$ - Imaginary part: $\omega R_4 L_x = \omega R_2 R_3 R_4 C_4 \implies L_x = R_2 R_3 C_4$ **Final Results:** $$ \boxed{R_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_4}, \qquad L_x = R_2 R_3 C_4} $$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q: In a Maxwell's inductance bridge, the values are: $R_2 = 1000\ \Omega$, $R_3 = 200\ \Omega$, $R_4 = 5000\ \Omega$, and $C_4 = 1\ \mu F$. The source frequency is 1000 Hz. Calculate (i) $L_x$, (ii) $R_x$, and (iii) $Q_x$.** **Model Solution:** Given: $R_2 = 1000\ \Omega$, $R_3 = 200\ \Omega$, $R_4 = 5000\ \Omega$, $C_4 = 1\ \mu F$, $f = 1000\ Hz$. **(i) Unknown Inductance $L_x$:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_x &= R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot C_4 \\ L_x &= 1000 \times 200 \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) \\ L_x &= 0.2\ \text{H} \\ L_x &= 200\ \text{mH} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Unknown Resistance $R_x$:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_x &= \dfrac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_4} \\ R_x &= \dfrac{1000 \times 200}{5000} \\ R_x &= 40\ \Omega \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Quality Factor $Q_x$:** Method 1 (direct from bridge components): $$ \begin{aligned} \omega &= 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.2\ \text{rad/s} \\ Q_x &= \omega R_4 C_4 \\ Q_x &= 6283.2 \times 5000 \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) \\ Q_x &= 31.42 \end{aligned} $$ Method 2 (verification): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_x &= \dfrac{\omega L_x}{R_x} = \dfrac{6283.2 \times 0.2}{40} = \dfrac{1256.6}{40} = 31.42 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Unit Errors:** A common mistake is using $C_4$ in farads without converting from $\mu F$. Another is forgetting to multiply by $2\pi$ when computing $\omega$ from $f$. Each error costs **1 mark**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Mark-Loss Areas in This Module (Compiled from KTU Valuation Reports):** > > 1. **Confusing earth resistance with conductor resistance.** Earth resistance is the resistance between the electrode and the *soil*, NOT the resistance of the metal rod. > 2. **Forgetting the parallel combination in Maxwell's bridge.** Many students write $Z_{DC} = 1/(j\omega C_4)$ instead of $\dfrac{R_4}{1+j\omega R_4 C_4}$. This single mistake invalidates the entire derivation. > 3. **Writing the Hay's bridge formula for Maxwell's bridge** (or vice versa). The two bridges look similar but yield different $L_x$ expressions. > 4. **Not specifying the unit of soil resistivity** as $\Omega \cdot m$ (not $\Omega/m$ or $\Omega/m^2$). > 5. **Omitting the 61.8% rule** in the Fall of Potential method — this is a frequently asked 2-mark sub-question in KTU 2024 papers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Earth Resistance & Maxwell's Bridge):** ### Earth Resistance - [ ] **Definition:** Resistance between electrode and remote earth (in Ω), NOT conductor resistance. - [ ] **Three methods:** Fall of Potential, Earth Tester, Compensation method. - [ ] **Auxiliary electrodes:** Current Probe (C) and Potential Probe (P). - [ ] **Distance rule:** C must be at least 40 m from E; P is moved in steps. - [ ] **61.8% rule:** The true $R_E$ is read at 61.8% of the E–C distance. - [ ] **Formulas to memorize:** - Hemispherical: $R_E = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r}$ - Plate: $R_E = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r}$ (radius of plate) - Rod: $R_E = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(4L/a) - 1\right]$ - [ ] **Standard soil resistivity:** 1–10,000 Ω·m; target $R_E < 1\ \Omega$ for substations. - [ ] **Why AC is used:** To avoid electrolytic polarization of soil. ### Maxwell's Inductance Bridge - [ ] **Arms:** AB = unknown $R_x + j\omega L_x$, AD = $R_3$ (variable), BC = $R_2$ (fixed), DC = $R_4 \parallel C_4$. - [ ] **Final balance equations:** - $L_x = R_2 R_3 C_4$ (frequency-independent) - $R_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_4}$ (frequency-independent) - [ ] **Q-factor:** $Q_x = \omega R_4 C_4 = \dfrac{\omega L_x}{R_x}$ - [ ] **Suitable Q range:** 1 < Q < 10 (medium-Q coils). For high-Q, use Hay's bridge. - [ ] **Reference standard:** Capacitor (superior to standard inductor). - [ ] **Convergence:** Easy and independent of supply frequency. - [ ] **Disadvantages:** Cannot measure high-Q coils accurately; $R_4$ range is limited. - [ ] **Detector:** Vibration galvanometer or tuned headphones. ### Cross-Reference Mnemonics - [ ] **"L R C"** → L = R₂R₃C₄, R = R₂R₃/R₄ — both involve R₂R₃ as a common factor. - [ ] **"Q ω R C"** → $Q = \omega R_4 C_4$ — same as the parallel arm's time constant. - [ ] **"Earth 3-point"** → E (electrode), P (potential), C (current) — E-P-C is the order of distances. ---

topics.title: Measurement of Capacitance - Schering's Bridge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT411 - ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENT AND MEASURING INSTRUMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Capacitance - Schering's Bridge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Schering's Bridge** is a four-arm alternating current (AC) bridge network specifically engineered for the precise measurement of **capacitance**, **equivalent series resistance (ESR)**, and the **dissipation factor (tan δ)** of capacitive specimens. It is the industry-standard bridge for evaluating the **dielectric properties of insulating materials** (cables, bushings, transformer oil, ceramic insulators) when subjected to high alternating voltages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Schering bridge is the **only** AC bridge recommended by international standards (IS, IEC, ASTM) for **high-voltage dielectric loss testing** of insulating materials used in power apparatus.

The bridge essentially compares an **unknown lossy capacitor** (modelled as a perfect capacitor $C_1$ in series with a small resistance $r_1$) against a **standard lossless capacitor** $C_2$, using two variable passive elements ($R_3$ and $R_4 \parallel C_4$) to achieve a null balance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tug-of-War of Charges"

Imagine a seesaw where two unknown weights are placed on either side. To balance the seesaw, you slide known weights and adjust the lever arm. The Schering bridge does exactly this — but with **electrical opposition (impedance)** instead of weight.

- The **standard capacitor** $C_2$ is like a *known weight*.
- The **unknown capacitor** $C_1$ (with leakage resistance) is like a *wrapped gift* — you cannot see it directly; you only know its outer appearance (a slight leak — that's $r_1$).
- The two adjustable arms ($R_3$ and $R_4 \parallel C_4$) are the *sliding weights* the operator uses until the detector (the "pointer of the seesaw") reads **zero**, indicating perfect electrical balance.

> [!NOTE]
> The **dissipation factor (D = tan δ)** quantifies how *leaky* a dielectric is. A perfect capacitor has $D = 0$. A bad insulator (e.g., moist cable) shows $D \approx 0.1$ to $1.0$ — meaning significant energy is wasted as heat.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Value in Practice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s | $\omega = 314$ rad/s at 50 Hz |
| Dissipation factor | $D = \tan \delta$ | dimensionless (pure number) | $0.0001$ to $0.1$ (good to poor dielectric) |
| Source frequency | $f$ | Hz | **50 Hz** (India) / **60 Hz** (USA) |
| Test voltage | $V_{test}$ | kV | $1$ kV to $100$ kV (depending on application) |

> [!TIP]
> In India, the **power frequency is 50 Hz**, giving $\omega = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159$ rad/s. Always use this value unless the question explicitly states otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bridge Circuit Topology

The Schering bridge is a four-arm network arranged in a **diamond configuration** with:
- **Source** (high-voltage AC) connected between nodes **A** and **C**.
- **Detector** (vibration galvanometer or headphones) connected between nodes **B** and **D**.

```
       A ●─────Z₁─────● B
        \             /
         \           /
       [Source]   [Detector]
         /           \
        /             \
       C ●─────Z₃─────● D
```

### 2.2 Arm-by-Arm Component Description

| Arm | Component | Symbol | Description | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AB (Z₁)** | Unknown lossy capacitor | $C_1$ with series $r_1$ | The device under test (DUT) | Stores charge + leaks small current |
| **BC (Z₂)** | Standard lossless capacitor | $C_2$ | A high-grade mica or air capacitor | Acts as reference |
| **CD (Z₃)** | Variable non-inductive resistor | $R_3$ | Decade resistance box | One balance control |
| **DA (Z₄)** | Variable resistor ‖ Variable capacitor | $R_4$ parallel with $C_4$ | Twin adjustment dials | Two balance controls |

### 2.3 Operational Logic — Why This Specific Topology?

1. **Standard $C_2$ chosen as lossless:** This simplifies the algebra and ensures all measured losses come purely from the unknown.
2. **$R_3$ made variable (non-inductive):** Provides a *purely real* adjustment, isolating the real-part balance (resistance matching).
3. **$R_4$ parallel with $C_4$ (both variable):** Provides *complex* adjustment. $C_4$ tunes the imaginary part (capacitance), while $R_4$ simultaneously fine-tunes the real part — achieving simultaneous real and imaginary balance.
4. **High-voltage operation:** The bridge is shielded so that the high-voltage test supply does not endanger the operator; only the low-voltage arms are touched during balancing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independent balance condition:** A major advantage of the Schering bridge is that $R_3$ and $C_4$ are *independent* balance controls — adjusting $C_4$ does not disturb the $R_3$ balance and vice versa. This makes balancing fast and unambiguous.

### 2.4 The Governing Balance Equation

At balance, the detector reads zero, meaning the **potential at node B equals the potential at node D**. The phasor condition is:

$$Z_1 \cdot Z_4 \;=\; Z_2 \cdot Z_3$$

This is the **complex impedance balance** — both **magnitude** and **phase** must match simultaneously. Substituting each arm's impedance:

$$\left(r_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{R_3}{j\omega C_2}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | What it Gives | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_1 = C_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$ | **Unknown capacitance** | Direct readout of DUT capacitance |
| 2 | $r_1 = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{C_4}{C_2}$ | **Equivalent series resistance** (ESR) | Loss component of the dielectric |
| 3 | $D = \tan\delta = \omega \, r_1 \, C_1$ | **Dissipation factor** | Quality indicator of the dielectric |
| 4 | $D = \tan\delta = \omega \, R_4 \, C_4$ | **Dissipation factor (alternate)** | Read directly from $R_4$, $C_4$ dials |
| 5 | $\delta = \tan^{-1}(\omega R_4 C_4)$ | **Loss angle** | The deviation from ideal $90^\circ$ phase |
| 6 | Balance condition: $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$ | Phasor balance | Universal AC bridge rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — Markdown Safety Rule:** In KTU answer sheets, write all absolute values or divisions using $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ notation in LaTeX to avoid table-rendering errors when copying to PDF.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Schering Bridge? |
|---|---|
| **Cable insulation testing** | Detects moisture ingress in underground cables |
| **Transformer oil testing** | Measures oil's dielectric loss before energization |
| **Capacitor manufacturing QA** | Verifies $C$ and $\tan\delta$ against rated values |
| **Bushings & insulators** | Ensures high-voltage equipment safety |
| **Research labs** | Measures permittivity of new dielectric materials |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of Balance Equations

We start with the complex impedance balance condition. We will derive the two key equations — for $C_1$ and $r_1$ — rigorously with no skipped steps.

---

**STEP 1 — Write the impedance of each arm.**

$$\text{Arm 1 (Z}_1\text{):} \quad Z_1 = r_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}$$

$$\text{Arm 2 (Z}_2\text{):} \quad Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}$$

$$\text{Arm 3 (Z}_3\text{):} \quad Z_3 = R_3$$

$$\text{Arm 4 (Z}_4\text{):} \quad Z_4 = R_4 \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4} = \dfrac{R_4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4}}{R_4 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4}} = \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4}$$

> **Logic:** The unknown capacitor is modelled as an *ideal capacitor $C_1$* in series with a *small leakage resistance $r_1$*. The fourth arm combines $R_4$ in parallel with $C_4$, yielding a single complex impedance using the standard parallel combination formula.

---

**STEP 2 — Apply the AC bridge balance condition.**

At detector null, the standard phasor balance for an AC bridge with source across diagonal **AC** and detector across diagonal **BD** is:

$$Z_1 \cdot Z_4 = Z_2 \cdot Z_3$$

Substituting the four impedances:

$$\left(r_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4}\right) = \left(\dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}\right) \cdot R_3$$

> **Logic:** This condition enforces that no current flows through the detector — meaning the two nodes B and D are at exactly the same potential (both magnitude AND phase).

---

**STEP 3 — Cross-multiply to simplify.**

Multiply both sides by $(1 + j\omega R_4 C_4)$ and by $j\omega C_2$:

$$\left(r_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}\right) \cdot R_4 \cdot j\omega C_2 = R_3 \cdot \left(1 + j\omega R_4 C_4\right)$$

Expanding the left-hand side:

$$j\omega C_2 R_4 \cdot r_1 + j\omega C_2 R_4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1} = R_3 + j\omega R_3 R_4 C_4$$

The middle term simplifies because $j\omega C_2 R_4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1} = \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{C_1}$ (the $j\omega$ cancels):

$$j\omega C_2 R_4 \, r_1 + \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{C_1} = R_3 + j\omega R_3 R_4 C_4$$

> **Logic:** Two cross-multiplications clear all fractions in one sweep, leaving a clean first-order equation in $j$.

---

**STEP 4 — Separate real and imaginary parts.**

Rearranging to group real and imaginary components on each side:

$$\underbrace{\dfrac{C_2 R_4}{C_1}}_{\text{real}} + \underbrace{j\omega C_2 R_4 \, r_1}_{\text{imaginary}} \;=\; \underbrace{R_3}_{\text{real}} + \underbrace{j\omega R_3 R_4 C_4}_{\text{imaginary}}$$

**Equating real parts:**

$$\dfrac{C_2 R_4}{C_1} = R_3$$

Solving for $C_1$:

$$C_1 = \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{R_3}$$

**Equating imaginary parts:**

$$\omega C_2 R_4 \, r_1 = \omega R_3 R_4 C_4$$

Cancel $\omega R_4$ from both sides:

$$C_2 \, r_1 = R_3 C_4$$

Solving for $r_1$:

$$r_1 = \dfrac{R_3 C_4}{C_2}$$

> **Logic:** Because complex numbers are equal *only* when their real parts AND imaginary parts are separately equal, we can split the single phasor equation into two real scalar equations — yielding both unknowns ($C_1$ and $r_1$) in closed form.

---

**STEP 5 — Derive the dissipation factor.**

The dissipation factor is defined as:

$$D = \tan\delta = \omega r_1 C_1$$

Substituting the expressions for $r_1$ and $C_1$:

$$D = \omega \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_3 C_4}{C_2}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{C_2 R_4}{R_3}\right)$$

The $R_3$ and $C_2$ cancel perfectly:

$$D = \tan\delta = \omega R_4 C_4$$

> **Logic:** This beautifully simple result shows that the dissipation factor depends ONLY on the components of arm 4 — making the Schering bridge a *direct-reading* instrument for $D$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A Schering bridge is used to measure an unknown capacitor. Given: $C_2 = 106$ pF (standard), $R_3 = 1000\ \Omega$, $R_4 = 500\ \Omega$, $C_4 = 0.01\ \mu\text{F}$, and supply frequency $f = 50$ Hz. Find $C_1$, $r_1$, and the dissipation factor $D$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute angular frequency:

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159\ \text{rad/s}$$

Step 2 — Apply the capacitance formula:

$$C_1 = \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{R_3} = \dfrac{106 \times 10^{-12} \times 500}{1000} = 5.3 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} = 53\ \text{pF}$$

Step 3 — Apply the resistance formula:

$$r_1 = \dfrac{R_3 C_4}{C_2} = \dfrac{1000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}{106 \times 10^{-12}} = \dfrac{10^{-5}}{1.06 \times 10^{-10}} = 94{,}340\ \Omega \approx 94.34\ \text{k}\Omega$$

Step 4 — Compute dissipation factor:

$$D = \omega R_4 C_4 = 314.159 \times 500 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} = 1.571 \times 10^{-3} = 0.001571$$

Step 5 — Final results (boxed for KTU exam):

$$\boxed{C_1 = 53\ \text{pF}, \quad r_1 = 94.34\ \text{k}\Omega, \quad D = 0.00157}$$

---

### 3.3 Permittivity Measurement (Extension)

When the unknown $C_1$ is a **parallel-plate dielectric specimen**, the capacitance relates to permittivity by:

$$C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d}$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the vacuum permittivity. By measuring $C_1$, the **relative permittivity** $\varepsilon_r$ can be calculated:

$$\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{C_1 \, d}{\varepsilon_0 \, A}$$

> **Logic:** This is why the Schering bridge is also used in *materials science labs* to characterize new dielectric materials, not just for quality control of finished insulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of Schering's Bridge

```mermaid
graph TB
    srcNode[HV AC Source 50Hz 1kV]:::source
    nodeA((Node A)):::node
    nodeB((Node B)):::node
    nodeC((Node C)):::node
    nodeD((Node D)):::node
    detNode[Vibration Galvanometer Detector]:::detector

    arm1[Z1: Unknown Capacitor C1 series r1]:::arm
    arm2[Z2: Standard Capacitor C2 lossless]:::arm
    arm3[Z3: Variable NonInductive R3]:::arm
    arm4[Z4: R4 parallel C4]:::arm

    out1[C1 = C2 R4 divide R3]:::output
    out2[r1 = R3 C4 divide C2]:::output
    out3[D = omega R4 C4]:::output

    srcNode --- nodeA
    srcNode --- nodeC
    nodeA --- arm1 --- nodeB
    nodeB --- arm2 --- nodeC
    nodeC --- arm3 --- nodeD
    nodeD --- arm4 --- nodeA
    nodeB --- detNode
    nodeD --- detNode

    arm1 -.-> out1
    arm4 -.-> out1
    arm3 -.-> out2
    arm4 -.-> out2
    arm4 -.-> out3

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef detector fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef arm fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Yellow node** = High-voltage AC source (excitation)
- **Blue circles** = The four bridge nodes (A, B, C, D)
- **Green blocks** = The four bridge arms with their impedances
- **Red block** = Detector (galvanometer/headphones) reading null
- **Purple blocks** = Computed output quantities ($C_1$, $r_1$, $D$)

### 4.2 Phasor Diagram at Balance (Descriptive)

At perfect balance, the bridge operates such that:

| Phasor | Symbol | Direction | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{AB}$ | Voltage across $Z_1$ | Lagging by small $\delta$ | $\vert I_1 \vert \cdot \vert Z_1 \vert$ |
| $V_{BC}$ | Voltage across $Z_2$ | $90^\circ$ lagging (purely capacitive) | $\vert I_1 \vert \cdot \vert Z_2 \vert$ |
| $V_{CD}$ | Voltage across $Z_3$ | In-phase with current | $\vert I_2 \vert \cdot R_3$ |
| $V_{DA}$ | Voltage across $Z_4$ | Lagging by angle $\phi_4 = \tan^{-1}(\omega R_4 C_4)$ | $\vert I_2 \vert \cdot \vert Z_4 \vert$ |

> **Key Phasor Insight:** $V_{AB}$ and $V_{BC}$ sum to $V_{AC}$ (source). $V_{AD}$ and $V_{DC}$ also sum to $V_{AC}$. For balance, the phasor tip of $V_{AB}$ must touch node B from above AND the tip of $V_{AD}$ (going opposite direction) must also reach the same point.

### 4.3 Physical Layout of Practical Schering Bridge

```
   +--------------------------------------+
   |  SCHERING BRIDGE — TEST CONSOLE      |
   +--------------------------------------+
   |                                      |
   |  [HV SOURCE] ──[FUSE]──[STEP-UP]     |
   |      │                                |
   |      ▼                                |
   |  [NODE A]═════════════════[NODE C]    |
   |      ║  Arm 1          Arm 2   ║      |
   |      ║  (DUT)          (Std)   ║      |
   |      ║  C1,r1          C2      ║      |
   |      ▼                         ▼      |
   |  [NODE B]═════════════════[NODE D]    |
   |      ║  Arm 4          Arm 3   ║      |
   |      ║  R4‖C4          R3      ║      |
   |      ▼                         ▼      |
   |  [DETECTOR] ←──[Amplifier]──[Filter]  |
   |                                      |
   |  Dials:  R3  ◐◑◐◑◐◑◐◑  R4  ◐◑◐◑    |
   |          C4  ◐◑◐◑◐◑◐◑              |
   +--------------------------------------+
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*List the components of a Schering bridge and state the quantity measured by it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Schering bridge consists of the following four arms:

1. **Arm AB:** Unknown lossy capacitor $C_1$ in series with its equivalent resistance $r_1$.
2. **Arm BC:** Standard lossless capacitor $C_2$ (usually mica or air).
3. **Arm CD:** Variable non-inductive resistor $R_3$.
4. **Arm DA:** Variable resistor $R_4$ in parallel with variable capacitor $C_4$.

The bridge is excited by a **high-voltage AC source** at industrial frequency (50 Hz in India) connected between nodes A and C. A **vibration galvanometer** serves as the null detector between nodes B and D.

**Quantity measured:** The bridge measures **(i)** the unknown capacitance $C_1$, **(ii)** its equivalent series resistance $r_1$, and **(iii)** the **dissipation factor (tan δ)** of the dielectric material under test.

> [Mark split: 2 marks for components + 1 mark for quantity measured]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Define dissipation factor. Why is it important in the testing of insulating materials?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **dissipation factor (D)** or **loss tangent (tan δ)** of a capacitor is defined as the ratio of its equivalent series resistance (ESR) to its capacitive reactance:

$$D = \tan\delta = \omega r_1 C_1 = \dfrac{1}{\omega C_1 r_1^{-1}}$$

Physically, $\delta$ is the small angle by which the current through a real (lossy) capacitor deviates from the ideal $90^\circ$ leading phase.

**Importance in insulation testing:**
- A high dissipation factor indicates **excessive dielectric loss**, meaning the insulator converts significant electrical energy into heat.
- This heat degrades the insulation, eventually leading to **breakdown** and equipment failure.
- Hence, D is a **direct quality indicator** for cables, transformer oil, bushings, and capacitor dielectrics. A low $D$ (typically $< 0.01$ for HV equipment) is mandatory for safe operation.

> [Mark split: 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for importance]

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of Schering's bridge used for the measurement of capacitance. Derive the balance equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Schering bridge is balanced with the following arm values: $C_2 = 500$ pF, $R_3 = 2000\ \Omega$, $R_4 = 1000\ \Omega$, $C_4 = 0.005\ \mu\text{F}$, and supply frequency 50 Hz. Calculate the unknown capacitance $C_1$, the equivalent series resistance $r_1$, and the dissipation factor $D$. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Working and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The Schering bridge is a four-arm AC network with:
- **Arm 1 (AB):** Unknown lossy capacitor $C_1$ in series with $r_1$.
- **Arm 2 (BC):** Standard lossless capacitor $C_2$.
- **Arm 3 (CD):** Variable non-inductive resistor $R_3$.
- **Arm 4 (DA):** Variable resistor $R_4$ in parallel with variable capacitor $C_4$.

A **high-voltage AC source** is connected between A and C, and a **vibration galvanometer** (null detector) is connected between B and D.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The two dials $R_3$ and $C_4$ are adjusted alternately until the detector reads zero (null). At null, the bridge is balanced. The variables $R_3$ and $C_4$ are *independent* controls — adjusting one does not disturb the other's balance. This is a key advantage of the Schering bridge topology.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Using the balance condition $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$:

$$\left(r_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4} = \dfrac{R_3}{j\omega C_2}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$j\omega C_2 R_4 \, r_1 + \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{C_1} = R_3 + j\omega R_3 R_4 C_4$$

Equating real parts:

$$\dfrac{C_2 R_4}{C_1} = R_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{C_1 = \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{R_3}}$$

Equating imaginary parts:

$$\omega C_2 R_4 \, r_1 = \omega R_3 R_4 C_4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{r_1 = \dfrac{R_3 C_4}{C_2}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Given data — $C_2 = 500$ pF $= 500 \times 10^{-12}$ F, $R_3 = 2000\ \Omega$, $R_4 = 1000\ \Omega$, $C_4 = 0.005\ \mu\text{F} = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ F, $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 2:** Angular frequency:

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $C_1$:

$$C_1 = \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{R_3} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{-12} \times 1000}{2000} = 250 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F} = \boxed{250\ \text{pF}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $r_1$:

$$r_1 = \dfrac{R_3 C_4}{C_2} = \dfrac{2000 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}}{500 \times 10^{-12}} = \dfrac{10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-10}} = 20{,}000\ \Omega = \boxed{20\ \text{k}\Omega}$$

**Step 5:** Compute dissipation factor:

$$D = \tan\delta = \omega R_4 C_4 = 314.159 \times 1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} = 1.571 \times 10^{-3} = \boxed{0.001571}$$

**Step 6:** Final summary:

$$\boxed{C_1 = 250\ \text{pF}, \quad r_1 = 20\ \text{k}\Omega, \quad D = 0.001571}$$

> [Mark split: Step 1 = 1, Step 2 = 1, Step 3 = 1.5, Step 4 = 1.5, Step 5 = 1, Final box = 1]

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with phasor diagram the operation of Schering's bridge for measurement of dissipation factor. How is it used for testing insulation of cables? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Schering bridge, the standard capacitor $C_2 = 0.002\ \mu\text{F}$, $R_3 = 100\ \Omega$, $R_4 = 200\ \Omega$, and $C_4 = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$. The supply frequency is 50 Hz. Determine: (i) the capacitance $C_1$, (ii) the equivalent series resistance $r_1$, (iii) the dissipation factor $D$, and (iv) the loss angle $\delta$ in degrees. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Phasor Diagram and Insulation Testing (7 marks):**

**Phasor Diagram (3.5 marks):**
At balance, the detector current is zero. The phasor diagram shows:
- $V_{AB}$ (across $Z_1$) is nearly $90^\circ$ leading the bridge current, with a small in-phase component due to $r_1$ — its angle is $90^\circ - \delta$.
- $V_{BC}$ (across $Z_2$, lossless) is exactly $90^\circ$ leading.
- $V_{CD}$ (across $R_3$) is in-phase with the current.
- $V_{DA}$ (across $R_4 \parallel C_4$) leads the current by angle $\phi_4 = \tan^{-1}(\omega R_4 C_4)$.

The small angle $\delta$ between $V_{AB}$ and the vertical axis represents the **loss angle** of the dielectric. The dissipation factor $D = \tan\delta = \omega R_4 C_4$ is read directly from the $R_4$ and $C_4$ dials.

**Insulation Testing of Cables (3.5 marks):**
- A sample of the cable insulation is sandwiched between two electrodes to form capacitor $C_1$.
- High voltage (typically 1–5 kV) at 50 Hz is applied.
- The bridge is balanced; the operator notes $C_1$, $r_1$, and $D$ from the dials.
- **Good insulation** has $D < 0.01$ (e.g., dry paper or XLPE cables).
- **Moist or contaminated insulation** shows $D > 0.05$ and is rejected.
- Schering's bridge can detect insulation defects *long before* they cause a catastrophic breakdown.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C_2 = 0.002\ \mu\text{F} = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ F, $R_3 = 100\ \Omega$, $R_4 = 200\ \Omega$, $C_4 = 0.1\ \mu\text{F} = 10^{-7}$ F, $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 1:** Compute angular frequency:

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $C_1$:

$$C_1 = \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{R_3} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-9} \times 200}{100} = 4 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F} = \boxed{0.004\ \mu\text{F}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $r_1$:

$$r_1 = \dfrac{R_3 C_4}{C_2} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 5{,}000\ \Omega = \boxed{5\ \text{k}\Omega}$$

**Step 4:** Compute dissipation factor:

$$D = \tan\delta = \omega R_4 C_4 = 314.159 \times 200 \times 10^{-7} = 6.28 \times 10^{-3} = \boxed{0.00628}$$

**Step 5:** Compute loss angle:

$$\delta = \tan^{-1}(D) = \tan^{-1}(0.00628) \approx 0.36^\circ = \boxed{0.36^\circ}$$

> [Mark split: Step 1 = 1, Step 2 = 1.5, Step 3 = 1.5, Step 4 = 1.5, Step 5 = 1.5]

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors (–2 marks):** Students frequently forget to convert pF to F, $\mu$F to F, etc. Always write units explicitly: $C_2 = 500 \times 10^{-12}$ F, NOT just "500 pF" in calculations.
>
> 2. **Confusing $R_4 \parallel C_4$ with $R_4$ in series with $C_4$ (–3 marks):** In the Schering bridge, $R_4$ is in **parallel** with $C_4$ — this is critical. Using the wrong topology gives wrong derivations.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $\omega$ in dissipation factor (–2 marks):** The formula $D = \omega R_4 C_4$ requires $\omega = 2\pi f$ explicitly. Some students write $D = R_4 C_4$ and lose marks.
>
> 4. **Not equating real and imaginary parts separately (–2 marks):** The KTU evaluator expects students to write *"Equating real parts"* and *"Equating imaginary parts"* as explicit steps. Simply writing the final formulas without showing the real/imaginary split loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing Schering with Wheatstone or Hay bridge (–4 marks):** Students often mix up bridge topologies. Always state the four arms *by name* in your answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Schering's bridge** is a four-arm AC bridge used to measure **capacitance, ESR, and dissipation factor (tan δ)** of dielectrics.
- **Arms:** (AB) Unknown $C_1$ with series $r_1$; (BC) Standard $C_2$; (CD) Variable $R_3$; (DA) $R_4$ in **parallel** with $C_4$.
- **Excitation:** High-voltage AC at 50 Hz; **Detector:** Vibration galvanometer.
- **Balance condition:** $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$.
- **Final equations:**
  - $\boxed{C_1 = \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{R_3}}$
  - $\boxed{r_1 = \dfrac{R_3 C_4}{C_2}}$
  - $\boxed{D = \tan\delta = \omega R_4 C_4 = \omega r_1 C_1}$
- **Independent balance controls:** $R_3$ and $C_4$ do not interact — fast balancing.
- **Primary application:** Testing insulation of cables, transformer oil, bushings, and ceramic insulators.
- **Phasor tip:** Voltage across $C_1$ deviates from $90^\circ$ by the small loss angle $\delta$ — this $\delta$ is the key quality metric.
- **Permittivity link:** $C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ — can be used to compute $\varepsilon_r$ for new materials.
- **At $\omega = 314$ rad/s (50 Hz):** Always use $\omega = 314.159$ rad/s unless otherwise specified.
- **Good dielectric:** $D < 0.01$; **Poor dielectric:** $D > 0.05$.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always write the balance derivation in **three** steps — (1) write impedances, (2) apply $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$, (3) equate real and imaginary parts. This is the marker-rewarded structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Frequency - Wien Bridge (Include Simple Problems). Q-Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT411 - ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENT AND MEASURING INSTRUMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Frequency - Wien Bridge (Include Simple Problems). Q-Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wien Bridge — Frequency Measurement

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** The **Wien Bridge** is a precision AC bridge network composed of two purely resistive ratio arms and two reactive arms (one series RC arm and one parallel RC arm). When the bridge is balanced, the unknown frequency of the AC source can be accurately determined from the values of the known resistors and capacitors.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Tuning Fork" Concept:**
Imagine a swing in a playground. It only swings at one natural rhythm (its resonant frequency). Push it at any other rhythm and it goes wild. The Wien Bridge works on the same principle: it is in "balance" (silent detector) **only** at one specific frequency. The detector reads zero (null) — like a silent swing — only when the input frequency exactly matches the frequency dictated by the R and C values in the arms. This exact matching frequency is what we measure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The Wien Bridge is a **null-type** instrument, meaning accuracy depends on the detector's sensitivity at null, not on absolute calibration. This makes it exceptionally accurate (error < 0.1%) for audio and low-RF frequency measurement.

---

### 1.2 Q-Meter — Quality Factor Measurement

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A **Q-Meter** is a laboratory instrument designed to measure the **Quality Factor (Q)** of an inductive coil (and indirectly, the losses in capacitors) at radio frequencies, based on the principle of **series resonance**.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Bell Test":**
Think of a high-quality brass bell: when struck, it rings for a long time with a clear tone (high Q). A cracked bell rings briefly with a dull sound (low Q). The Q-Meter is like a controlled "bell tester" — it energizes a coil at its resonant frequency and measures how sharply the coil responds. The sharper the response, the higher the Q.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Factor (Q) Definition:** $Q = 2\pi \times \dfrac{\text{Maximum Energy Stored}}{\text{Energy Dissipated per Cycle}} = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R_s} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C R_s}$ for a series RLC circuit at resonance.

---

### 1.3 Standard Symbols and Constants Used

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value/Unit |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| $f$ | Frequency | **Hz** |
| $\omega$ | Angular frequency, $\omega = 2\pi f$ | **rad/s** |
| $Q$ | Quality factor (dimensionless) | **unitless** |
| $C$ | Capacitance | **Farad (F)** |
| $R_s$ | Effective series resistance | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** |
| $\pi$ | Mathematical constant | **3.14159...** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wien Bridge — Circuit Topology and Balance Theory

The Wien Bridge has **four arms** between nodes A, B, C, and D:

- **Arm 1 (A–B):** Pure resistor $R_1$
- **Arm 2 (B–C):** Pure resistor $R_2$
- **Arm 3 (C–D):** $R_3$ in **series** with $C_1$
- **Arm 4 (A–D):** $R_4$ in **parallel** with $C_2$

A null detector is connected between nodes **B and D**, and an AC source drives nodes A and C.

**Step-by-Step Balance Logic:**

1. **Detector reads zero** → no current flows through the detector branch.
2. This means nodes B and D are at the **same potential**.
3. Applying the general AC bridge balance equation: $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$
4. Substituting impedances: $R_1 \cdot Z_4 = R_2 \cdot Z_3$
5. The series arm impedance: $Z_3 = R_3 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_1}$
6. The parallel arm impedance: $Z_4 = \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_2}$
7. Equating real and imaginary parts yields **two independent balance equations**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Wien Bridge

| Parameter | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------------------|
| Angular frequency | $\omega^2 = \dfrac{1}{R_3 R_4 C_1 C_2}$ | Imaginary part condition |
| Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_3 R_4 C_1 C_2}}$ | **Primary measuring equation** |
| Resistance ratio | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4} + \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$ | Real part condition |
| Simplified (when $R_3 = R_4 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$) | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Most common practical form |
| Simplified resistance ratio | $R_1 = 2R_2$ | When both pairs are equal |

---

### 2.3 Q-Meter — Circuit Topology and Theory

**Block-level construction of a Q-Meter:**

1. **RF Oscillator** (variable frequency, 50 kHz to 50 MHz) → provides test signal.
2. **Wide-band Amplifier** → boosts the oscillator output to a stable, low-impedance level.
3. **Injecting Resistor $R_s$** (very small, typically 0.01–0.5 $\Omega$) → converts input voltage to current.
4. **Test Coil** (unknown $L$ and $r$) → the device under test.
5. **Variable Standard Capacitor $C$** → tuned to achieve resonance.
6. **Two Voltmeters:** $V_1$ across $R_s$ and $V_2$ across $C$.

**Working Principle — The "Voltage Multiplication" Effect:**

- At resonance: $X_L = X_C$ → the circuit is purely resistive.
- The current $I = V_1 / R_s$ is in phase with the source.
- Voltage across the capacitor: $V_2 = I \cdot X_C = \dfrac{I}{\omega C}$
- Since $X_C = X_L = \omega L$ at resonance: $V_2 = I \cdot \omega L = \dfrac{V_1 \cdot \omega L}{R_s}$
- Therefore: $\boxed{Q = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{\omega L}{R_s}}$

> [!TIP]
