# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q:** Why is a dynamometer wattmeter preferred over an induction-type wattmeter for the measurement of power in a circuit with a non-sinusoidal waveform?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A dynamometer wattmeter responds to the **true average of the instantaneous product $v(t) \cdot i(t)$**, i.e., it is a *true RMS wattmeter* irrespective of waveform.
- **(1 mark)** An induction-type wattmeter, in contrast, has a deflecting torque proportional to $V^2$ and $I^2 \sin\phi$ (or $\cos\phi$) but its B-H loop is non-linear and is calibrated for **50 Hz sinusoidal supplies only**.
- **(1 mark)** Hence, for distorted / non-sinusoidal / transient waveforms (e.g., in power electronics, inverter-fed drives), the dynamometer is the *only* accurate choice — it has a flat frequency response limited only by the PC inductance.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q:** Define *multiplying factor* of a dynamometer wattmeter. State its expression.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** *Multiplying factor* $K_m$ is the **ratio of true power consumed by the load to the power indicated by the wattmeter**: $K_m = P_{\text{true}} / P_{\text{ind}}$.
- **(1 mark)** Expression: $K_m = \dfrac{\cos\phi}{\cos(\phi - \beta)}$ where $\phi$ is the load PF angle and $\beta$ is the pressure-coil impedance angle.
- **(1 mark)** Simplified form: $K_m \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + \tan\phi \tan\beta}$ for small $\beta$.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice:** Answer **any ONE** full question from the pair. Each full question has two sub-parts, each carrying 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a dynamometer-type wattmeter. Derive the expression for its deflecting torque.

**(b) [7 marks)** A dynamometer wattmeter, with pressure coil resistance $R_p = 3000\ \Omega$ and inductance $L_p = 8\ \text{mH}$, is used on a 50 Hz, 200 V circuit. The load current is 5 A at a power factor of 0.5 lagging. Calculate: (i) the pressure-coil impedance angle, (ii) the multiplying factor, and (iii) the true power if the meter reads 480 W.

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Construction, Working & Torque Derivation

**Construction (3 marks)**
- **Fixed current coils (CC):** Two identical coils of thick wire wound in series with the load; air-cored to avoid hysteresis. Placed symmetrically above and below the moving coil to produce a near-uniform field in the air gap.
- **Moving potential coil (PC):** A small coil of fine wire wound on a non-metallic (Bakelite) former, pivoted on jewel bearings, and connected across the load through a high-value series multiplier resistor $R_s$.
- **Control system:** Two phosphor-bronze spiral springs that provide controlling torque and act as PC current leads.
- **Damping system:** Air-friction damping vane (eddy-current damping is unsuitable because the main field is alternating).
- **Pointer & scale:** Knife-edge pointer over a uniformly graduated scale; the entire movement is enclosed in a soft-iron laminated case to shield against external stray fields.

**Working principle (2 marks)**
- Load current $I$ through the CC creates a magnetic field $B \propto I$ in the air gap.
- Voltage $V$ across the load drives a current $I_p = V/Z_p$ through the PC, where $Z_p = \sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2}$.
- The electromagnetic interaction between the CC field and the PC current produces a deflecting torque:
$$
T_d = I \cdot I_p \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}
$$
- This torque is balanced by the spring control torque $T_c = C_s \theta$, and the resulting deflection $\theta$ is proportional to the average real power $VI\cos\phi$ (with a small correction factor $\cos\beta$).

**Derivation (2 marks)** — Steps as in Section 3.1 above:
- From $T_d = I_1 I_2 \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$, with $I_1 = I$, $I_2 = I_p = V/Z_p$, time-averaging the AC product gives:
$$
T_d = \dfrac{1}{Z_p}\, V I \cos(\phi - \beta) \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}
$$
- With uniform-field construction, $dM/d\theta = K_1 = $ constant, so $\theta \propto VI\cos\phi$ — confirming the wattmeter behaviour.

##### Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:** $R_p = 3000\ \Omega$, $L_p = 8 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{H}$, $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$, $V = 200\ \text{V}$, $I = 5\ \text{A}$, $\cos\phi = 0.5$ lag, $P_{\text{ind}} = 480\ \text{W}$.

**(i) Impedance angle $\beta$:**
$$
\omega L_p = 2\pi \times 50 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 2.513\ \Omega
$$
$$
\tan\beta = \dfrac{\omega L_p}{R_p} = \dfrac{2.513}{3000} = 8.378 \times 10^{-4}
$$
$$
\boxed{\beta = 0.0480^\circ = 8.378 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad}}
$$

**(ii) Multiplying factor $K_m$:**

Since $\cos\phi = 0.5$, $\phi = 60^\circ$ and $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} = 1.732$.

$$
K_m = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tan\phi \tan\beta} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 1.732 \times 8.378 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{1}{1.001451}
$$
$$
\boxed{K_m = 0.99855}
$$

**(iii) True power:**
$$
P_{\text{true}} = P_{\text{ind}} \times K_m = 480 \times 0.99855
$$
$$
\boxed{P_{\text{true}} = 479.3\ \text{W}}
$$

> The instrument **over-reads** by about $0.7\ \text{W}$ because the PC inductance causes it to register $VI\cos(\phi - \beta) > VI\cos\phi$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Alternative Module Choice]

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the various errors that occur in a dynamometer-type wattmeter. What remedies are adopted to minimize these errors?

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the principle of *phantom loading* used for testing of energy meters. State two of its advantages.

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Errors and Remedies

| # | Error | Cause | Remedy |
|---|-------|-------|--------|
| 1 | **PC inductance error** | $L_p$ causes $I_p$ to lag $V$ by $\beta$, instrument reads $VI\cos(\phi - \beta)$ instead of $VI\cos\phi$ | Add large series multiplier $R_s$ so that $\omega L_p \ll R_p$ (typically $R_s / \omega L_p > 1000$). |
| 2 | **PC capacitance error** | Distributed capacitance $C_p$ of multiplier winding introduces a leading component that cancels some of $L_p$ error — but not exactly | Use a **capacitance compensation winding (CCW)** — a few extra turns near the resistor end to balance. |
| 3 | **Mutual inductance error** | Inductive coupling between CC and PC induces spurious EMF in the PC | Wind the PC as a **"non-inductive" doubled-back winding** so that the net loop area is zero. |
| 4 | **Eddy-current error** | Time-varying flux induces eddy currents in any metallic former; loss appears as additional +ve torque | Use a **non-metallic former** (Bakelite, phenolic). |
| 5 | **Stray magnetic field error** | Earth's field, adjacent bus-bars, panel magnetisation superimpose an extra field | Enclose movement in a **soft-iron case** or use an **astatic** (double) winding. |
| 6 | **Temperature error** | $R_p$ changes with temperature, altering $K_m$ | Use **Manganin** ($R$ changes by only $0.0001 / ^\circ\text{C}$) for the multiplier. |
| 7 | **Frequency error** | $\beta$ depends on $\omega$, so $K_m$ changes with frequency | Design for the rated frequency with $\tan\beta \le 10^{-3}$ even at the upper limit. |
| 8 | **Spring aging** | Spring constant $C_s$ drifts with time | Periodically calibrate against a sub-standard wattmeter. |

**Marks distribution:**
- (1 mark) — Naming the 4–5 major errors.
- (2 marks) — Explaining any 2 errors with formulas.
- (2 marks) — Stating remedies for each.
- (2 marks) — Drawing/citing the multiplying-factor compensation.

##### Part (b) — Phantom Loading

**Principle (3 marks):** Phantom loading (also called *fictitious loading*) is a method of testing energy meters and wattmeters at their *full-scale rating* without actually dissipating the full rated power in the load. The current coil is supplied from a **low-voltage high-current source**, and the potential coil is supplied from an **isolated high-voltage source**, with a **phase-shifting transformer** used to obtain the desired phase angle between them. Because the two supplies are isolated, the *voltage across the PC* and the *current through the CC* are independent — but the wattmeter still responds to the *product* $V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ as if the load were a real one.

**Circuit description (2 marks):**
- LV side: variac → step-down transformer → series-connected current coil.
- HV side: variac → step-up transformer (isolated) → potential coil through multiplier.
- Phase-shifter: a three-phase variac arrangement or a phase-shifting transformer to set $\phi$.
- Total supply power = $V_{\text{reduce}} \cdot I_{\text{normal}} \cdot \sin\phi'$ (very small).

**Numerical example (2 marks):** For a 240 V, 10 A, 0.8 PF load: full-load power = $1920\ \text{W}$. With phantom loading using 5 V CC supply:
- CC power = $5 \times 10 \times \sin\phi' = 50 \sin\phi'\ \text{W}$
- For PF 0.8, $\phi = 36.87^\circ$ and we can arrange $\phi' = 90^\circ - 36.87^\circ = 53.13^\circ$.
- Total supply power = $50 \times \sin(53.13^\circ) = 50 \times 0.8 = 40\ \text{W}$.
- Power saved = $1920 - 40 = 1880\ \text{W}$ (98 % saving).

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Enormous saving in power consumption → testing of large energy meters (up to 100 A / 100 kW) becomes economical.
2. Compact test bench — only a few-kW supply suffices to test a 100 kW meter.
3. Heating of the meter is negligible, so the test can be continuous without thermostat.
4. Calibration of precision wattmeters and energy meters is more stable and repeatable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Traps**
> 1. **Sign of the angle:** Students frequently write $T_d \propto \cos(\phi + \beta)$ instead of $\cos(\phi - \beta)$ — both KTU and the standard text (Sawhney, Golding) use $\cos(\phi - \beta)$ because the PC current *lags* the voltage and the **field-current-product integral** gives a minus sign in the conventional sense. **Memorise the formula correctly.**
> 2. **Confusion between $Z_p$ and $R_p$:** The PC current is $V/Z_p$, **not** $V/R_p$. If you forget $Z_p$, the entire derivation collapses. — *Lose 1 mark*.
> 3. **Eddy-current error sign:** It is *positive* (over-reading), not negative. Do not state it makes the meter read low. — *Lose 0.5 mark*.
> 4. **Mutual inductance error:** Many students wrongly claim it can be reduced by *increasing $R_s$*. The correct remedy is the *non-inductive winding of the moving coil*, not the multiplier. — *Lose 1 mark*.
> 5. **Phantom loading — phase angle:** Students write $90^\circ$ between LV and HV supplies by default. The correct phase angle is $(90^\circ - \phi)$ so that the *internal wattmeter sees a PF equal to $\cos\phi$*. — *Lose 1 mark*.
> 6. **Multiplying factor at unity PF:** At $\phi = 0$, $K_m = 1/\cos\beta$. So the wattmeter has a *small but non-zero* systematic error even at unity PF. — *Lose 0.5 mark* if not mentioned.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

### Must-Know Definitions
- ✅ **Dynamometer wattmeter** — Electrodynamic instrument for true RMS power measurement.
- ✅ **Multiplying factor** $K_m = \dfrac{\cos\phi}{\cos(\phi - \beta)}$.
- ✅ **Pressure coil impedance angle** $\beta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega L_p}{R_p}\right)$.
- ✅ **Phantom loading** — Testing at full-scale rating without dissipating full power.

### Must-Know Constructional Facts
- ✅ **Fixed CC** = thick wire, air-cored, in series with load.
- ✅ **Moving PC** = fine wire, on Bakelite former, across load via multiplier.
- ✅ **Damping** = air friction (not eddy current).
- ✅ **Control** = phosphor-bronze springs (also serve as PC leads).
- ✅ **Shielding** = iron case or astatic winding.

### Must-Know Errors (8 — remember all 8!)
- ✅ PC inductance, PC capacitance, mutual inductance, eddy current, stray field, temperature, frequency, spring aging.

### Must-Know Numericals
- ✅ Compute $K_m$ from $L_p$, $R_p$, $f$, and $\cos\phi$.
- ✅ Compute true power from indicated power using $K_m$.
- ✅ Compute percentage error $= (\tan\phi \cdot \tan\beta) \times 100$.
- ✅ Phantom-loading power savings calculation.

### Must-Know Advantages of Dynamometer Wattmeter
- ✅ Works on both AC and DC.
- ✅ Highest accuracy (0.1 – 0.2 class) — used as a sub-standard.
- ✅ True RMS / true power — works on non-sinusoidal waveforms.
- ✅ Uniformly graduated scale.
- ✅ Free from waveform and frequency errors (within design range).

### Must-Know Disadvantages
- ❌ Low torque-to-weight ratio → heavier mechanism, fragile.
- ❌ Sensitive to overload, vibration, and external fields.
- ❌ Power consumption of PC is significant (≈ 3–5 W for 250 V range).
- ❌ More expensive than MI or induction types.
- ❌ Scale length is shorter than MI-type instruments.

### KTU 14-Mark Blueprint
- ✅ Construction + Sketch = 2 marks
- ✅ Deflecting torque derivation = 2 marks
- ✅ Numerical on $K_m$ and true power = 5 marks
- ✅ Errors + remedies (table) = 5 marks

> **One-line memory hook for the exam:**
> *"**D**eflecting torque = $I_1 I_2 \, dM/d\theta$, **D**ivided by $Z_p$, **D**elivers $VI\cos(\phi - \beta)$."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Phase Power Measurement Using Two Wattmeter Method (Include Phasor Diagrams and Expressions)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT411 - ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENT AND MEASURING INSTRUMENTS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Three Phase Power Measurement Using Two Wattmeter Method (Include Phasor Diagrams and Expressions) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Two-Wattmeter Method** is the standard, universally accepted technique for measuring the total active (real) power consumed by a **three-phase, three-wire system** (either balanced or unbalanced), without requiring a neutral connection. The method is a direct practical application of **Blondel's Theorem**, which states that the total power in an *n*-wire system can be measured using *(n − 1)* wattmeters. For a 3-wire system, this mandates the use of exactly **two wattmeters**, each placed in one of the two lines with their current coils, while their pressure (voltage) coils are connected between that line and the third (reference) line. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** This method is restricted to **three-phase, three-wire systems only**. For systems with a neutral wire (four-wire systems), the two-wattmeter method gives erroneous readings and the **three-wattmeter method** must be used. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine three friends (Phases A, B, C) sharing a pizza bill at a restaurant. The total bill is unknown. They agree that two of them will each calculate a portion of the bill based on what they think they consumed plus what they "observe" their third friend consuming from across the table. The sum of the two calculated portions equals the total bill. In electrical terms: - **The two wattmeters** are the two "friends" calculating the power. - **Each wattmeter measures** its own line current times the **voltage between its line and the third reference line**. - **The algebraic sum** of the two wattmeter readings gives the **total three-phase power**. > [!NOTE] > **Why the Algebraic Sum?** The two wattmeters do not directly measure power in a single physical phase. Each measures a *fictitious* (mathematical) power that is the result of a particular phasor product. The total real power emerges only when these two phasor products are summed, which is a beautiful consequence of Kirchhoff's Current Law applied across the three lines. ## 1.3 Physical Standards and Constants - **Number of Wattmeters Required:** $\mathbf{n - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2}$ (Blondel's Theorem) - **Phasor Reference Line:** The **third line** (whichever is not carrying the current coil current) acts as the common reference for the voltage coil. - **Phase Sequence Effect:** The readings of $W_1$ and $W_2$ **interchange values** if the phase sequence is reversed. This is a key experimental observation. - **Power Factor Range:** The method works for **all power factors (lagging, unity, and leading)**, although the readings can become zero or even negative for low power factors. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Circuit Connection Logic (Operational Steps) The two wattmeters are connected in a three-phase, three-wire system as follows: 1. The **current coil** of Wattmeter 1 ($W_1$) is inserted in **Line A**, and that of Wattmeter 2 ($W_2$) is inserted in **Line B**. 2. The **pressure coil (voltage coil)** of $W_1$ is connected between **Line A and Line C** (the third line, not carrying $W_1$'s current). 3. The **pressure coil of $W_2$** is connected between **Line B and Line C**. 4. The other ends of both pressure coils meet at the common point, which is **Line C**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Point:** The "common reference" for the voltage coils is *not* the neutral; it is the **third line (Line C)**. This is what makes the two-wattmeter method possible in a three-wire system. ## 2.2 Phasor Analysis (The Core "Why") Let us take a balanced star-connected load with phase sequence **A-B-C**. Let the phase voltages be: - $V_{AN} = V_p \angle 0°$ - $V_{BN} = V_p \angle -120°$ - $V_{CN} = V_p \angle +120°$ The corresponding line voltages (and the voltages across the pressure coils) are: - $V_{AC} = V_L \angle -30°$ (voltage across $W_1$'s pressure coil) - $V_{BC} = V_L \angle -90°$ (voltage across $W_2$'s pressure coil) The line currents (with load impedance angle $\phi$) are: - $I_A = I_L \angle -\phi$ (with respect to $V_{AN}$) - $I_B = I_L \angle (-120° - \phi)$ - $I_C = I_L \angle (120° - \phi)$ ## 2.3 Expressions for Wattmeter Readings The instantaneous power measured by each wattmeter is the average of the product of the current through its current coil and the voltage across its pressure coil. Working out the phasor product (dot product of the two phasors) gives the **active power reading**. - **Angle between $I_A$ and $V_{AC}$:** $(-\phi) - (-30°) = (30° - \phi)$ - **Angle between $I_B$ and $V_{BC}$:** $(-120° - \phi) - (-90°) = -(30° + \phi)$ Therefore: $$W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$$ $$W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention:** $W_2$ can become **negative** when $\phi > 60°$ (i.e., power factor $\cos\phi < 0.5$). A negative reading is indicated by the wattmeter pointer trying to deflect below zero, requiring the reversal of the pressure coil connection and a negative sign in the algebraic sum. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Quantity** | **Expression** | **Special Cases / Conditions** | |---|---|---| | Wattmeter 1 Reading | $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$ | Valid for all $\phi$ | | Wattmeter 2 Reading | $W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$ | Negative if $\phi > 60°$ | | Total Active Power | $P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | For balanced load | | Total Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | Var (Volt-Ampere Reactive) | | Power Factor Angle | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}$ | Universal PF relation | | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \cos\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}\right)\right]$ | Always positive for PF | | Reading Ratio | $\dfrac{W_1}{W_2} = \dfrac{\cos(30° - \phi)}{\cos(30° + \phi)}$ | Used to find $\phi$ graphically | | At Unity PF ($\phi = 0°$) | $W_1 = W_2 = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L I_L$ | Both meters read equal | | At PF = 0.5 ($\phi = 60°$) | $W_1 = V_L I_L,\; W_2 = 0$ | $W_2$ reads zero | | At PF < 0.5 ($\phi > 60°$) | $W_2 < 0$ | $W_2$ reading is **negative** | > [!NOTE] > **LaTeX Note for Tables:** All absolute-value-style symbols in the formulas above have been replaced with appropriate LaTeX notation (e.g., `\tan^{-1}`, `\dfrac`, `\phi`) to ensure the markdown table is not broken by stray pipe characters. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Energy Billing:** All commercial three-phase energy meters (kWh meters) in industries, factories, and HT consumers are essentially two-wattmeter-method instruments in disguise. - **Power Factor Correction Studies:** The reactive power formula $Q = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)$ allows engineers to compute the **reactive volt-amperes** directly from the two meter readings, which is vital for sizing capacitor banks. - **Transformer & Motor Testing:** During load testing of three-phase induction motors and transformers, the two-wattmeter method is the standard laboratory technique. - **Transmission Line Monitoring:** Used in substations to measure power flow on three-phase transmission lines without requiring a neutral. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Wattmeter Readings ($W_1$ and $W_2$) We adopt a balanced star-connected load with phase sequence **A-B-C** and load power factor angle $\phi$ (lagging). **Step 1: Define the phase voltages (taken as the reference frame).** $$V_{AN} = V_p \angle 0°$$ $$V_{BN} = V_p \angle -120°$$ $$V_{CN} = V_p \angle +120°$$ **Step 2: Derive the line voltages (which are the voltages across the pressure coils).** The voltage between lines A and C is: $$V_{AC} = V_{AN} - V_{CN}$$ Converting to phasor form using $V_p \angle 0° - V_p \angle 120°$: $$V_{AC} = V_p(1 - \cos 120° - j\sin 120°)$$ $$V_{AC} = V_p\!\left(1 - \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) - j\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = V_p\!\left(\tfrac{3}{2} - j\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$ Converting to polar form (magnitude = $\sqrt{(3/2)^2 + (\sqrt{3}/2)^2} = \sqrt{3}\,V_p = V_L$; angle = $\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-\sqrt{3}/2}{3/2}\right) = -30°$): $$V_{AC} = \sqrt{3}\,V_p \angle -30° = V_L \angle -30°$$ Similarly, the voltage between lines B and C is: $$V_{BC} = V_{BN} - V_{CN} = V_p \angle -120° - V_p \angle +120°$$ By similar analysis, this simplifies to: $$V_{BC} = V_L \angle -90°$$ **Step 3: Define the line currents (with load impedance angle $\phi$ lagging).** $$I_A = I_L \angle -\phi$$ $$I_B = I_L \angle (-120° - \phi)$$ **Step 4: Compute the reading of Wattmeter 1 ($W_1$).** $W_1$ measures the active power using the phasor dot product of $I_A$ and $V_{AC}$: $$W_1 = \text{Re}\{V_{AC} \cdot I_A^*\} = |V_{AC}| \cdot |I_A| \cos(\angle V_{AC} - \angle I_A)$$ The angle between $V_{AC}$ and $I_A$: $$\theta_1 = \angle V_{AC} - \angle I_A = (-30°) - (-\phi) = (30° - \phi)$$ Therefore: $$W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$$ **Step 5: Compute the reading of Wattmeter 2 ($W_2$).** $W_2$ uses the phasor dot product of $I_B$ and $V_{BC}$: $$W_2 = |V_{BC}| \cdot |I_B| \cos(\angle V_{BC} - \angle I_B)$$ The angle between $V_{BC}$ and $I_B$: $$\theta_2 = \angle V_{BC} - \angle I_B = (-90°) - (-120° - \phi) = -(30° + \phi)$$ Since $\cos(-\theta) = \cos(\theta)$: $$W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Total Active Power ($P$) The total active power is the **algebraic sum** of the two wattmeter readings: $$P = W_1 + W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi) + V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$$ Factor out $V_L I_L$ and use the trigonometric identity $\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B) = 2\cos A \cos B$: $$P = V_L I_L \left[\cos(30° - \phi) + \cos(30° + \phi)\right]$$ $$P = V_L I_L \left[2 \cos 30° \cos \phi\right]$$ Substitute $\cos 30° = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$: $$P = V_L I_L \left[2 \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos\phi\right] = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the famous result that proves the two-wattmeter method is valid for a balanced load:** The total real power equals $\sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$, which matches the standard formula for three-phase power. This also proves **Blondel's Theorem** experimentally. ## 3.3 Derivation of Total Reactive Power ($Q$) The reactive power is the **algebraic difference** of the two wattmeter readings, scaled by $\sqrt{3}$: $$Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L\left[\cos(30° - \phi) - \cos(30° + \phi)\right]$$ Using the identity $\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B) = 2\sin A \sin B$: $$Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \left[2 \sin 30° \sin \phi\right]$$ Substitute $\sin 30° = \dfrac{1}{2}$: $$Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \left[2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \sin\phi\right] = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \sin\phi$$ This is exactly the standard three-phase reactive power expression. ## 3.4 Derivation of Power Factor Formula Divide the expression for $Q$ by the expression for $P$: $$\dfrac{Q}{P} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi} = \tan\phi$$ Therefore: $$\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}$$ The power factor is: $$\cos\phi = \cos\!\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}\right)\right]$$ ## 3.5 Proof of Validity for Unbalanced Loads The above derivations assumed a balanced load, but the two-wattmeter method works for **unbalanced loads** as well. The proof uses the instantaneous power approach: The instantaneous power in a three-wire system is: $$p(t) = v_{AN} i_A + v_{BN} i_B + v_{CN} i_C$$ Since $i_A + i_B + i_C = 0$ (Kirchhoff's Current Law at the load star point), we have $i_C = -(i_A + i_B)$. Substituting: $$p(t) = v_{AN} i_A + v_{BN} i_B - v_{CN}(i_A + i_B) = (v_{AN} - v_{CN}) i_A + (v_{BN} - v_{CN}) i_B$$ $$p(t) = v_{AC} i_A + v_{BC} i_B$$ Taking the time average (i.e., the active power): $$P = \overline{v_{AC} i_A} + \overline{v_{BC} i_B} = W_1 + W_2$$ This derivation **makes no assumption of balance** and proves that the sum $W_1 + W_2$ equals the total three-phase power for any three-wire system, balanced or unbalanced. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Connection Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional connection architecture** of the two wattmeter method, mapping the line currents, voltage coil references, and the load. ```mermaid graph TB Source["Three-Phase Source
Lines A, B, C"]:::source LineA["Line A
V_AN = V_p angle 0 deg"]:::line LineB["Line B
V_BN = V_p angle -120 deg"]:::line LineC["Line C
V_CN = V_p angle 120 deg"]:::line WC1A["Wattmeter 1
Current Coil"]:::wc WC2B["Wattmeter 2
Current Coil"]:::wc VC1AC["Wattmeter 1
Pressure Coil
V_AC = V_L angle -30 deg"]:::vc VC2BC["Wattmeter 2
Pressure Coil
V_BC = V_L angle -90 deg"]:::vc Load["Balanced / Unbalanced
3-Phase Load
Impedance Z angle phi"]:::load Source --> LineA Source --> LineB Source --> LineC LineA --> WC1A WC1A --> LineA LineB --> WC2B WC2B --> LineB LineC --> Load LineA --> Load LineB --> Load VC1AC --- LineA VC1AC --- LineC VC2BC --- LineB VC2BC --- LineC classDef source fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef line fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef wc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef vc fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#9370DB,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Phasor Diagram (Mermaid Block Representation) The actual phasor diagram is a vector representation in 2D space. Since Mermaid cannot render precise trigonometric phasor drawings, the following block diagram provides the **functional relationship map** between the phasors and the derived angles. ```mermaid graph LR VAN["V_AN
Reference 0 deg"]:::phasor VBN["V_BN
-120 deg"]:::phasor VCN["V_CN
+120 deg"]:::phasor VAC["V_AC
-30 deg
Pressure Coil of W1"]:::phasorphase VBC["V_BC
-90 deg
Pressure Coil of W2"]:::phasorphase IA["I_A
Angle -phi
Lagging V_AN by phi"]:::current IB["I_B
Angle -120 minus phi
Lagging V_BN by phi"]:::current ANGLE1["Angle between I_A and V_AC
equals 30 deg minus phi
Determines W1"]:::angle ANGLE2["Angle between I_B and V_BC
equals 30 deg plus phi
Determines W2"]:::angle VAN --- VCN VAN --- VBN VCN --- VAC VBN --- VBC IA --- VAC IB --- VBC IA --- ANGLE1 VAC --- ANGLE1 IB --- ANGLE2 VBC --- ANGLE2 classDef phasor fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef phasorphase fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#CD5C5C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef current fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef angle fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Phasor Diagram Description (Verbal Walkthrough) For a balanced star-connected load with **lagging** power factor angle $\phi$ and phase sequence **A-B-C**, the phasor diagram has the following key features: 1. **Three Phase Voltage Phasors:** $V_{AN}$, $V_{BN}$, $V_{CN}$ are drawn from a common origin, separated by **120°** each. 2. **Three Line Current Phasors:** $I_A$, $I_B$, $I_C$ are drawn lagging their respective phase voltages by angle $\phi$. 3. **Line Voltage $V_{AC}$:** Drawn from tip of $V_{CN}$ to tip of $V_{AN}$, leading $V_{AN}$ by 30° in the **lagging sense** (or equivalently, $V_{AC}$ is at -30° when $V_{AN}$ is at 0°). 4. **Line Voltage $V_{BC}$:** Drawn at -90° (perpendicular to $V_{AN}$). 5. **Angle for $W_1$:** The angle between $I_A$ and $V_{AC}$ is **$(30° - \phi)$** — this is the smaller angle. 6. **Angle for $W_2$:** The angle between $I_B$ and $V_{BC}$ is **$(30° + \phi)$** — this is the larger angle. > [!NOTE] > **As $\phi$ varies from 0° to 90°:** > - The angle for $W_1$ shrinks from 30° to -60° (i.e., $W_1$ keeps increasing). > - The angle for $W_2$ grows from 30° to 120° (i.e., $W_2$ decreases, crosses zero at $\phi = 60°$, and becomes negative for $\phi > 60°$). ## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB Start(["3-Phase 3-Wire
Load Connected"]):::start Step1["Measure V_L, I_L, phi"]:::step Step2["Compute W1 = V_L I_L cos 30 deg minus phi"]:::step Step3["Compute W2 = V_L I_L cos 30 deg plus phi"]:::step Step4{"Is W2 less than 0?"}:::decision Step5["Note: W2 is negative
Reverse pressure coil
Use negative sign"]:::special Step6["Total P equals W1 plus W2
equals sqrt 3 V_L I_L cos phi"]:::compute Step7["Compute tan phi equals
sqrt 3 times W1 minus W2
over W1 plus W2"]:::compute Step8["PF equals cos phi"]:::result End(["Power and PF
Determined"]):::end Start --> Step1 Step1 --> Step2 Step1 --> Step3 Step2 --> Step4 Step3 --> Step4 Step4 -->|Yes| Step5 Step4 -->|No| Step6 Step5 --> Step6 Step2 --> Step6 Step3 --> Step6 Step6 --> Step7 Step7 --> Step8 Step8 --> End classDef start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef step fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef special fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef compute fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef result fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef end fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State and explain Blondel's Theorem. Why is it relevant to the two-wattmeter method?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Blondel's Theorem states that the total power in an *n*-wire system can be accurately measured using *(n − 1)* wattmeters, provided the common point of the pressure coils is connected to the wire that is not carrying the current coil of any wattmeter. For a 3-phase, 3-wire system, *n = 3*, so *n − 1 = 2* wattmeters are required. This theorem is the theoretical foundation of the two-wattmeter method, justifying the use of exactly two wattmeters for three-wire systems and three wattmeters for four-wire systems (with neutral). **(3 marks)** ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **In the two-wattmeter method, the readings are 1000 W and 500 W. Calculate the total power and power factor of the load.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - Total Power: $P = W_1 + W_2 = 1000 + 500 = 1500$ W. **(1 mark)** - Power factor angle: $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(1000 - 500)}{1000 + 500} = \dfrac{500\sqrt{3}}{1500} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$. **(1 mark)** - Therefore, $\phi = 30°$ and Power Factor $= \cos 30° = 0.866$ (lagging). **(1 mark)** ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Derive the expressions for the readings of the two wattmeters in the two-wattmeter method for a balanced star-connected load. Hence obtain the expressions for total active power, total reactive power, and power factor.** #### **Part (a): Derivation of Wattmeter Readings — 7 Marks** The two wattmeters are connected such that the current coil of $W_1$ is in line A and that of $W_2$ is in line B. The pressure coil of $W_1$ is across lines A and C, while that of $W_2$ is across lines B and C. For phase sequence A-B-C, with phase voltage $V_p$ as reference and balanced load with impedance angle $\phi$ (lagging): - $V_{AN} = V_p \angle 0°$, so $V_{AC} = V_L \angle -30°$ - $V_{BN} = V_p \angle -120°$, so $V_{BC} = V_L \angle -90°$ - $I_A = I_L \angle -\phi$, $I_B = I_L \angle (-120° - \phi)$ The wattmeter reading is the real part of the phasor product: - $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(\angle V_{AC} - \angle I_A) = V_L I_L \cos(-30° + \phi) = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$ **(3 marks)** - $W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(\angle V_{BC} - \angle I_B) = V_L I_L \cos(-90° + 120° + \phi) = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$ **(3 marks)** - Phasor diagram with proper labeling **(1 mark)** #### **Part (b): Derivation of P, Q, and Power Factor — 7 Marks** **Total Active Power:** $$P = W_1 + W_2 = V_L I_L[\cos(30° - \phi) + \cos(30° + \phi)]$$ Using the identity $\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B) = 2\cos A \cos B$: $$P = 2 V_L I_L \cos 30° \cos\phi = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi \quad \text{...(1)} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **Total Reactive Power:** $$Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L[\cos(30° - \phi) - \cos(30° + \phi)]$$ Using the identity $\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B) = 2\sin A \sin B$: $$Q = 2\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin 30° \sin\phi = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \sin\phi \quad \text{...(2)} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **Power Factor:** Dividing equation (2) by equation (1): $$\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$ $$\text{Power Factor} = \cos\phi = \cos\!\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}\right)\right] \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **In a two-wattmeter test on a balanced 3-phase, 3-wire, 415 V load, the readings are $W_1 = 5000$ W and $W_2 = 3000$ W. Calculate: (a) the total active power, reactive power, and power factor of the load, and (b) the line current and the phase impedance (assuming star connection).** #### **Part (a): Power, Q, and PF — 7 Marks** **Total Active Power:** $$P = W_1 + W_2 = 5000 + 3000 = 8000 \text{ W} = 8 \text{ kW} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$ **Power Factor Angle:** $$\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(5000 - 3000)}{8000} = \dfrac{2000\sqrt{3}}{8000} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.433) = 23.41° \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$ **Power Factor:** $$\cos\phi = \cos(23.41°) = 0.9178 \text{ (lagging)} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$ **Total Reactive Power:** $$Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3} \times 2000 = 3464.1 \text{ VAR} \approx 3.464 \text{ kVAR} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$ #### **Part (b): Line Current and Phase Impedance — 7 Marks** **Line Current:** From $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$: $$I_L = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L \cos\phi} = \dfrac{8000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.9178} = \dfrac{8000}{659.16} = 12.14 \text{ A} \quad \textbf{(2.5 marks)}$$ **Phase Voltage (Star Connection):** $$V_p = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6 \text{ V} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$ **Phase Impedance Magnitude:** $$Z_p = \dfrac{V_p}{I_L} = \dfrac{239.6}{12.14} = 19.74 \text{ }\Omega \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$ **Phase Impedance (with angle):** $$Z_p = 19.74 \angle 23.41° \text{ }\Omega = (19.74 \cos 23.41° + j\,19.74 \sin 23.41°)\text{ }\Omega = (18.12 + j\,7.84)\text{ }\Omega \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign of $W_2$:** Many students forget that $W_2$ can be **negative** when power factor is below 0.5 (i.e., $\phi > 60°$). If the wattmeter reads below zero, **reverse the pressure coil connection**, take the reading as **negative**, and substitute with a negative sign in $P = W_1 + W_2$ and the PF formula. **(Common 2-mark loss)** > 2. **Phase Sequence:** Reversing the phase sequence (B-A-C instead of A-B-C) swaps the values of $W_1$ and $W_2$. The total power and power factor remain unchanged, but the individual readings swap. **(Common 1-mark loss)** > 3. **Three-Wire Restriction:** Do not apply the two-wattmeter method to a system with a neutral wire; it will give incorrect readings. Use three wattmeters for four-wire systems. **(Common 1-mark loss)** > 4. **Unit Confusion:** Reactive power $Q$ is in **VAR** (Volt-Ampere Reactive), not Watts. Make sure to use the correct unit. **(Common 1-mark loss)** > 5. **Formula Substitution Errors:** Be careful with the order in the $\tan\phi$ formula. It is $\sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)$, not $(W_1 - W_2)/\sqrt{3}$. A common error is to forget the $\sqrt{3}$ factor. **(Common 2-mark loss)** ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Two-Wattmeter Method:** - **Blondel's Theorem** → Use $(n-1)$ wattmeters for an $n$-wire system; for 3-wire, use **2 wattmeters**. - **Connection Rule** → Current coil of $W_1$ in line A, $W_2$ in line B; pressure coils of both meters terminate at the **third line (C)**. - **Reading Expressions** → $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$ and $W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$. - **Total Power** → $P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ (works for balanced AND unbalanced loads). - **Reactive Power** → $Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$ (in VAR). - **Power Factor** → $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}$, so $\text{PF} = \cos\!\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}\right)\right]$. - **Reading at Unity PF** → $W_1 = W_2 = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L I_L$ (both positive and equal). - **Reading at PF = 0.5** → $W_2 = 0$, $W_1 = V_L I_L$ (one meter reads zero). - **Reading at PF < 0.5** → $W_2$ is **negative**; reverse pressure coil and use negative sign. - **Phase Sequence Reversal** → $W_1$ and $W_2$ readings **swap values**; total power and PF remain unchanged. - **Three-Wire Restriction** → Method is valid only for 3-phase, 3-wire systems (no neutral). - **Sign of $\phi$** → $\phi$ is **positive** for lagging PF and **negative** for leading PF in the $\tan\phi$ formula; always take $\cos\phi$ as positive for the magnitude of PF. - **KTU High-Yield Result** → $W_1 - W_2$ gives reactive volt-amperes (scaled by $\sqrt{3}$); $W_1 + W_2$ gives real power in watts. - **Real-World Use** → All industrial 3-phase kWh meters; transformer and motor testing; PF correction studies.

topics.title: Include Simple Problems of Two Wattmeter Method. Measurement of Energy Using Induction Type Energy Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT411 - ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENT AND MEASURING INSTRUMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Include Simple Problems of Two Wattmeter Method. Measurement of Energy Using Induction Type Energy Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Two-Wattmeter Method (TWM) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Two-Wattmeter Method)**
> The Two-Wattmeter Method is a **three-phase power measurement technique** in which the total active power consumed by a balanced or unbalanced three-phase, three-wire system is measured using **only two wattmeters**, whose current coils are connected in any two of the three line conductors and whose pressure (voltage) coils are connected between those two lines and the third (common) line. It is governed by Blondel's Theorem, which states that the power in an *n*-wire system can be measured using *(n − 1)* wattmeters.

**Standard Boundary Conditions of the Method:**
- Number of wires in the system = **3** (three-wire, no neutral available).
- Wattmeters required = **n − 1 = 2** (Blondel's Compensation Condition).
- Power factor range measurable: **0 to unity** (both leading and lagging), with a **blind region near unity p.f.** for lagging loads where one reading may go negative.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Wattmeters Are Sufficient**
> The instantaneous power in a 3-phase system is $p = v_a i_a + v_b i_b + v_c i_c$. Since $i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$ (Kirchhoff's Current Law with no neutral), the expression can be rewritten so that the third term is absorbed into the other two. Hence, only two products need to be measured.

---

## 1.2 Induction Type Energy Meter — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Induction Type Energy Meter)**
> An Induction Type Energy Meter is a **kilowatt-hour (kWh) integrating instrument** that operates on the principle of a **two-phase induction motor**, where a thin **aluminum (Al) disc** is acted upon by two alternating fluxes produced by **shaded-pole electromagnets**, inducing eddy currents in the disc. The interaction of these fluxes with the eddy currents produces a torque proportional to the **instantaneous power**, and the speed of the disc becomes proportional to power. A **permanent magnet** provides braking, and a **gear-train counter** integrates revolutions to display energy in **kWh**.

**Standard Boundary Conditions:**
- Operating supply: **Single-phase AC, 50 Hz** (typical Indian/Kerala domestic supply: **230 V, 50 Hz**).
- Operating quantity: **Active energy (kWh)**, NOT reactive energy (kVArh).
- Operating principle: **Electromagnetic induction + Faraday's Law + Lorentz force**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

### Analogy for Two-Wattmeter Method
Imagine three friends (**R, Y, B**) sharing the cost of dinner equally but each paying different amounts. Instead of asking each one individually (which would need three receipts = three wattmeters), we observe that whatever one of them pays extra, the other pays less. So the total cost can be reconstructed by just **two cleverly placed receipts** = **Two Wattmeters**. The "clever placement" is connecting the two wattmeters in lines R and B with their voltage coil returns tied to line Y (the common line).

### Analogy for Induction Energy Meter
Think of a **ceiling fan with a disc instead of blades**. The fan (electromagnet) tries to spin the disc, but a **magnet on the side acts as a brake** to balance it. The harder the fan pushes (more power consumed), the faster the disc rotates against the brake. A **bicycle odometer (gear counter)** then counts the revolutions to show total distance (= total energy). The meter literally **counts how many "energy circles" the appliance has used**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Often Confused in Exams)**
> A *wattmeter* measures **instantaneous power (W or kW)**. An *energy meter* measures **total energy consumed over time (Wh or kWh) = Power × Time**. The energy meter is essentially a **wattmeter + time integrator (gear train)**.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Line Voltage (Star) | $V_L$ | **415 V** (KTU Kerala domestic/industrial) | TWM problems |
| Line Voltage (Delta) | $V_L$ | **415 V** | TWM problems |
| Phase Voltage | $V_{ph}$ | **230 V** | TWM problems |
| Supply Frequency | $f$ | **50 Hz** | Energy meter design |
| Disc material | Al | Conductivity $\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^7 \text{ S/m}$ | Energy meter |
| Disc thickness | $t$ | **0.5 to 1.0 mm** | Energy meter |
| Integration range | kWh | 0 to 99999.9 (typical) | Energy meter display |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two-Wattmeter Method — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The two wattmeters are connected such that:
- **Wattmeter 1 ($W_1$)**: Current coil in line **R**, Pressure coil between **R and Y** (i.e., across $V_{RY}$).
- **Wattmeter 2 ($W_2$)**: Current coil in line **B**, Pressure coil between **B and Y** (i.e., across $V_{BY}$).
- Line **Y** acts as the **common return (fictitious neutral point)**.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Reading Derivation

1. **Identify Line Voltages:**
   $V_{RY}$ leads $V_{BY}$ by **60°** in phase sequence R-Y-B (positive sequence).
2. **Identify Line Currents:**
   Each line current lags its respective phase voltage by angle $\phi$.
   - $I_R$ lags $V_{RN}$ by $\phi$
   - $I_B$ lags $V_{BN}$ by $\phi$
3. **Compute Phase Angle Between V and I in Each Wattmeter:**
   - For $W_1$: angle between $V_{RY}$ and $I_R = (30° - \phi)$ for a balanced load.
   - For $W_2$: angle between $V_{BY}$ and $I_B = (30° + \phi)$ for a balanced load.
4. **Apply Wattmeter Equation:** $W = V_L I_L \cos(\text{angle between } V \text{ and } I)$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "30° Trick"**
> The 30° appears because **line voltage leads phase voltage by 30°** in a star connection. For a balanced load, the angle the current makes with the line voltage becomes $(30° \mp \phi)$ — minus for $W_1$ and plus for $W_2$.

---

## 2.2 Final Formulas for Two-Wattmeter Method (Balanced Load)

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Sign Convention | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reading of Wattmeter 1 | $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$ | May be negative | W |
| 2 | Reading of Wattmeter 2 | $W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$ | May be negative | W |
| 3 | Total Active Power | $P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | Always positive | W |
| 4 | Total Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | Sign indicates leading/lagging | VAR |
| 5 | Power Factor Angle | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}$ | — | radians/degrees |
| 6 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{W_1 + W_2}{2\,V_L I_L}$ | Lagging if $W_1 > W_2$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | — | VA |
| 8 | Ratio of Readings | $\dfrac{W_1}{W_2} = \dfrac{\cos(30° - \phi)}{\cos(30° + \phi)}$ | Diagnostic of p.f. | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL Sign Pitfall:** When $\phi > 60°$ (p.f. < 0.5 lagging), the reading $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$ becomes **positive** while $W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$ becomes **NEGATIVE** because the argument exceeds 90°. In such cases, the total power is $P = W_1 - \mid W_2 \mid$, and the pressure coil connection of $W_2$ must be reversed.

---

## 2.3 Limiting Cases of Power Factor (KTU Favourite)

| Load Power Factor $\cos\phi$ | $\phi$ | $W_1$ | $W_2$ | $\dfrac{W_1}{W_2}$ | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1.0 (Unity)** | 0° | $V_L I_L \cos 30° = 0.866 V_L I_L$ | $V_L I_L \cos 30° = 0.866 V_L I_L$ | 1.0 | Both equal |
| **0.866 (Lagging)** | 30° | $V_L I_L \cos 0° = V_L I_L$ | $V_L I_L \cos 60° = 0.5 V_L I_L$ | 2.0 | $W_1$ maximum |
| **0.5 (Lagging)** | 60° | $V_L I_L \cos(-30°) = 0.866 V_L I_L$ | $V_L I_L \cos 90° = 0$ | $\infty$ | $W_2 = 0$ |
| **< 0.5 (Lagging)** | > 60° | Positive | **Negative** | Negative ratio | Reverse $W_2$ |
| **0 (ZPSL — Pure Reactance)** | 90° | $V_L I_L \cos(-60°) = 0.5 V_L I_L$ | $V_L I_L \cos 120° = -0.5 V_L I_L$ | −1.0 | $P = 0$ |

---

## 2.4 Induction Type Energy Meter — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.4.1 Construction (Five Functional Systems)

| System | Component | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Driving System** | Series electromagnet (current coil) | Silicon-iron laminations + Cu winding | Carries **line current** → produces flux $\phi_1 \propto I$ |
| **1. Driving System** | Shunt electromagnet (pressure coil) | Silicon-iron laminations + fine Cu winding | Carries current $\propto V/ R_p$ → produces flux $\phi_2 \propto V$ |
| **2. Moving System** | Aluminum disc | High-conductivity Al, thin | Carries **induced eddy currents** $i_e$ |
| **3. Controlling System** | Permanent magnet (C-shaped) | Alnico or modern rare-earth | Produces **braking torque** $T_b$ |
| **4. Registering System** | Gear train + decade dials | Brass/Plastic gears | Integrates **revolutions → kWh** |
| **5. Compensating Device** | Shading band + lag plate | Copper ring on shunt pole | Reduces **phase error** and **friction error** |

### 2.4.2 Working Principle — Physics Behind the Meter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-Stage Operation**
> 1. **Driving Flux Generation:** Two fluxes, $\phi_1$ (from series coil, $\propto I$) and $\phi_2$ (from shunt coil, $\propto V$), are produced with a **90° phase difference** between them (achieved by the shading band and the inductive nature of the pressure coil).
> 2. **Eddy Current Induction:** These alternating fluxes cut the aluminum disc, inducing **eddy currents** $i_1$ and $i_2$ in the disc by **Faraday's Law**: $e = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$.
> 3. **Torque Production (Lorentz Force):** The interaction of each flux with the eddy current produced by the other flux yields a **tangential force** on the disc, giving **driving torque** $T_d \propto \phi_1 \phi_2 \sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the two fluxes.

---

## 2.5 Driving Torque Equation (Analytical Derivation Setup)

Let the fluxes be:
- $\phi_1 = \Phi_{1m} \sin(\omega t)$ (in phase with current $I$)
- $\phi_2 = \Phi_{2m} \sin(\omega t - \alpha)$ (lagging by angle $\alpha$ due to coil inductance)

The induced EMF and eddy current in disc due to $\phi_1$:
- $e_1 = -k_1 \dfrac{d\phi_1}{dt} = -k_1 \omega \Phi_{1m} \cos(\omega t)$
- $i_1 = \dfrac{e_1}{Z_{disc}}$

The eddy current $i_1$ interacts with $\phi_2$ to produce force:
- $df = B_2 \cdot i_1 \cdot dl$, where $B_2 \propto \phi_2$

The **average driving torque** over one cycle:
$$T_d = k \cdot \phi_1 \phi_2 \sin\alpha$$

When **properly compensated** such that $\alpha = 90°$ (fluxes in quadrature), $\sin\alpha = 1$, and:
$$T_d = k \cdot \phi_1 \phi_2 = k \cdot V \cdot I = k \cdot P_{instantaneous}$$

### 2.5.1 Braking Torque (Permanent Magnet)

The aluminum disc rotating with angular velocity $\omega_m$ cuts the permanent magnet flux $\phi_m$, inducing an EMF and a current. The reaction produces a **retarding torque**:
$$T_b = K_b \cdot \phi_m^2 \cdot \omega_m$$

### 2.5.2 Steady-State Speed Equation

At equilibrium, $T_d = T_b$:
$$k \cdot P = K_b \cdot \phi_m^2 \cdot \omega_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_m = \dfrac{k}{K_b \phi_m^2}\, P = K \cdot P$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Result**
> Disc speed $\omega_m$ is **directly proportional to power** $P$. This is why a counter (gear train) integrating the revolutions (∫ω dt) gives total **energy** (∫P dt = kWh).

**Number of revolutions** in time $T$:
$$N = \int_0^T \omega_m\, dt = K \int_0^T P\, dt = K \cdot E_{energy}$$

The **meter constant** $K_m$ (rev/kWh):
$$K_m = \dfrac{N}{E(\text{kWh})} = \dfrac{500 \text{ to } 1000 \text{ rev}}{\text{kWh}} \quad \text{(typical KTU value)}$$

---

## 2.6 Errors and Compensations in Induction Energy Meter (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Error | Cause | Symptom | Compensation Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Phase Error** | Pressure-coil flux not exactly 90° to series flux | Meter reads high at low p.f. lagging | **Shading band** (copper loop) on shunt pole shifts flux phase |
| 2 | **Speed Error** | Variation in supply voltage | Speed changes with voltage | Permanent magnet (brake flux $\phi_m$ varies little with V) |
| 3 | **Friction Error** | Bearing + gear-train friction | Stops at low loads (no rotation) | **Shading band (friction compensation)** — creates a small extra torque at low load |
| 4 | **Creep** | Friction over-compensation at low loads | Disc rotates with **no load** | Two **symmetric holes** in the disc on opposite sides of the spindle |
| 5 | **Temperature Error** | Disc resistance & permanent magnet flux change with temperature | Reading changes with ambient temp. | **Temperature compensation plate** (Ni-Cu alloy) on permanent magnet pole |
| 6 | **Over-voltage Error** | Supply voltage > rated | Excessive shunt flux | Use **magnetic shunt** (iron piece) to divert excess flux |
| 7 | **Frequency Error** | Frequency deviation from 50 Hz | Both fluxes change but unequally | Use **non-inductive shunt coil resistor** to reduce frequency sensitivity |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction**
> *Creep* is **continuous rotation of the disc with no load** (just the pressure coil energized). It is NOT the same as friction. Friction is a **passive resisting torque**; creep is an **active unwanted rotation**.

---

## 2.7 Three-Phase Energy Meter (Brief — KTU Module 3 Coverage)

For a 3-phase, 4-wire system: **Three** driving systems on **one or two** discs.
For a 3-phase, 3-wire system: **Two** driving systems (equivalent to two-wattmeter method) on **two discs** mounted on a common spindle.

> The reading = **sum of the two** registrations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Two-Wattmeter Method Formulae

### 3.1.1 Derivation of $W_1$ and $W_2$ Expressions

**Assumption:** Balanced, 3-phase, 3-wire, star-connected load with phase sequence R-Y-B (positive sequence).

**Step 1 — Define the phase voltages (taken as reference):**

Let $V_{RN} = V_{ph} \angle 0°$ (reference for phase R).
Then, $V_{YN} = V_{ph} \angle -120°$ and $V_{BN} = V_{ph} \angle -240° = V_{ph} \angle +120°$.

**Step 2 — Define the line currents (each lagging phase voltage by $\phi$):**

$$I_R = I_L \angle -\phi$$
$$I_Y = I_L \angle -(120° + \phi)$$
$$I_B = I_L \angle (120° - \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Determine the line voltages across the wattmeter pressure coils:**

The pressure coil of $W_1$ sees $V_{RY} = V_{RN} - V_{YN}$.

Using phasor subtraction:
$$V_{RY} = V_{ph}\angle 0° - V_{ph}\angle -120° = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ph} \angle 30° = V_L \angle 30°$$

Similarly:
$$V_{BY} = V_{BN} - V_{YN} = V_{ph}\angle 120° - V_{ph}\angle -120° = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ph} \angle 90° = V_L \angle 90°$$

Wait — for a more standard derivation aligned with the **star-connected load** convention, we get:
- $V_{RY} = V_L \angle 30°$ (line voltage leads phase voltage of R by 30°)
- $V_{BY} = V_L \angle -30°$ when measured from the same reference (for R-Y-B sequence, $V_{BY}$ is the *third* line, lagging $V_{RY}$ by 60°)

**Step 4 — Compute the angle between current and voltage for each wattmeter:**

For **Wattmeter 1** (current $I_R$ through current coil, voltage $V_{RY}$ across pressure coil):
- Voltage phasor: $V_{RY} = V_L \angle 30°$
- Current phasor: $I_R = I_L \angle -\phi$
- Angle between them: $30° - (-\phi) = 30° + \phi$ ... 

**Corrected and Standard Form:** For a balanced load, the angle between $I_R$ and $V_{RY}$ is $\phi - 30°$ if we use a particular reference; but the universally accepted KTU form (matching the most common textbook convention) is:
- $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$
- $W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$

The convention depends on whether we treat $I_R$ as lagging $V_{RN}$ or as leading it, and on the chosen reference. The derivation follows.

Let us set: $V_{RN} = V_{ph}\angle 0°$, $V_{YN} = V_{ph}\angle -120°$, $V_{BN} = V_{ph}\angle -240°$.

Then: $I_R = I_L \angle (0° - \phi) = I_L \angle -\phi$ (lagging).

Compute $V_{RY} = V_{RN} - V_{YN}$:
$$V_{RY} = V_{ph}\left[\cos 0° + j\sin 0°\right] - V_{ph}\left[\cos(-120°) + j\sin(-120°)\right]$$
$$V_{RY} = V_{ph} - V_{ph}\left[-\frac{1}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right] = V_{ph}\left[\frac{3}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]$$
$$V_{RY} = V_{ph} \sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} \angle \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}/2}{3/2}\right) = V_{ph} \sqrt{3} \angle 30° = V_L \angle 30°$$

The angle between $V_{RY}$ (at $30°$) and $I_R$ (at $-\phi$) is:
$$\text{Angle} = 30° - (-\phi) = 30° + \phi$$

This would give $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$, but KTU convention (matching **A.K. Sawhney** and **Bakshi**) treats the angle as $(30° - \phi)$ because they measure angle from the current to the voltage, not the other way. The cosine is even, but the **derivation sign** matters for **$W_1$ vs $W_2$ asymmetry**. KTU uses the convention:

$$W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi), \quad W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$$

where $W_1$ corresponds to the wattmeter whose current coil is in the **leading line**.

**Step 5 — Add the wattmeter readings to get total power:**

$$P = W_1 + W_2 = V_L I_L \left[\cos(30° - \phi) + \cos(30° + \phi)\right]$$

Using the sum-to-product identity:
$$\cos A + \cos B = 2 \cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cos\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$$

With $A = (30° - \phi)$ and $B = (30° + \phi)$:
$$\frac{A+B}{2} = 30°, \quad \frac{A-B}{2} = -\phi$$

Therefore:
$$W_1 + W_2 = V_L I_L \cdot 2\cos 30° \cos \phi = V_L I_L \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cos\phi = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$$

$$\boxed{P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi}$$

**Step 6 — Subtract to get reactive power:**

$$Q_{reading} = W_1 - W_2 = V_L I_L \left[\cos(30° - \phi) - \cos(30° + \phi)\right]$$

Using the difference identity:
$$\cos A - \cos B = -2 \sin\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$$

$$W_1 - W_2 = V_L I_L \left[-2 \sin 30° \sin(-\phi)\right] = V_L I_L \left[-2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot (-\sin\phi)\right] = V_L I_L \sin\phi$$

Multiplying by $\sqrt{3}$ to get reactive power in VAR:
$$\boxed{Q = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \sin\phi}$$

**Step 7 — Power factor from wattmeter ratio:**

Dividing:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{\sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}$$

$$\boxed{\cos\phi = \frac{W_1 + W_2}{2\,V_L I_L}}$$

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation: Driving Torque of Induction Energy Meter

### Setup
- Series coil flux: $\phi_1 = \Phi_{1m} \sin(\omega t)$ — **in phase** with line current $i = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$, so $\phi_1 = K_1 I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$.
- Shunt (pressure) coil flux: $\phi_2 = \Phi_{2m} \sin(\omega t - \alpha)$ — **lagging** by angle $\alpha$ (designed to be ~90° for proper operation).

### Step 1: EMF and Eddy Current Induced by $\phi_1$

The flux $\phi_1$ linking a hypothetical secondary loop in the disc induces EMF:
$$e_1 = -M_1 \frac{d\phi_1}{dt} = -M_1 \omega \Phi_{1m} \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

The eddy current due to $e_1$ (assuming purely resistive disc, $R_1$ is the disc resistance):
$$i_1 = \frac{e_1}{R_1} = -\frac{M_1 \omega \Phi_{1m}}{R_1} \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

### Step 2: Force on Disc Due to $i_1$ and $\phi_2$

The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field ($F = BIL$, integrated over the loop):
$$df \propto \phi_2 \cdot i_1$$

The instantaneous torque on the disc element:
$$dT_1 = k_1 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot i_1 \cdot dr$$

### Step 3: Average Torque Over One Cycle

$$T_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} k_1 \phi_2 i_1\, d(\omega t)$$

Substituting:
$$T_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} k_1 \Phi_{2m} \sin(\omega t - \alpha) \cdot \left[-\frac{M_1 \omega \Phi_{1m}}{R_1} \cos(\omega t - \phi)\right] d(\omega t)$$

Using the identity $\sin A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$:

$$T_1 = -\frac{k_1 M_1 \omega \Phi_{1m} \Phi_{2m}}{2\pi R_1} \int_0^{2\pi} \sin(\omega t - \alpha) \cos(\omega t - \phi)\, d(\omega t)$$

Over a complete cycle, the integral of $\sin A \cos B$ over $2\pi$ is $-\pi \sin(B - A)$:
$$T_1 = -\frac{k_1 M_1 \omega \Phi_{1m} \Phi_{2m}}{2\pi R_1} \cdot \left[-\pi \sin(\phi - \alpha)\right] = \frac{k_1 M_1 \omega \Phi_{1m} \Phi_{2m}}{2 R_1} \sin(\phi - \alpha)$$

By symmetry, the torque $T_2$ due to the other interaction ($\phi_1$ with eddy current from $\phi_2$) gives an equal and additive contribution. Thus:

$$\boxed{T_d = K_d \cdot \phi_1 \phi_2 \sin\alpha}$$

where $K_d$ is a meter constant, and the angle $\alpha$ between the two fluxes is the operating angle.

### Step 4: Specialization to $\alpha = 90°$ (Correct Compensation)

When the energy meter is properly phase-compensated, the pressure-coil flux $\phi_2$ leads the series-coil flux $\phi_1$ by **exactly 90°** (i.e., $\alpha = 90°$). Then:
$$T_d = K_d \cdot \phi_1 \phi_2 \sin 90° = K_d \cdot \phi_1 \phi_2$$

Since $\phi_1 \propto I$ (series flux) and $\phi_2 \propto V$ (shunt flux):
$$T_d = K \cdot V \cdot I = K \cdot P$$

---

## 3.3 Full Derivation: Braking Torque & Speed Equation

### Step 1: EMF Induced by Permanent Magnet Flux

The permanent magnet produces a constant flux $\phi_m$ through the disc. As the disc rotates with angular velocity $\omega_m$, the rate of flux cutting is $\phi_m \omega_m$. The induced EMF in the disc (across a radial path) is:
$$e_b = K_b \cdot \phi_m \cdot \omega_m$$

### Step 2: Eddy Current and Braking Force

The induced eddy current in the disc:
$$i_b = \frac{e_b}{R_b} = \frac{K_b \phi_m \omega_m}{R_b}$$

The Lorentz force between $i_b$ and the flux $\phi_m$ (acting tangentially to oppose motion by **Lenz's Law**):
$$F_b \propto \phi_m \cdot i_b \propto \phi_m^2 \cdot \omega_m$$

Therefore, the braking torque:
$$\boxed{T_b = K_b \cdot \phi_m^2 \cdot \omega_m}$$

### Step 3: Steady-State Equilibrium

In steady state, $T_d = T_b$:
$$K_d \cdot V \cdot I = K_b \cdot \phi_m^2 \cdot \omega_m$$
$$\omega_m = \frac{K_d}{K_b \phi_m^2} \cdot P = K \cdot P$$

The total number of revolutions in time $T$:
$$N = \int_0^T \omega_m\, dt = K \int_0^T P\, dt = K \cdot E_{kWh}$$

**Number of revolutions per kWh** (Meter Constant):
$$K_m = \frac{N}{E} = \frac{3600 \cdot 1000}{P_{\text{rated}} / N_{\text{rated}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**
> The two-wattmeter method is used in **switchboard instruments** for industrial three-phase loads (motors, transformers testing) and is the principle behind **three-phase power analyzers**. The induction energy meter is the workhorse of **domestic electricity billing in India (BESCOM, KSEB)** and most global utilities, calibrated to read directly in **kWh** with a typical accuracy class of **1.0 or 2.0** as per IS 722 / IEC 62053-21.

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Problems (KTU Style)

### Problem 1 — Standard Balanced Load

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2022 Pattern]**
> In a balanced 3-phase, 3-wire, 415 V system, two wattmeters read $W_1 = 5000$ W and $W_2 = 1000$ W. Determine:
> (i) Total power
> (ii) Power factor
> (iii) Reactive power
> (iv) Line current

**Solution:**

(i) Total power:
$$P = W_1 + W_2 = 5000 + 1000 = 6000 \text{ W} = 6 \text{ kW}$$

(ii) Power factor:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,(5000 - 1000)}{5000 + 1000} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot 4000}{6000} = \frac{4\sqrt{3}}{6} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}$$
$$\tan\phi = 1.1547 \Rightarrow \phi = 49.11°$$
$$\cos\phi = \cos(49.11°) = 0.6547 \text{ (lagging)}$$

(iii) Reactive power:
$$Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3} \cdot 4000 = 6928.2 \text{ VAR} \approx 6.928 \text{ kVAR}$$

(iv) Line current:
$$I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L \cos\phi} = \frac{6000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 0.6547} = \frac{6000}{470.84} = 12.74 \text{ A}$$

**Verification using $W_1$ and $W_2$ individually:**
- $V_L I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\cos\phi} = \frac{6000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 0.6547} = 5292$ VA
- $W_1 = 5292 \cos(30° - 49.11°) = 5292 \cos(-19.11°) = 5292 \cdot 0.9449 = 5000$ W ✓
- $W_2 = 5292 \cos(30° + 49.11°) = 5292 \cos(79.11°) = 5292 \cdot 0.1890 = 1000$ W ✓

---

### Problem 2 — Leading Power Factor (Capacitive Load)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2021 Pattern]**
> A balanced 3-phase, 3-wire, 400 V load takes 30 kW at 0.8 p.f. leading. Find the readings of two wattmeters connected in the standard manner.

**Solution:**

Given: $P = 30$ kW, $V_L = 400$ V, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ leading, so $\phi = 36.87°$ (leading, hence $-36.87°$).

**Step 1 — Line current:**
$$I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L \cos\phi} = \frac{30000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 0.8} = \frac{30000}{554.26} = 54.13 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Wattmeter readings:**

For **leading** p.f., the formula becomes:
$$W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi) \quad \text{(for leading)}$$
$$W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi) \quad \text{(for leading)}$$

This is because for leading load, the current phasor is "ahead" of the voltage by $\phi$, and the angle subtended is inverted.

- $W_1 = 400 \cdot 54.13 \cdot \cos(30° + 36.87°) = 21652 \cdot \cos(66.87°) = 21652 \cdot 0.3923 = 8494$ W
- $W_2 = 400 \cdot 54.13 \cdot \cos(30° - 36.87°) = 21652 \cdot \cos(-6.87°) = 21652 \cdot 0.9928 = 21495$ W

**Verification:**
$$W_1 + W_2 = 8494 + 21495 = 29989 \approx 30000 \text{ W} ✓$$
$$W_2 - W_1 = 21495 - 8494 = 13001, \quad \sqrt{3}(W_2 - W_1) = 22518 \text{ VAR} \approx Q$$

**Note:** For a leading load, $W_2 > W_1$, opposite to lagging case.

---

### Problem 3 — Determination of Phase Sequence Using TWM

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Pattern]**
> Two wattmeters connected to read total power in a 3-phase system give $W_1 = 2000$ W and $W_2 = -500$ W (with reversed pressure coil). The line voltage is 400 V. Calculate: (a) Total power, (b) Power factor, (c) Reactive power, (d) State the load nature.

**Solution:**

Since $W_2$ was measured with reversed pressure coil, the **true** reading is $W_2 = +500$ W (algebraically, the reverse connection gives a negative of the true reading).

Wait — let us follow the standard convention: $W_2$ is read as $-500$ W on the meter. The true magnitude is $500$ W, but the sign convention requires:
$$W_2^{true} = -500 \text{ W (algebraic)}$$

**Step 1 — Total power:**
$$P = W_1 + W_2 = 2000 + (-500) = 1500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Reactive power:**
$$Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\,(2000 - (-500)) = \sqrt{3} \cdot 2500 = 4330.13 \text{ VAR}$$

**Step 3 — Power factor:**
$$\tan\phi = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{4330.13}{1500} = 2.8867 \Rightarrow \phi = 70.89°$$
$$\cos\phi = \cos(70.89°) = 0.327 \text{ (lagging)}$$

**Step 4 — Load nature:** Since $W_2$ is negative, the load is **lagging with p.f. < 0.5**. With $P > 0$ and $Q > 0$, the load is **inductive (lagging)**.

---

### Problem 4 — Wattmeter Reading Reversal (KTU Common Question)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**
> A 3-phase, 415 V, balanced load draws 20 A at a lagging p.f. of 0.4. The two-wattmeter method is used. Calculate the two wattmeter readings. Will any wattmeter show a reversed reading? Justify.

**Solution:**

Given: $V_L = 415$ V, $I_L = 20$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.4$ lagging, so $\phi = 66.42°$.

**Step 1 — Compute $W_1$:**
$$W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi) = 415 \cdot 20 \cdot \cos(30° - 66.42°) = 8300 \cdot \cos(-36.42°) = 8300 \cdot 0.8044 = 6676.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $W_2$:**
$$W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi) = 415 \cdot 20 \cdot \cos(30° + 66.42°) = 8300 \cdot \cos(96.42°) = 8300 \cdot (-0.1116) = -926.3 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** Yes, $W_2$ shows a **negative reading** because $\cos\phi = 0.4$ lagging corresponds to $\phi > 60°$, which makes $\cos(30° + \phi)$ negative. The pressure coil of $W_2$ must be **reversed** to obtain the magnitude, and the total power is:
$$P = W_1 + W_2 = 6676.4 + (-926.3) = 5750.1 \text{ W} \approx 5.75 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification:** $P = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 20 \cdot 0.4 = 5750.4$ W ✓

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    Y["LINE Y COMMON RETURN"]:::common

    W1PC["W1 PRESSURE COIL"]:::coil
    W2CC["W2 CURRENT COIL"]:::coil
    W2PC["W2 PRESSURE COIL"]:::coil
    
    LOAD["BALANCED 3-PHASE LOAD"]:::load
    
    R --> W1CC

    B --> W2CC
    W2CC --> SOURCE["3-PHASE SOURCE"]:::src

    W1PC -.-> Y

    W2PC -.-> Y

```

**Caption:** Standard TWM connection. The current coil of W1 is in line R, the current coil of W2 is in line B, and the pressure coils of both are returned to line Y (the common reference). The 3-phase load is delta- or star-connected at the other end.
