# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> State Blondel's Theorem. How many wattmeters are required to measure power in a 3-phase, 4-wire system? Justify.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Blondel's Theorem (1 mark)**
> The total power in an *n*-wire polyphase system can be measured using *(n − 1)* wattmeters, provided that the common point of the pressure coil circuits is connected to one of the phases and the current coils are connected in the remaining *n − 1* wires.

For a 3-phase, **4-wire system**, $n = 4$, so the number of wattmeters required is $n - 1 = \mathbf{3}$ (1 mark).

**Justification (1 mark):** In a 4-wire system, the neutral wire carries a current $I_N = I_R + I_Y + I_B$ (KCL), which is generally **non-zero**. Hence the third current cannot be eliminated algebraically, and all three line currents must be measured independently with three wattmeters. The two-wattmeter method fails here because the assumption $I_R + I_Y + I_B = 0$ is violated.

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
> What is *creep* in an induction type energy meter? State **two** causes of creep and explain how it is prevented.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition of Creep (1 mark)**
> Creep is the **slow, continuous rotation of the aluminium disc** of an induction type energy meter **with no load** connected (i.e., only the pressure coil is energized). It leads to **erroneous energy registration** even when the consumer is not drawing any load.

**Two causes (1 mark):**
1. **Over-compensation of friction** by the shading band — the small auxiliary torque created to overcome friction persists even at zero load.
2. **Stray magnetic fields** from the supply wiring or external sources that induce additional eddy currents in the disc.
3. **Excess voltage** on the pressure coil (over-voltage condition) creating disproportionate shunt flux.
4. Vibration or mechanical imbalance in the meter assembly.

**Prevention (1 mark):**
- Drilling **two small diametrically opposite holes** in the aluminium disc on either side of the spindle. These holes interrupt the eddy current path under the shunt pole at no-load, eliminating the driving torque.
- Alternatively, attaching a **small piece of iron** (called a *creep stopper*) to the edge of the disc that is attracted to a pole piece at no-load, creating a frictional restraint.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Pattern**
> Each Part B question is for 14 marks with internal choice (Or option). Each carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. We provide **Question A (Or)** and **Question B (Or)** as two full-length alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 3, Pattern A]**
> 
> **(a)** With a neat phasor diagram, derive the expressions for the readings of two wattmeters used to measure power in a balanced 3-phase, 3-wire system. Hence, derive the formula for power factor in terms of the two wattmeter readings. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** In a 3-phase, 400 V, balanced load, the two wattmeters read $W_1 = 3000$ W and $W_2 = -1000$ W (with the pressure coil of $W_2$ reversed). Determine: (i) Total active power, (ii) Total reactive power, (iii) Power factor and its nature, (iv) Line current, (v) Phase impedance of the load assuming star connection. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**[1 mark — Setup]**
Consider a balanced, 3-phase, 3-wire, star-connected load with phase sequence R-Y-B. The two wattmeters are connected such that:
- $W_1$: Current coil in line R, pressure coil between R and Y.
- $W_2$: Current coil in line B, pressure coil between B and Y.

Line Y acts as the common (fictitious neutral) return.

**[2 marks — Phasor definitions]**
Let the phase voltages be:
$$V_{RN} = V_{ph}\angle 0°, \quad V_{YN} = V_{ph}\angle -120°, \quad V_{BN} = V_{ph}\angle +120°$$

Let the line currents (each lagging phase voltage by $\phi$):
$$I_R = I_L \angle -\phi, \quad I_B = I_L \angle (120° - \phi)$$

The line voltage across $W_1$'s pressure coil is $V_{RY}$:
$$V_{RY} = V_{RN} - V_{YN} = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ph}\angle 30° = V_L \angle 30°$$

The line voltage across $W_2$'s pressure coil is $V_{BY}$:
$$V_{BY} = V_{BN} - V_{YN} = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ph}\angle 90° = V_L \angle 90°$$

**[1 mark — Wattmeter equations]**
The angle between $V_{RY}$ and $I_R$ is $(30° + \phi)$ (using KTU convention with reference frame adjustments, this simplifies to $(30° - \phi)$ for the standard form):
$$W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$$
$$W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$$

**[2 marks — Total power and p.f.]**
$$P = W_1 + W_2 = V_L I_L [\cos(30° - \phi) + \cos(30° + \phi)]$$

Using $\cos A + \cos B = 2\cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)\cos\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$:
$$P = V_L I_L \cdot 2\cos 30° \cos\phi = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$$

Similarly:
$$Q = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$$

**[1 mark — Power factor formula]**
$$\tan\phi = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{\sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}$$
$$\boxed{\cos\phi = \frac{W_1 + W_2}{2\,V_L I_L}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**[Given data]**
$V_L = 400$ V, $W_1 = 3000$ W, $W_2 = -1000$ W (reversed).

**[1 mark] (i) Total Active Power:**
$$P = W_1 + W_2 = 3000 + (-1000) = 2000 \text{ W} = 2 \text{ kW}$$

**[1 mark] (ii) Total Reactive Power:**
$$Q = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\,(3000 - (-1000)) = \sqrt{3} \cdot 4000 = 6928.2 \text{ VAR}$$

**[2 marks] (iii) Power Factor:**
$$\tan\phi = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{6928.2}{2000} = 3.464$$
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(3.464) = 73.9°$$
$$\cos\phi = \cos(73.9°) = 0.277 \text{ (lagging)}$$

**Nature:** $W_2$ is negative AND $W_1 > |W_2|$, hence load is **lagging with p.f. < 0.5**.

**[1 mark] (iv) Line Current:**
$$I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L \cos\phi} = \frac{2000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 0.277} = \frac{2000}{191.85} = 10.42 \text{ A}$$

**[2 marks] (v) Phase Impedance (Star Connection):**
$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V}$$
$$Z_{ph} = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_{ph}} = \frac{230.94}{10.42} = 22.16 \text{ }\Omega$$
$$R = Z_{ph}\cos\phi = 22.16 \cdot 0.277 = 6.14 \text{ }\Omega$$
$$X_L = Z_{ph}\sin\phi = 22.16 \cdot \sin(73.9°) = 22.16 \cdot 0.961 = 21.30 \text{ }\Omega$$
$$L = \frac{X_L}{2\pi f} = \frac{21.30}{2\pi \cdot 50} = 67.79 \text{ mH}$$

**Final Answer:** $Z_{ph} = 6.14 + j21.30 \text{ }\Omega = 22.16 \angle 73.9° \text{ }\Omega$ per phase.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 3, Pattern B]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a single-phase induction type energy meter with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for driving torque and explain how the meter registers energy in kWh. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** What is meant by *phase error* and *friction error* in an induction type energy meter? Explain how each is compensated. Also explain the phenomenon of *creep* and the methods to prevent it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**[2 marks] Construction:**
A single-phase induction type energy meter consists of the following sub-systems:
1. **Driving System:** Two electromagnets — *series electromagnet* (carries line current, with few turns of thick wire) and *shunt electromagnet* (carries current proportional to voltage, with many turns of fine wire). Both have **laminated silicon-iron cores** to reduce eddy current losses.
2. **Moving System:** A thin **aluminium disc** mounted on a vertical spindle, positioned so that it passes through the air gaps of both electromagnets.
3. **Controlling System:** A **permanent magnet** (C-shaped or rectangular) placed diametrically opposite to the electromagnets so that the disc passes through its air gap too.
4. **Registering System:** A **gear train** coupled to the spindle, driving a set of **decade dials** that indicate energy in **kWh**.
5. **Compensating Devices:** A **shading band** (copper strip) wound around part of the shunt pole, a **temperature compensator** (Ni-Cu plate), and **two creep-prevention holes** in the disc.

**[2 marks] Working:**
- The series coil carries the **load current** $I$ and produces a flux $\phi_1$ in phase with $I$.
- The shunt coil is connected across the supply, draws a current proportional to $V$, and produces a flux $\phi_2$ lagging the supply voltage by some angle (designed to be ~90° to $\phi_1$).
- The two alternating fluxes $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ cut the aluminium disc and induce **eddy currents** in it.
- The interaction between $\phi_1$ and eddy current $i_2$ (induced by $\phi_2$), and between $\phi_2$ and eddy current $i_1$ (induced by $\phi_1$), produces a **tangential driving torque** on the disc, causing it to rotate.

**[2 marks] Driving Torque Derivation:**
As derived in Section 3.2:
$$T_d = K_d \cdot \phi_1 \phi_2 \sin\alpha$$

For properly compensated meter, $\alpha = 90°$, so:
$$T_d = K_d \cdot \phi_1 \phi_2 = K \cdot V \cdot I = K \cdot P \text{ (active power)}$$

**[1 mark] Energy Registration:**
The braking torque from the permanent magnet is:
$$T_b = K_b \phi_m^2 \omega_m$$

At steady state, $T_d = T_b$:
$$\omega_m = \frac{K_d}{K_b \phi_m^2} \cdot P = K \cdot P$$

Hence disc **speed is proportional to power**. The number of revolutions in time $T$:
$$N = \int_0^T \omega_m\, dt = K \int_0^T P\, dt = K \cdot E_{kWh}$$

The gear train integrates these revolutions, and the dial counter displays total **energy in kWh**.

---

**Part (b) — Errors and Compensations (7 marks):**

**[2 marks] Phase Error:**
- **Definition:** Phase error occurs when the angle between the series flux $\phi_1$ and the shunt flux $\phi_2$ is **not exactly 90°**, causing the driving torque to be $T_d = K_d \phi_1 \phi_2 \sin\alpha \neq K \cdot P$.
- **Effect:** The meter reads **high at low power factors** (lagging), because $\sin\alpha > 1$ cannot occur, but the deviation is most pronounced when $\phi$ is large.
- **Compensation:** A **shading band** (a closed copper loop) is wound around a portion of the shunt pole. The flux linking this band induces an EMF and a current in the band, which produces an **additional flux** that shifts the phase of the total shunt flux. By adjusting the position and cross-section of the band, the angle $\alpha$ is set to exactly 90° (or as close as possible). This is the **lag adjustment**.

**[1 mark] Friction Error:**
- **Definition:** Mechanical friction in the bearings, gear train, and at the spindle causes a **passive resisting torque** $T_f$ that opposes the driving torque. At low loads, $T_d < T_f$ and the disc fails to rotate (the meter does not register).
- **Compensation:** The same **shading band** is positioned asymmetrically on the shunt pole such that it produces a small **auxiliary torque** in the same direction as the main driving torque. This auxiliary torque is sufficient to overcome friction at low loads, but is small enough to be negligible at high loads. This is the **friction adjustment** (also called *light load adjustment*).

**[2 marks] Creep:**
- **Definition:** Creep is the **slow, unwanted rotation of the aluminium disc** of an energy meter when **no load is connected** (i.e., only the pressure coil is energized). The shading band over-compensation and stray magnetic fields cause a small residual torque.
- **Effects:** The meter registers energy even when the consumer is not using any power, leading to **inflated electricity bills**.
- **Prevention (2 methods):**
  1. **Drilling two diametrically opposite holes** in the aluminium disc, on either side of the spindle, in the path of the shunt flux. These holes interrupt the eddy current path under the shunt pole at no-load, eliminating the residual driving torque.
  2. **Attaching a small iron piece (creep stopper)** to the edge of the disc, which is attracted to a pole piece at the shunt electromagnet when no-load, creating a frictional force that prevents rotation.

**[2 marks] Other Compensations (additional marks if covered):**
- **Temperature Error:** A **Ni-Cu alloy shunt** is attached to the permanent magnet. As temperature rises, the magnet's flux decreases, but the disc resistance increases — these two effects are made to compensate each other.
- **Over-voltage Error:** A **magnetic shunt** (piece of low-reluctance iron) is placed across the permanent magnet's pole pieces. Excessive supply voltage causes saturation in the main iron, diverting the extra flux to the magnetic shunt and limiting the increase in shunt flux.
- **Frequency Error:** A **non-inductive resistor** is connected in series with the pressure coil so that the pressure-coil current becomes more independent of frequency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully**
> 
> **Common Mark Loss Areas in This Topic:**
> 1. **Sign Error in TWM:** Students often forget to invert $W_2$ when it is negative (reversed pressure coil). KTU explicitly checks: *"Will any wattmeter show a reversed reading?"* — if you say "No" when $W_2$ is negative, you lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong Formula Variant:** Using $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$ instead of $(30° - \phi)$ (or vice versa) for the leading wattmeter. Always state the **reference wattmeter** clearly.
> 3. **Confusing Phase Error and Friction Error:** Many students interchange these two errors. **Phase error** is about the *phase angle* between fluxes; **friction error** is about *mechanical resistance*. The compensation method (shading band) is shared, but the purpose is different.
> 4. **Forgetting the Disc Speed Equation:** The derivation is incomplete without showing $T_d = T_b$ and hence $\omega_m \propto P$. KTU deducts 1 mark if this is missing.
> 5. **Mixing up Creep Prevention Methods:** The two holes are for creep; the shading band is for phase and friction compensation. Do NOT swap them.
> 6. **Star/Delta Assumption in TWM Problems:** The formula $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ is valid for **any** connection. But for computing per-phase impedance, you must state whether the load is star or delta. KTU explicitly tests this.
> 7. **Missing the 3600 × 1000 conversion:** The meter constant is in **rev/kWh**, not rev/Wh. Forgetting to convert leads to a 10³ error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Two-Wattmeter Method — Key Points
- ☐ **Blondel's Theorem** is the foundation: $n - 1$ wattmeters for an $n$-wire system.
- ☐ Formula: $P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ (valid for **balanced and unbalanced** loads).
- ☐ Formula: $Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$.
- ☐ Power factor: $\cos\phi = \dfrac{W_1 + W_2}{2 V_L I_L}$ (or via the $\tan\phi$ formula).
- ☐ For **lagging** p.f. < 0.5 ($\phi > 60°$), $W_2$ is **negative** — pressure coil must be reversed.
- ☐ For **leading** load, $W_1 < W_2$ (opposite of lagging case for the same p.f.).
- ☐ At unity p.f., $W_1 = W_2$. At zero p.f. (pure reactor), $W_1 = -W_2$, $P = 0$.
- ☐ **Cannot be used for 3-phase, 4-wire system** with significant neutral current.

### Induction Energy Meter — Key Points
- ☐ **Five sub-systems:** Driving, Moving, Controlling, Registering, Compensating.
- ☐ Operating principle: **Two fluxes in quadrature** (90° apart) interact with eddy currents.
- ☐ Driving torque: $T_d = K_d \phi_1 \phi_2 \sin\alpha$. When $\alpha = 90°$, $T_d \propto P$.
- ☐ Braking torque: $T_b = K_b \phi_m^2 \omega_m$. At equilibrium, $\omega_m \propto P$.
- ☐ **Number of revolutions $\propto$ Energy consumed (kWh)**.
- ☐ **Phase error** is compensated by the **shading band** (lag adjustment).
- ☐ **Friction error** is compensated by the **shading band** (light-load adjustment) — same component, different purpose.
- ☐ **Creep** is prevented by **two holes in the disc** (and/or a creep stopper iron piece).
- ☐ **Temperature error** is compensated by a **Ni-Cu shunt** on the permanent magnet.
- ☐ **Over-voltage error** is limited by a **magnetic shunt**.
- ☐ The meter is a **kWh meter**, not a kVAh or kVArh meter (it integrates **active power only**).
- ☐ Standard meter constant range: **500 to 1000 rev/kWh** for domestic meters.

### Common KTU 2024 Question Patterns
- ☐ Derivation of $W_1$, $W_2$ expressions (5–7 marks).
- ☐ Numerical: Given $W_1$, $W_2$, find $P$, $Q$, $\cos\phi$, $I_L$ (7 marks).
- ☐ Special case: $W_2 = 0$ implies p.f. = 0.5; $W_1 = W_2$ implies p.f. = 1.0.
- ☐ Construction and working of energy meter (5–7 marks).
- ☐ List of errors and their compensations (3–5 marks).
- ☐ Creep definition + prevention (2–3 marks).
- ☐ Driving torque derivation (5–7 marks).

### Mnemonic for Quick Recall
> **"W BOTH — SUM is Real, DIFF gives Reactive (with √3 multiplier) → tan φ"**
> **"Phase error = Shading band, Friction error = Shading band, Creep = Two Holes"**
> **"Series flux with I, Shunt flux with V, 90° between, Disc rotates"**
> **"Permanent magnet brakes, Gear train counts, kWh is the answer"**

---

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Two-Element Energy Meter (3-Phase, 3-Wire Energy Meter)

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Two-Element Energy Meter** is a precision integrating instrument used to measure total active (or reactive) energy consumed in a **3-phase, 3-wire system**. It is constructed by mechanically coupling **two single-phase induction-type watt-hour meter elements** on a common spindle, with a common braking (aluminum disc) system and a single cyclometer register. The total power/energy is the **algebraic sum** of the two individual element readings, based on the **Two-Wattmeter Method** of Blondel's Theorem.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Imagine two separate water flow meters (the "elements") placed in two different pipes, both feeding into a common large tank. Each meter measures the flow in its pipe independently. Now, mount both meters' rotating discs on the **same common shaft** (spindle) and attach **one single odometer-style counter** to record the combined rotation. Whatever the total count shows, that is the total water passed. The two-element energy meter works exactly like this — each element "sees" its phase current and voltage, produces a torque proportional to its local power, and the two torques act **together on a common spindle** to drive a single counter that displays total energy in **kWh**.

### Key Features (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight)
- **Two driving systems** acting on a common aluminum disc (or two separate discs on a common spindle)
- **Single braking magnet** (permanent magnet) for the whole assembly
- **Single registering mechanism** (cyclometer type)
- Operates on the principle of the **Two-Wattmeter Method** ($P_T = P_1 + P_2$)
- Used in **3-phase, 3-wire** systems (no neutral required)
- Range typically **5 A, 250–500 V** per phase
- Available in both **single-disc and double-disc** constructions

---

## 1.2 Instrument Transformers — Current Transformer (CT) and Potential Transformer (PT)

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Instrument Transformers** are precision magnetic-core transformers used to extend the range of AC measuring instruments (ammeters, voltmeters, wattmeters, energy meters) and to **electrically isolate** the measuring circuit from the high-voltage/high-current power circuit. The two principal types are:
> 1. **Current Transformer (CT)** — a *step-down* current transformer (primary current is much larger than secondary current).
> 2. **Potential Transformer (PT)** — a *step-down* voltage transformer (primary voltage is much larger than secondary voltage).

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Think of a CT as a **"current reducer"** — like a gear reduction in a mechanical gearbox. The huge current flowing in the power line is "geared down" to a small, safe, standard value (usually **5 A** secondary) so a normal small ammeter can read it. Similarly, a PT is a **"voltage reducer"** — like a step-down transformer — that brings dangerously high line voltages (say **11 kV**) down to a safe, standard **110 V** secondary, so a small voltmeter can measure them. In both cases, the meter's reading × the transformer's ratio gives the actual primary quantity.

### Range Extension Concept (Description Only)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Range Extension (Description Only)** is the technique of using a CT or PT to make a low-range measuring instrument measure quantities far beyond its direct (internal) rating. For example, a **5 A, 110 V** watt-hour meter fitted with a **100/5 A CT** and a **11000/110 V PT** can measure the energy of an **11 kV, 100 A** line. The meter's own internal coils never see the dangerous line values — they only see the safe standardized secondary values of the CT/PT. This enables a **single low-range instrument** to serve an extremely wide range of primary measurements simply by changing the CT/PT ratio.

---

## 1.3 Basic Principles of Electronic (Digital) Multimeter

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> An **Electronic Multimeter** (commonly a **Digital Multimeter / DMM**) is a multi-function measuring instrument that uses **active electronic circuits** (op-amps, rectifiers, attenuators, A/D converters, microcontrollers) to measure voltage, current, and resistance — and displays the result on a **digital numerical readout** (LCD/LED). It replaces the mechanical moving-coil movement of analog meters with a high-impedance electronic front-end and a digital display.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
A traditional analog multimeter uses a delicate mechanical "needle-and-spring" mechanism that physically deflects proportional to the current flowing through it. An **electronic multimeter** removes all moving parts at the input. The signal is first **conditioned** (amplified/attenuated/rectified), then **digitized** by an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), and finally the **digital number** is shown on a screen. Think of it as the difference between an **old weighing scale with a moving pointer** and a **modern digital bathroom scale** that shows "72.5 kg" on an LCD — the principle of measuring force/weight is the same, but the way the answer is presented is far more precise, faster, and easier to read.

### Block-Level Intuition
- **Input** (V, I, or Ω) → **Signal Conditioner** (range, amplification, rectification) → **ADC** (converts analog to digital) → **Display Driver** → **Numerical Display (LCD)**
- Standard secondary of a CT is always **5 A** (IEC/IS standard)
- Standard secondary of a PT is always **110 V** (or 110/√3 V for phase-to-neutral)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Key Standardization Reminder**
> - CT secondary standard current: **5 A**
> - PT secondary standard voltage: **110 V** (line-to-line) or **110/√3 V** (line-to-neutral)
> - Energy meter standard: **Class 1.0 / Class 0.5** accuracy per IS 722 / IEC 62053
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two-Element Energy Meter — Operating Theory

### Why Two Elements?
A 3-phase, 3-wire system (e.g., R-Y-B without neutral) has **no return path**, so instantaneous power in any conductor cannot be measured directly. **Blondel's Theorem** states that the total power in an *n*-wire system can be measured using *(n − 1)* wattmeters. For a 3-wire system, this means **2 wattmeters are sufficient**, and that is exactly what a two-element meter packages into one unit.

### Constructional Details
| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Two Current Coils (CC)** | Carry line currents $I_R$ and $I_Y$ (R-phase and Y-phase) — or any two line currents |
| **Two Pressure Coils (PC)** | Connected across line voltages; one across $V_{RY}$ and other across $V_{YB}$ |
| **Aluminum Disc (1 or 2)** | Spinning rotor; eddy currents induced in it interact with flux to produce torque |
| **Permanent Magnet (Braking)** | Provides braking torque proportional to disc speed (for correct integration) |
| **Spindle** | Common shaft carrying both discs and the cyclometer pinion |
| **Cyclometer Register** | Counts revolutions and displays **kWh** |
| **Compensating Coil / Power Factor Adjuster** | Corrects for low-power-factor errors |

### Operating Principle
- Each element independently produces a driving torque $T_{d1} \propto V_{RY} I_R \cos(\phi_{RY})$ and $T_{d2} \propto V_{YB} I_Y \cos(\phi_{YB})$.
- Both torques act on a **common spindle** (or share flux through a common disc), so they **add algebraically**.
- Total driving torque: $T_d = T_{d1} + T_{d2} \propto P_1 + P_2 = P_{3\phi}$ (total 3-phase power).
- The permanent magnet provides braking torque $T_b \propto N$ (speed).
- At steady state: $T_d = T_b$, so $N \propto P$, hence **number of revolutions $\propto$ energy consumed**.

### Advantages of Two-Element Construction
- **Compact** — one housing, one register, one terminal block
- **Lower cost** than two separate single-phase meters
- **Common braking** gives uniform braking torque → no inter-element interference
- **Phantom loading** is easier — only one test circuit needed
- **No neutral required** — works on 3-phase, 3-wire systems

### Important Torque Equation
For a balanced load at power factor $\cos\phi$ and a phase angle of $30°$ between line and phase voltages, the **torque per element** of a two-element meter (referring to total meter) is:

$$T = \frac{V_L I_L \cos\phi}{2} = \text{torque contribution from one element at unity PF}$$

For **balanced load** the two element torques are **equal**. For **unbalanced load** they differ — the algebraic sum still correctly represents total power.

---

## 2.2 Current Transformer (CT) — Deep Theory

### Construction
- **Primary winding:** Very few turns (often just **a single bar / straight conductor passing through the core**) — heavy gauge.
- **Secondary winding:** Many turns of fine enameled copper wire.
- **Core:** High-permeability silicon steel (or **mumetal / nanocrystalline** for precision CTs) — operates at low flux density to keep core loss and magnetizing current low.
- **Secondary burden:** Connected to ammeter (or wattmeter current coil + energy meter + relays).

### Polarity Convention
- **Primary current** enters at the **P1** (or **H1**) terminal and leaves from **P2** (**H2**).
- **Secondary current** leaves from **S1** (**K1**) when primary enters P1.
- P1 and S1 are of the **same polarity (instantaneous)**.

### Phasor Diagram & Ratio
- $I_1$ = primary current; $I_2$ = secondary current; $I_0$ = exciting (magnetizing) current.
- By ampere-turn balance (ideal): $N_1 I_1 = N_2 I_2$ → $\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = K_n$ (turns ratio = nominal ratio).

### Why CT Must NEVER Have Open Secondary
> [!WARNING]
> If the CT secondary is opened while primary current is flowing, the demagnetizing MMF of $N_2 I_2$ disappears, so the entire primary MMF $N_1 I_1$ becomes **magnetizing MMF**. This drives the core into deep saturation, producing:
> 1. **Dangerously high secondary voltage** (several kV) — lethal hazard.
> 2. **Severe core saturation** → high flux density → excessive core heating.
> 3. **Permanent damage** to insulation and accuracy.
> **Always short-circuit the CT secondary before disconnecting the meter.**

### Range Extension with CT
A standard ammeter rated **0–5 A** can be used to measure any primary current $I_1$ simply by selecting a CT with appropriate nominal ratio $K_n = I_{1,\text{rated}} / 5$. The ammeter reading $\times K_n$ = primary current.

---

## 2.3 Potential Transformer (PT) — Deep Theory

### Construction
- **Primary winding:** Many turns of fine wire, connected across the high-voltage line.
- **Secondary winding:** Few turns, connected to voltmeter (or wattmeter PC + relays).
- **Core:** High-permeability steel, designed for low magnetizing current.
- Similar in construction to a small **step-down power transformer**, but optimized for **measurement accuracy** over a small burden range.

### Phasor Diagram & Ratio
- $V_1$ = primary voltage; $V_2$ = secondary voltage.
- **Nominal ratio:** $K_n = V_{1,\text{rated}} / V_{2,\text{rated}}$ (e.g., 11000/110 = 100).
- **Turns ratio:** $N_1 / N_2 \approx K_n$.

### Range Extension with PT
A standard voltmeter rated **0–110 V** (or **0–150 V**) can be used to measure any line voltage $V_1$ by selecting a PT with appropriate $K_n$. The voltmeter reading $\times K_n$ = primary voltage.

---

## 2.4 Electronic (Digital) Multimeter — Deep Theory

### Basic Block Diagram (in words)
1. **Function/Range Selector Switch** — chooses V, mA, Ω, etc. and selects proper attenuator/shunt.
2. **Input Attenuator / Shunt Network** — scales input to within ADC range.
3. **AC-to-DC Converter (Rectifier + Filter)** — for AC measurements (true-RMS or average-responding).
4. **Ohm-to-Voltage Converter** — applies known current through unknown resistance, measures $V = IR$.
5. **Current-to-Voltage Converter (Shunt + Op-Amp)** — for current ranges.
6. **A/D Converter (ADC)** — typically **dual-slope integrating ADC** (for high noise rejection) or **sigma-delta ADC**.
7. **Display Driver + LCD** — shows numerical value with auto-decimal-point placement.
8. **Microcontroller** — auto-ranging logic, RMS calculation, range switching.

### Key Specifications
- **Input impedance (DC V mode):** typically **10 MΩ** (vs. ~20 kΩ/Volt for analog) — much higher, so less loading error.
- **Resolution:** 3½ digit (1999 counts), 4½ digit (19999 counts), or higher.
- **Accuracy:** ±(0.05% rdg + 2 digits) typical for 4½-digit DMM.
- **Common mode / Normal mode rejection** due to integrating ADC.

### Measurement Modes
| Mode | Underlying Principle |
|---|---|
| **DC Voltage** | Voltage divider attenuator → ADC |
| **AC Voltage** | Attenuator → True-RMS converter (or average-responding with form-factor correction) → ADC |
| **DC Current** | Internal shunt resistor → I-to-V → ADC |
| **AC Current** | Shunt + AC-DC converter → ADC |
| **Resistance** | Constant current source → measure $V = IR$ → ADC |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Blondel / 2-Wattmeter Total Power | $P_{3\phi} = P_1 + P_2 = V_{RY} I_R \cos\phi_1 + V_{YB} I_Y \cos\phi_2$ | Watts; $\phi_1, \phi_2$ are phase angles of each element |
| 2 | Two-Element Meter: Balanced PF Reading (each) | $P_1 = P_2 = V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | For balanced load only |
| 3 | Energy Registered | $E = \int P \, dt \approx N \times K_h$ | kWh; $K_h$ = meter constant (rev/kWh) |
| 4 | Meter Constant | $K_h = \dfrac{\text{No. of revolutions}}{\text{Energy in kWh}}$ | rev/kWh |
| 5 | CT Nominal (Turns) Ratio | $K_n = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{I_{1,\text{rated}}}{I_{2,\text{rated}}}$ | Dimensionless; $I_{2,\text{rated}} = 5$ A standard |
| 6 | CT Ratio Error | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{K_n - R}{R} \times 100\%$ | $R$ = actual ratio $I_1/I_2$ |
| 7 | CT Primary Current (Range Ext.) | $I_1 = I_2 \times K_n$ | Amperes |
| 8 | PT Nominal (Turns) Ratio | $K_n = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_{1,\text{rated}}}{V_{2,\text{rated}}}$ | $V_{2,\text{rated}} = 110$ V standard |
| 9 | PT Primary Voltage (Range Ext.) | $V_1 = V_2 \times K_n$ | Volts |
| 10 | PT Ratio Error | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{K_n - R}{R} \times 100\%$ | $R = V_1/V_2$ actual |
| 11 | Phase Angle Error (CT/PT) | $\theta = \angle (V_2 / I_2) - \angle (V_1 / I_1)$ | Minutes of arc |
| 12 | Induction Meter Driving Torque | $T_d = k \cdot \phi_v \phi_i \cos\phi = k \cdot P$ | N·m |
| 13 | Braking Torque | $T_b = K_b \cdot N$ | N·m; $K_b$ = braking constant |
| 14 | Steady-State Speed | $N = \dfrac{k}{K_b} \cdot P$ | rad/s |
| 15 | DMM Input Impedance | $Z_{in} = 10 \text{ M}\Omega$ (DC V) | vs. ~ 20 kΩ/V analog |
| 16 | Burden of CT (rated) | $Z_b$ (e.g., 2.5 VA, 5 VA, 10 VA, 15 VA) | VA; total secondary burden including leads + meter |
| 17 | Burden of PT (rated) | $Z_b$ in VA on secondary side | VA; limits connected instruments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember**: For an *ideal* CT, $N_1 I_1 = N_2 I_2$ exactly. In a *real* CT, a small portion of primary MMF is used to magnetize the core ($I_0$), which causes both **ratio error** and **phase-angle error**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Power in a Two-Element Meter (Blondel's Theorem for 3-Phase, 3-Wire)

### Given
A 3-phase, 3-wire system with line voltages $V_{RY}, V_{YB}, V_{BR}$ and line currents $I_R, I_Y, I_B$.

### Assumption
KCL at the load node gives: $I_R + I_Y + I_B = 0$ (no neutral conductor).

### Derivation
The instantaneous total power delivered to the 3-phase load is:

$$p(t) = v_{RN}(t) \, i_R(t) + v_{YN}(t) \, i_Y(t) + v_{BN}(t) \, i_B(t)$$

Since there is **no neutral**, $v_{RN}$ etc. are measured with respect to an **artificial neutral N'** (false neutral formed by the two pressure coils' common point). Taking the line-to-line voltage differences instead:

$$v_{RN'} = v_{RY} - v_{BN'}, \quad v_{YN'} = v_{YB} - v_{BN'}, \quad v_{BN'} = v_{BN'}$$

Substituting into the power expression and using $i_R + i_Y + i_B = 0$:

$$p(t) = v_{RY} \, i_R(t) + v_{YB} \, i_Y(t)$$

Averaging over a complete cycle gives the **average active power**:

$$P_{3\phi} = V_{RY} I_R \cos(\phi_{RY}) + V_{YB} I_Y \cos(\phi_{YB})$$

where $\phi_{XY}$ is the angle between $V_{XY}$ and the chosen line current.

### Conclusion
**Two wattmeter readings $P_1$ and $P_2$ are sufficient — and necessary — to determine the total 3-phase power in a 3-wire system.** This is exactly what a two-element energy meter implements mechanically.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CT Transformation Ratio and Ampere-Turn Balance

### Given
A CT with $N_1$ primary turns and $N_2$ secondary turns. Primary current $I_1$ and secondary current $I_2$ both produce MMFs on the core.

### Step 1 — MMF balance (ideal)
In an ideal transformer, the net MMF on the core is zero (no net flux change required to support the secondary current — counter-MMF exactly balances primary MMF):

$$N_1 I_1' = N_2 I_2$$

where $I_1'$ is the portion of $I_1$ actually transformed (i.e., $I_1' = I_1 - I_0$, with $I_0$ = exciting current).

### Step 2 — Nominal ratio definition
In practice, CTs are designed so that the **nominal ratio** equals the turns ratio:

$$K_n = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

### Step 3 — Actual ratio
From step 1:

$$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{I_0}{I_1}}$$

For small $I_0/I_1$:

$$R = \frac{I_1}{I_2} \approx K_n \left(1 + \frac{I_0}{I_1}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Ratio error

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{K_n - R}{R} = -\frac{I_0/I_1}{1 + I_0/I_1} \approx -\frac{I_0}{I_1}$$

The negative sign indicates the **actual ratio $R$ is larger than nominal** (i.e., secondary current is slightly less than expected).

### Step 5 — Numerical example
A 200/5 A CT ($K_n = 40$). At full primary current $I_1 = 200$ A, the magnetizing current $I_0 = 0.4$ A. Find ratio error.

$$R = K_n \left(1 + \frac{I_0}{I_1}\right) = 40 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.4}{200}\right) = 40 \times 1.002 = 40.08$$

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{40 - 40.08}{40.08} \times 100\% = -0.1996\% \approx -0.2\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A ratio error of −0.2% means the CT secondary delivers slightly *less* current than nominal → the meter reading must be *corrected upward* by 0.2% to obtain the true primary current.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Speed & Energy in a Two-Element Induction Energy Meter

### Given
A two-element energy meter with each element producing torque $T_{d,i} = k \cdot V_i I_i \cos\phi_i$ (i = 1, 2), a common braking magnet with $T_b = K_b \cdot N$.

### Step 1 — Total driving torque
$$T_d = T_{d,1} + T_{d,2} = k(V_1 I_1 \cos\phi_1 + V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2) = k \cdot P_{3\phi}$$

### Step 2 — At steady state, $T_d = T_b$
$$k \cdot P_{3\phi} = K_b \cdot N$$

### Step 3 — Solve for disc speed
$$N = \frac{k}{K_b} \cdot P_{3\phi}$$

So **speed is directly proportional to total power**.

### Step 4 — Energy over time interval $T$
$$E = \int_0^T P_{3\phi} \, dt = \int_0^T \frac{K_b}{k} N \, dt = \frac{K_b}{k} \cdot N_{rev} \cdot 2\pi \cdot (\text{factor})$$

In practice, this reduces to the **meter-constant relation**:

$$E \text{ (kWh)} = \frac{N_{rev}}{K_h}$$

where $K_h$ (rev/kWh) is the **meter constant** stamped on every energy meter nameplate.

### Step 5 — Numerical example
A 2-element energy meter has $K_h = 1250$ rev/kWh. In 10 minutes, the disc makes 750 revolutions. Find energy consumed.

$$E = \frac{N_{rev}}{K_h} = \frac{750}{1250} = 0.6 \text{ kWh}$$

Average power:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{E}{t} = \frac{0.6 \text{ kWh}}{10/60 \text{ h}} = \frac{0.6 \times 60}{10} = 3.6 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Errors in Two-Element Meters

| Source of Error | Effect | Compensation Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Low power factor error** | Braking torque becomes significant relative to driving torque at low PF | Provide **shading band / power-factor adjuster** on pressure-coil flux |
| **Friction error** | Spindle friction causes slow start (creep) | **Light-load adjustment** (small holes on disc) |
| **Inter-element interference** | One element's flux affects the other | **Magnetic shielding** between elements; careful flux positioning |
| **Voltage drift** (over/under voltage) | Pressure-coil flux $\neq V$ | Operating range restricted (e.g., ±10% of rated $V$) |
| **Frequency drift** | Pressure-coil impedance $\propto f$ | Compensating winding in PC; wide-band design |
| **Temperature drift** | Disc resistance varies | Use **magnetic shunt** (temperature-compensated alloy) |
| **Creep (slow continuous rotation without load)** | Friction compensation over-compensates | **Two holes** on opposite sides of disc; or anti-creep holes |
| **Phase-angle error of CT/PT** (in metering) | False power reading | Use CT/PT of **Class 0.5** or better; **matched ratio and phase** |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem: Range Extension using CT and PT

### Problem
A single-phase watt-hour meter rated **5 A, 230 V** is to be used in a **2300 V, 100 A** line via a CT and a PT. Find the required CT and PT ratios and the meter constant correction factor.

### Step 1 — CT ratio
CT must step the line current from 100 A down to 5 A (the meter's rated current).

$$K_{n,CT} = \frac{I_{1,\text{rated}}}{I_{2,\text{rated}}} = \frac{100}{5} = 20$$

So use a **100/5 A CT** (nominal ratio 20).

### Step 2 — PT ratio
PT must step the line voltage from 2300 V down to 230 V (the meter's rated voltage).

$$K_{n,PT} = \frac{V_{1,\text{rated}}}{V_{2,\text{rated}}} = \frac{2300}{230} = 10$$

So use a **2300/230 V PT** (nominal ratio 10).

### Step 3 — Energy multiplication factor
The **actual energy** consumed on the line is:

$$E_{\text{line}} = K_{n,CT} \times K_{n,PT} \times E_{\text{meter}} = 20 \times 10 \times E_{\text{meter}} = 200 \times E_{\text{meter}}$$

So the meter reading must be **multiplied by 200** to obtain the true line energy.

### Step 4 — Verification
Line apparent power: $S_{\text{line}} = V \cdot I = 2300 \times 100 = 230,000 \text{ VA} = 230 \text{ kVA}$.
Meter-side apparent: $S_{\text{meter}} = 230 \times 5 = 1150 \text{ VA}$.
Ratio check: $230{,}000 / 1150 = 200$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Insight:** Examiners often award full marks for the multiplication factor $K = K_{n,CT} \times K_{n,PT}$ (also called the **register constant** or **meter multiplier**). Always state it explicitly.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**List any three advantages of a two-element energy meter over using two separate single-phase energy meters for measuring 3-phase, 3-wire energy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Compact construction** — Two driving systems share a common spindle and a common housing, saving space and cost compared to two separate meter units.
2. **Common braking system** — A single permanent-magnet brake provides uniform braking torque for the combined torque of both elements, eliminating inter-meter interactions and improving accuracy.
3. **Single registering mechanism** — A common cyclometer register directly displays the total 3-phase energy in kWh, so no manual addition of two meter readings is required; this also enables easy **phantom loading** during testing.

*(Each point = 1 mark)*

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]
**Why must the secondary of a Current Transformer (CT) never be open-circuited while the primary is energized?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
When a CT secondary is open, the demagnetizing MMF of the secondary ($N_2 I_2$) becomes zero. As a result, the **entire primary MMF ($N_1 I_1$) acts as magnetizing MMF**, driving the core deep into saturation. This produces three hazardous effects:
1. **Very high voltage** (several kV) across the secondary terminals — risk of insulation breakdown and electrocution.
2. **Excessive core heating** — increased hysteresis and eddy-current losses in the saturated core.
3. **Permanent loss of accuracy** — residual magnetism remains in the core even after the secondary is re-closed, causing ratio and phase-angle errors in all future measurements.

**Conclusion:** CT secondary terminals must always be **short-circuited** (or connected to a low-burden instrument) when not in use. *(1 mark for each valid point + ½ for conclusion = 3 marks)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse the nominal ratio $K_n$ with the actual ratio $R$** — many students write $I_1 = I_2 \times K_n$ without acknowledging ratio error. Always state the **assumed ideal ratio** and then apply the **correction factor** if asked.
> - In two-element meter questions, students often forget the **"common spindle + common brake"** construction details and lose 2–3 marks.
> - For CT/PT range-extension problems, the examiner specifically checks whether you have **multiplied the meter reading by $K_{n,CT} \times K_{n,PT}$**. Many candidates write only the CT or only the PT ratio.
> - In CT numericals, if a burden is given in **VA**, you must compute **secondary voltage = VA / I_2** and then compare to the voltage across the burden — this is a common 2-mark trap.
> - For DMM block diagrams, examiners often award marks only for the **specific functional block names** (attenuator, AC-DC converter, ADC, display) — vague labels like "processing unit" fetch zero.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat constructional diagram, explain the working of a **two-element energy meter** used for measuring energy in a 3-phase, 3-wire system. Show that the total power is the **algebraic sum of the two element powers**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 3-phase, 3-wire, 100 V, 50 Hz system supplies a balanced star-connected load of impedance $(8 + j6)\,\Omega$ per phase. A two-element energy meter is connected to measure the energy. The meter constant is **500 rev/kWh**. If the disc makes **600 revolutions in 6 minutes**, verify the meter reading. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
A two-element energy meter consists of:
- **Two current coils (CC):** mounted on the central limb of two separate electromagnets. They carry the line currents $I_R$ and $I_Y$ (R-phase and Y-phase line currents).
- **Two pressure coils (PC):** wound on the outer limbs of the two electromagnets and connected across the line voltages $V_{RY}$ and $V_{YB}$ respectively. These coils are highly inductive (large number of turns, connected in series with a high non-inductive resistance).
- **Aluminum disc (one or two):** mounted on a common vertical spindle. Each element induces eddy currents in the disc (or in a common disc), and the interaction with its own driving flux produces a torque.
- **Common permanent magnet:** provides braking torque proportional to disc speed.
- **Common cyclometer register:** geared to the spindle; displays the total energy in kWh.
- **Compensating devices:** light-load (friction) adjuster, power-factor (overload) adjuster, and creep-adjusting holes.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
Each element independently produces a driving torque proportional to the average power it senses:

$$T_{d,1} = k \cdot V_{RY} I_R \cos\phi_1, \quad T_{d,2} = k \cdot V_{YB} I_Y \cos\phi_2$$

These two torques are mechanically added (algebraically) on the common spindle:

$$T_d = T_{d,1} + T_{d,2} = k (P_1 + P_2) = k \cdot P_{3\phi}$$

The permanent magnet applies a braking torque $T_b = K_b \cdot N$. At steady state, $T_d = T_b$, so $N \propto P_{3\phi}$. The cyclometer integrates revolutions over time, displaying total energy in kWh.

**Proof of Algebraic Sum (1 mark):**
From Blondel's theorem (derived in §3.1 above) for a 3-wire system with $I_R + I_Y + I_B = 0$:

$$P_{3\phi} = V_{RY} I_R \cos\phi_1 + V_{YB} I_Y \cos\phi_2 = P_1 + P_2$$

Hence the algebraic sum of the two element readings gives total 3-phase power. ✓

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute phase current and power factor.**
Impedance per phase: $Z = \sqrt{8^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{64+36} = \sqrt{100} = 10\,\Omega$.

$$I_{ph} = \frac{V_{ph}}{Z} = \frac{100/\sqrt{3}}{10} = \frac{100}{10\sqrt{3}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 5.7735 \text{ A}$$

Power factor: $\cos\phi = R/Z = 8/10 = 0.8$ (lagging).

**Step 2 — Total 3-phase active power.**
$$P_{3\phi} = 3 \cdot I_{ph}^2 \cdot R = 3 \times \left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 \times 8 = 3 \times \frac{100}{3} \times 8 = 800 \text{ W} = 0.8 \text{ kW}$$

*(Alternative: $P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 100 \times 5.7735 \times 0.8 = 800$ W ✓)*

**Step 3 — Energy in 6 minutes.**
$$E = P_{3\phi} \times t = 0.8 \text{ kW} \times \frac{6}{60} \text{ h} = 0.8 \times 0.1 = 0.08 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Expected number of revolutions from meter constant.**
$$N_{expected} = E \times K_h = 0.08 \times 500 = 40 \text{ revolutions}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with observed reading.**
Observed revolutions = **600**, Expected = 40. The observed reading is **15×** the expected — i.e., the meter is **reading 15 times too high**, or the meter constant is **7500 rev/kWh** (mismatch). 

**Verification conclusion:** Either the meter is faulty / mis-calibrated, or the **meter constant is actually 7500 rev/kWh** (not 500 as stated in the problem). The student should explicitly state which interpretation is adopted. *(1 mark for correct conclusion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the **principle of operation of a Current Transformer (CT)** and a **Potential Transformer (PT)**. State clearly how they are used for **range extension** of measuring instruments. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **100/5 A CT** and a **11000/110 V PT** are used in conjunction with a **5 A, 110 V** watt-hour meter to measure the energy in a **3-phase, 11 kV, 100 A** line. The meter registers **0.5 kWh** in a given period. Calculate:
- (i) the actual energy consumed in the line, and
- (ii) the average power drawn by the load. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Current Transformer (CT) — Principle (2 marks):**
A CT is a **step-down transformer** in which the primary winding (carrying the line current to be measured) has **very few turns** — often just a single straight bar passing through the core. The secondary winding has **many turns** of fine wire and is connected across an ammeter (or wattmeter current coil). Because the secondary must deliver rated current (typically 5 A) even when primary current is small, the turns ratio $N_2 / N_1$ is large. The ammeter reads the secondary current $I_2$, and the **actual primary current is** $I_1 = K_n \times I_2 = (N_2/N_1) \times I_2$.

**Potential Transformer (PT) — Principle (2 marks):**
A PT is a **step-down transformer** in which the primary winding (connected across the high-voltage line) has **many turns**, and the secondary winding has **few turns**. The secondary delivers a standardized low voltage (110 V line-to-line or 110/√3 V line-to-neutral), and a voltmeter is connected across the secondary. The **actual primary voltage is** $V_1 = K_n \times V_2 = (N_1/N_2) \times V_2$.

**Range Extension — Description Only (3 marks):**
- **For Current:** A standard ammeter (0–5 A) can be used to measure any primary current up to thousands of amps simply by selecting a CT of the appropriate nominal ratio (e.g., 100/5, 500/5, 1000/5). The CT **isolates the instrument from the high-voltage line** and **scales down the current** to a safe, measurable value.
- **For Voltage:** A standard voltmeter (0–110 V) can measure any line voltage up to hundreds of kV simply by selecting a PT of the appropriate ratio (e.g., 11 kV/110 V, 33 kV/110 V). The PT **isolates the instrument** from the high-voltage line and **scales down the voltage** to a safe, measurable value.
- **Combined with Energy Meters:** When both CT and PT are used, the meter is connected to the **safe secondary** of both, and the meter reading is **multiplied by the product $K_{n,CT} \times K_{n,PT}$** to obtain the true line energy. This is called the **register multiplier** or **meter multiplier**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — CT and PT ratios.**
$$K_{n,CT} = \frac{100}{5} = 20, \quad K_{n,PT} = \frac{11000}{110} = 100$$

**Step 2 — Register multiplier.**
$$K_{register} = K_{n,CT} \times K_{n,PT} = 20 \times 100 = 2000$$

**Step 3 — (i) Actual energy consumed.**
$$E_{line} = K_{register} \times E_{meter} = 2000 \times 0.5 = 1000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Average power.**

The time period is not given in the problem. We compute the **apparent power** reference first:
$$S_{line} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L = \sqrt{3} \times 11{,}000 \times 100 \approx 1{,}905{,}256 \text{ VA} \approx 1905.3 \text{ kVA}$$

If we **assume a measurement time of 1 hour** (the most common assumption in KTU problems unless stated otherwise), then the average power drawn equals the energy per hour:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_{line}}{t} = \frac{1000 \text{ kWh}}{1 \text{ h}} = 1000 \text{ kW} = 1 \text{ MW}$$

*(If a different time period is given in the actual exam paper, replace 1 h with that value. The student should always state the assumption clearly.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note for Part (b):** A common mistake is to compute the **apparent power** ($1905.3$ kVA) and call it the active power. Apparent power is $V \cdot I \cdot \sqrt{3}$ regardless of PF. Active power requires the energy data and the time period.

---

# 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

## Two-Element Energy Meter
- Used for **3-phase, 3-wire** systems (no neutral needed).
- Based on **Blondel's Theorem**: total power = algebraic sum of two element powers.
- Construction: **two driving systems + one braking magnet + one register + one common spindle**.
- **Single-disc and double-disc** types exist.
- Meter constant $K_h$ relates revolutions to energy: $E = N_{rev} / K_h$ (kWh).
- Critical errors: **low-PF error, friction error, inter-element interference, creep** — all compensated by adjusting devices in the meter housing.

## Current Transformer (CT)
- **Step-down** current transformer; $N_1 I_1 \approx N_2 I_2$.
- **Secondary standardized at 5 A**; primary rating can be 50 A, 100 A, 500 A, 1000 A, etc.
- Range extension: ammeter reading $\times K_n$ = primary current.
- **CT secondary must NEVER be open-circuited** while primary is energized → high voltage hazard and core saturation damage.
- Two main errors: **ratio error** (magnitude) and **phase-angle error** (minutes of arc).
- Polarity: P1 and S1 are of the same instantaneous polarity.

## Potential Transformer (PT)
- **Step-down** voltage transformer; $V_1 / V_2 \approx N_1 / N_2$.
- **Secondary standardized at 110 V** (or 110/√3 V for phase-to-neutral).
- Range extension: voltmeter reading $\times K_n$ = primary voltage.
- Similar ratio error and phase-angle error structure to CT.
- Burden in VA must not exceed rated value, or accuracy drops.

## Range Extension (Description Only)
- Combine CT + PT + standard meter → measure HV/EHV line quantities with a low-voltage instrument.
- **Register Multiplier = $K_{n,CT} \times K_{n,PT}$**.
- Benefits: **isolation, safety, cost reduction, standardization**.

## Electronic (Digital) Multimeter
- **No moving parts** at the input → high reliability, low loading.
- **Block diagram:** Attenuator/Shunt → Signal Conditioner (AC-DC converter / ohm-to-V / I-to-V) → ADC → Display.
- Uses **dual-slope integrating ADC** (high noise rejection) or **sigma-delta ADC** (high resolution).
- **Input impedance ≈ 10 MΩ** in DC V mode — far higher than analog (~20 kΩ/V).
- **Auto-ranging**, **True-RMS**, **diode test**, **continuity buzzer**, **capacitance**, **frequency** are common features in modern DMMs.
- Resolution: 3½, 4½, 5½, 6½ digits (e.g., 19999 counts for 4½-digit).

## 🔑 Quick-Recall Table

| Instrument | Standard Secondary | Range Extension Tool | Critical Safety Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CT** | 5 A | $I_1 = I_2 \times K_n$ | **Never open-circuit secondary** |
| **PT** | 110 V | $V_1 = V_2 \times K_n$ | Burden must be within VA rating |
| **2-Element Meter** | 5 A / 110 V per phase | $E_{line} = E_{meter} \times K_{CT} \times K_{PT}$ | Common spindle — single disc alignment critical |
| **DMM** | Direct digital read-out | Internal auto-ranging | Observe max input voltage/current ratings |

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Two elements for 3-wire, CT for current, PT for voltage, DMM for digital readout — and always short-circuit a CT secondary!"*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Voltmeter. Digital Energy Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT411 - ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENT AND MEASURING INSTRUMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Digital Voltmeter. Digital Energy Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Digital Voltmeter (DVM) — Formal Definition

A **Digital Voltmeter (DVM)** is an electronic measuring instrument that converts an **analog input voltage** into a **discrete digital output**, which is then displayed numerically on an LED/LCD readout. It employs an internal **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** as its heart, enabling direct, automatic, and high-precision measurement of DC and AC voltages without the parallax and resolution limitations of analog (pointer-type) meters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must master the **block diagram, working principle, and waveform analysis** of at least two DVM types — namely the **Ramp-type (Staircase / Linear-ramp)** and the **Dual-Slope Integrating** DVM. Comparison with Successive Approximation type is also commonly examined.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Numbered Ruler"

Imagine trying to measure the length of a table with a **rubber measuring tape** (analog meter) — you squint at the needle and guess it's "about 72 cm." Now replace that with a **rigid, finely-printed ruler with millimeter marks** (DVM). The DVM doesn't stretch or guess; it **physically compares** the unknown voltage against a known, ultra-stable reference (an internal precision voltage source) and **counts how many tiny digital "steps"** fit in. The finer the steps (more bits in the ADC), the more accurate the reading.

- **Parallax error** = 0 (no needle, no eye-position error)
- **Reading speed** = instant (digital latch)
- **Interface friendly** = can drive printers, computers, and PLCs directly

### 1.3 Digital Energy Meter — Formal Definition

A **Digital Energy Meter** (also called **Static Energy Meter** or **Electronic Energy Meter**) is a solid-state instrument that measures **active electrical energy** consumed by a load over a period of time, expressed in **kilowatt-hours (kWh)**. It replaces the older electromechanical induction-type meter by performing instantaneous multiplication of voltage and current waveforms, integrating the result digitally using a **time-base (V/f converter)** and **counter**, and displaying the cumulative energy on a digital display.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Static"?** KTU uses the term *static energy meter* to distinguish it from the *electromechanical (induction)* meter. "Static" implies no moving parts — all processing is done by **electronic multipliers (Hall sensors, Time-Division Multipliers, or A/D + Microcontroller)**.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bill Calculator"

Picture your home water usage: instead of a spinning disk that whirls faster with more flow, a digital energy meter is like a **smart flow computer**:
- It **samples** voltage (pressure) and current (flow rate) thousands of times per second.
- It **multiplies** them instantly (P = V × I × cos φ).
- It **adds up** the power over time (Energy = Power × Time).
- The **total** is shown on a digital register — just like a digital odometer in a car.

This is why tampering is harder, accuracy is class 0.5 or 0.2 (vs. 2.0 for old meters), and remote reading via **AMR (Automated Meter Reading)** becomes possible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block Diagram of a Generic DVM

A DVM, regardless of type, consists of the following functional blocks:

| # | Block | Function |
|---|-------|----------|
| 1 | **Input Attenuator / Amplifier** | Scales the unknown voltage to a range acceptable by the ADC |
| 2 | **Comparator / Integrator** | Core conversion element (type-specific) |
| 3 | **Reference Voltage Source (Vref)** | Ultra-stable zener-based DC reference (e.g., 10.000 V) |
| 4 | **Clock & Gating Circuit** | Provides time-base pulses for the counter |
| 5 | **Counter (BCD / Binary)** | Counts clock pulses proportional to input |
| 6 | **Decoder & Display** | Converts count to 7-segment LED/LCD readout |
| 7 | **Range Selector** | Switches full-scale ranges (200 mV, 2 V, 20 V, 200 V, 1000 V) |

---

### 2.2 Type I — Ramp-Type (Linear-Ramp / Staircase) DVM

**Working Principle (Step-by-step):**

1. The **START pulse** initiates a **negative-going linear ramp voltage** generated by an integrator.
2. Simultaneously, the ramp voltage is applied to one input of a **comparator**; the **unknown DC input voltage Vin** is applied to the other input.
3. The comparator output is **HIGH** as long as ramp > Vin, and **switches LOW** the moment the ramp equals Vin.
4. The time between START and the comparator switching is **directly proportional to Vin**:
   
   $$T = \frac{V_{in}}{\text{Slope of Ramp}}$$

5. During this interval, a **gating circuit** allows clock pulses to pass to the counter.
6. The counter's reading is **decoded and displayed** as the digital value of Vin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Waveform Insight (KTU favourite):** Draw the negative ramp and mark the intersection point with Vin. The horizontal distance (ΔT) from START to intersection = measurement. Examiners often ask to *sketch and label* this waveform.

**Disadvantage:** Susceptible to noise; ramp linearity depends on op-amp quality. Now largely obsolete in favor of dual-slope and SAR types.

---

### 2.3 Type II — Dual-Slope Integrating DVM (Industry Standard)

**Why it's preferred:** Excellent **noise rejection** (integrates over a full line cycle), high accuracy, low cost.

**Working Principle — Two Phases:**

**Phase 1 — RUN-UP (Signal Integration):**
- The unknown input voltage **Vin** is switched to the integrator for a **fixed time T1**.
- Output of integrator ramps **up** (or down) linearly to a peak value proportional to Vin.

$$V_{A} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{T_1} V_{in}\, dt = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC}$$

**Phase 2 — RUN-DOWN (Reference Integration):**
- A **known reference voltage Vref** of **opposite polarity** is applied to the integrator.
- The output ramps **back to zero** in time **T2**, which is measured by a counter.

$$0 = V_{A} + \left(-\frac{1}{RC}\right)\int_{0}^{T_2} (-V_{ref})\, dt$$

$$0 = -\frac{V_{in} T_1}{RC} + \frac{V_{ref} T_2}{RC}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}\,}$$

Since T1 is fixed and Vref is constant, **Vin is directly proportional to T2** — which is what the counter measures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam Pearl:** The **R and C components cancel out** because they appear in both phases. This makes dual-slope DVMs **immune to component drift, temperature effects, and clock frequency changes over short intervals** — explaining their use in precision digital multimeters.

---

### 2.4 Type III — Successive Approximation (SAR) DVM — Quick Overview

- Uses an internal **DAC** and a **single comparator**.
- Performs a **binary search** to find the closest digital code to Vin.
- **Conversion time is fixed** (n clock cycles for n-bit ADC) — very fast.
- Used in **high-speed data acquisition**, oscilloscopes, and DMMs.
- KTU may ask for a **comparison table** between DVM types.

---

### 2.5 Digital (Static) Energy Meter — Block Diagram & Working

**Functional Blocks:**

| # | Block | Function |
|---|-------|----------|
| 1 | **Voltage Sensor (V-Sensor)** | Potential divider / PT gives scaled-down voltage waveform |
| 2 | **Current Sensor (I-Sensor)** | Current shunt / CT / Hall-effect sensor gives scaled current waveform |
| 3 | **Multiplier** | Computes instantaneous power p(t) = v(t) × i(t) |
| 4 | **V/f Converter** | Converts analog power signal to a **frequency pulse train** |
| 5 | **Counter / Accumulator** | Counts pulses over time → total energy |
| 6 | **Display (LCD/LED)** | Shows kWh with decimal (e.g., 00427.8) |
| 7 | **Microcontroller (modern)** | Samples v(t) and i(t) via ADC, computes digitally |

**Two Implementations:**

**(a) Time-Division Multiplier (TDM) — analog multiplier inside:**
- Pulse-width modulation × Pulse-amplitude modulation
- Output pulses whose **average value = instantaneous power**

**(b) A/D + Microcontroller (modern digital meter):**
- Voltage and current sampled by dual ADCs
- Microcontroller computes:
  
  $$E = \sum_{k=1}^{N} v(k) \cdot i(k) \cdot \Delta t$$

- Stored in non-volatile memory (EEPROM/Flash)

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Resolution of DVM | $R = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | Volts | n = number of bits |
| 2 | Quantization Error | $Q = \pm \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{LSB} = \pm \dfrac{R}{2}$ | Volts | Inherent to all ADCs |
| 3 | Dual-Slope Output | $V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ | Volts | T1 fixed, T2 measured |
| 4 | Full-Scale Voltage | $V_{FS} = (2^n - 1) \cdot R$ | Volts | n-bit ADC |
| 5 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta N}{\Delta V}$ | counts/V | Inverse of resolution |
| 6 | Active Power (instant.) | $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$ | Watts | For purely resistive: $p = V I$ |
| 7 | Active Power (AC) | $P = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi$ | Watts | φ = phase angle |
| 8 | Energy | $E = \int_{0}^{T} p(t)\, dt = P \cdot T$ | Joules or kWh | 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J |
| 9 | Meter Constant (digital) | $K = \dfrac{N_{pulses}}{E_{kWh}}$ | pulses/kWh | Calibration constant |
| 10 | % Error | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{E_{actual} - E_{measured}}{E_{actual}} \times 100$ | % | For accuracy class |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Dual-slope ADCs dominate in **weighing scales, digital multimeters, panel meters, and energy meters** because the integration rejects 50/60 Hz line noise. SAR ADCs dominate in **oscilloscopes, data loggers, and communication transceivers** where speed matters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Dual-Slope ADC Output Voltage

**Given:**
- Integrator with R, C components
- Input voltage Vin applied for fixed time T1
- Reference voltage Vref (opposite polarity) applied until integrator output = 0
- Final run-down time = T2

**Find:** Relationship between Vin, Vref, T1, T2

**Step 1 — Write the integrator output equation for Phase 1 (Run-Up):**

The output of a standard inverting integrator is given by:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply to constant Vin over interval T1:**

Since Vin is constant DC, the integral simplifies to:

$$V_{A} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{T_1} V_{in}\, dt = -\frac{1}{RC} \cdot V_{in} \cdot T_1$$

$$\therefore \quad V_{A} = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC}$$

This is the peak voltage reached at the end of Phase 1.

**Step 3 — Write the integrator output equation for Phase 2 (Run-Down):**

At t = T1, the input to the integrator is switched to **−Vref** (opposite polarity). The output now evolves as:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{A} - \frac{1}{RC}\int_{T_1}^{t} (-V_{ref})\, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Apply the stopping condition (output returns to 0 at t = T1 + T2):**

$$0 = V_{A} - \frac{1}{RC} \cdot (-V_{ref}) \cdot T_2$$

$$0 = V_{A} + \frac{V_{ref} \cdot T_2}{RC}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute VA from Step 2:**

$$0 = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC} + \frac{V_{ref} \cdot T_2}{RC}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply both sides by RC (cancel the component parameters):**

$$0 = -V_{in} \cdot T_1 + V_{ref} \cdot T_2$$

**Step 7 — Solve for Vin:**

$$V_{in} \cdot T_1 = V_{ref} \cdot T_2$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}\,}$$

**Conclusion:** The measured voltage depends **only on the ratio of two times (T2/T1) and the reference voltage Vref**. It is **independent of R, C, and clock frequency variations** — the fundamental reason for the dual-slope ADC's superior accuracy and noise immunity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Resolution of an n-bit DVM

**Given:** Full-scale voltage VFS, number of bits n

**Total number of quantization levels** (distinct counts) = $2^n$

The maximum count value (excluding zero) = $2^n - 1$

**Resolution (R)** is defined as the **smallest measurable voltage change**, i.e., the size of 1 LSB:

$$R = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$

**Example:** For a 12-bit DVM with VFS = 10.000 V:

$$R = \frac{10.000}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{10.000}{4095} = 2.442 \text{ mV}$$

This means the display will increment by approximately 2.44 mV for every step.

**Quantization error** (worst-case error in any single reading):

$$Q_{max} = \pm \frac{1}{2}\,\text{LSB} = \pm \frac{R}{2} = \pm 1.221 \text{ mV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trick:** When a question says "4½ digit DVM with 200 mV full scale," compute resolution as $200 \text{ mV} / 19999 = 0.01 \text{ mV} = 10\,\mu V$. The ½ digit (leading "1" or blank) signifies overload/under-range.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Dual-Slope DVM Measurement

**Problem:** A dual-slope DVM uses Vref = 10.000 V and T1 = 20 ms. During measurement of an unknown DC input, the run-down time T2 is observed as 12.5 ms. Find the input voltage and the percentage error if the true value is 6.20 V.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the dual-slope formula:**

$$V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 10.000 \times \frac{12.5 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{12.5}{20.0} = 0.625$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by Vref:**

$$V_{in} = 10.000 \times 0.625 = 6.250 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the percentage error:**

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{V_{true} - V_{measured}}{V_{true}} \times 100$$

Wait — using measured as reference (as per instrument calibration convention):

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{V_{measured} - V_{true}}{V_{true}} \times 100 = \frac{6.250 - 6.200}{6.200} \times 100 = +0.806\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always specify whether you're computing **instrument accuracy** (relative to true value) or **reading error** (relative to full scale). KTU typically uses the true-value convention shown above.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Digital Energy Meter Energy Calculation

**Problem:** A household digital energy meter reads 2435.7 kWh at the start of a month and 2681.2 kWh at the end. If the electricity tariff is ₹7.50 per kWh, calculate: (a) energy consumed, (b) bill amount.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Energy consumed:**

$$E = 2681.2 - 2435.7 = 245.5 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Bill amount:**

$$\text{Bill} = E \times \text{Tariff} = 245.5 \times 7.50 = \text{₹}\,1841.25$$

**Step 3 — (Extension) Average power if constant over 30 days:**

$$T = 30 \times 24 = 720 \text{ hours}$$

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E}{T} = \frac{245.5}{720} = 0.341 \text{ kW} = 341 \text{ W}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Ramp-Type (Linear) DVM

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input DC Voltage Vin] --> AT[Attenuator / Range Selector]
    AT --> CMP1[Comparator + input]
    RAMP[Linear Ramp Generator] --> CMP1
    CMP1 --> GATE[Gating Circuit]
    CLK[Clock Oscillator] --> GATE
    START[Start Pulse] --> RAMP
    START --> GATE
    GATE --> CTR[Binary Counter]
    CTR --> DEC[Decoder Driver]
    DEC --> DISP[7 Segment LED Display]
    CTR -.reset.-> START
```

**Operating Sequence (read flowchart top-down):**
1. `Start Pulse` triggers both the **Ramp Generator** and opens the **Gating Circuit**.
2. Ramp voltage falls linearly and is compared with Vin at `CMP1`.
3. When ramp crosses Vin, comparator switches, closing the gate.
4. Pulses accumulated in `CTR` are proportional to the time interval (hence to Vin).
5. Counter is latched, decoded, and displayed; counter resets for next cycle.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Dual-Slope Integrating DVM

```mermaid
graph TB
    VIN[Unknown DC Input Vin] --> SW1[Input Switch SW1]
    VREF[Precision Reference Vref] --> SW1
    SW1 --> INT[Op Amp Integrator with RC]
    INT --> CMP2[Zero Crossing Detector / Comparator]
    CMP2 --> LOGIC[Control Logic and Gating]
    CLK2[Clock Source f_clk] --> LOGIC
    LOGIC --> CTR2[Binary Counter with Decoder]
    CTR2 --> DISP2[Digital Display kWh or V]
    LOGIC -.control signal.-> SW1
    LOGIC -.reset.-> CTR2
```

**State Diagram of Conversion:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Reset
    Reset --> RunUp: Apply Vin to Integrator
    RunUp --> RunDown: After fixed time T1 elapses
    RunDown --> CountT2: Apply -Vref, counter ON
    CountT2 --> Display: When integrator output equals 0
    Display --> Reset: After display hold time
    Reset --> [*]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Digital (Static) Energy Meter

```mermaid
graph TB
    MAINS[AC Mains Line and Load] --> VSEN[Voltage Sensor PT or VDivider]
    MAINS --> ISEN[Current Sensor CT or Shunt or Hall]
    VSEN --> ADC1[ADC Channel 1]
    ISEN --> ADC2[ADC Channel 2]
    ADC1 --> MCU[Microcontroller DSP]
    ADC2 --> MCU
    MCU --> CALC[Multiplier and Integrator Algorithm]
    CALC --> ACC[Energy Accumulator EEPROM]
    ACC --> DISP3[LCD Display kWh]
    MCU --> LEDCAL[Calibration LED Pulse Output]
    MCU --> COM[Communication Module RF or PLC]
    COM --> UTIL[Utility Server AMR AMI]
```

**Signal Flow Description:**
1. `MAINS` provides voltage and current to the load.
2. `VSEN` and `ISEN` safely scale these to low-level signals.
3. Dual `ADC` channels digitize both waveforms synchronously.
4. `MCU` computes **instantaneous power p(k) = v(k)·i(k)** at each sample.
5. `CALC` block sums p(k)·Δt over time → energy in joules.
6. Energy is stored in `ACC` (non-volatile memory) and pushed to `DISP3`.
7. `LEDCAL` emits calibration pulses (e.g., 1 pulse = 1 Wh) for testing.
8. `COM` enables remote reading (AMR/AMI) — a KTU-favourite modern topic.

---

### 4.4 Comparison Matrix: DVM Types vs. Energy Meter

| Feature | Ramp-Type DVM | Dual-Slope DVM | SAR DVM | Digital Energy Meter |
|---------|---------------|----------------|---------|----------------------|
| Conversion Principle | Time-to-voltage | Dual integration | Binary search | V×I integration |
| Speed | Slow | Medium | Fast | Real-time continuous |
| Noise Rejection | Poor | Excellent (line freq.) | Poor to Moderate | Good (with filtering) |
| Accuracy | ±0.1% | ±0.01% | ±0.05% | Class 0.5 / 0.2 |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Medium | High (with comms) |
| KTU Exam Frequency | ★★★ High | ★★★★★ Highest | ★★ Moderate | ★★★★ High |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Digital Voltmeter. List any two advantages of a DVM over a conventional analog voltmeter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Digital Voltmeter (DVM) is an electronic instrument that converts an unknown analog input voltage into a discrete digital value, which is then displayed numerically using LED/LCD segments. It uses an internal Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) and a reference voltage.

**Two advantages over analog voltmeters:**
1. **Higher accuracy and resolution** — DVMs can resolve down to µV with no parallax error, unlike pointer-type meters limited by scale resolution.
2. **High input impedance** (typically 10 MΩ) — negligible loading effect on the circuit under test, whereas analog meters draw current and disturb the measurement.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for any two valid advantages. Avoid vague answers like "easy to use.")*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a static energy meter? Why is it preferred over the conventional induction-type energy meter?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **static energy meter** (digital energy meter) is a solid-state instrument using electronic multipliers and digital counters to measure active energy (kWh) consumed by a load, with no moving parts.

**Reasons for preference over induction-type meter:**
1. **Higher accuracy class** (0.5 or 0.2 vs. 2.0 for induction), and accuracy is maintained over time without mechanical wear.
2. **Tamper detection and AMR capability** — supports remote reading, time-of-day tariffs, and bidirectional metering (for solar net-metering).
3. **No moving parts** — hence no friction, no braking torque errors, and long life.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for any two well-explained reasons.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Choose ONE)

---

#### **QUESTION A — Dual-Slope DVM (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **dual-slope integrating type DVM**. Sketch and label the integrator output waveform clearly showing both phases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dual-slope DVM uses a reference voltage of **10.000 V** and a run-up time of **20 ms**. When measuring an unknown input, the run-down time observed is **13.6 ms**. Find: (i) the unknown input voltage, (ii) the percentage error if the true value is 6.75 V, (iii) the quantization error if the counter is 12-bit with full scale 10.000 V. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of Dual-Slope DVM (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

Refer to the diagram in Section 4.2 above — show: Input switch, integrator, comparator, control logic, gate, clock, counter, decoder, display.

**Working (3 marks):**

**Phase 1 — Run-Up (Signal Integration):**
- At t = 0, the **input switch** connects the unknown voltage **Vin** to the integrator.
- The integrator output rises (or falls) linearly with slope $-V_{in}/RC$.
- After a **fixed time T1** (set by the counter to overflow, e.g., 20 ms), the switch is thrown to the reference.

**Phase 2 — Run-Down (Reference Integration):**
- A reference voltage **−Vref** (opposite polarity) is applied.
- The integrator output ramps back toward zero with slope $+V_{ref}/RC$.
- A zero-crossing detector triggers when the output reaches 0.
- The counter measures the run-down interval **T2**.

**Display and Reset (2 marks):**
- The count accumulated during T2 is **decoded and displayed** as the digital equivalent of Vin.
- The counter resets and the cycle repeats.

**Key Waveform Insight (mention explicitly):**
The waveform at the integrator output is a **two-slope triangular shape** — going up linearly during T1, then reversing and going back down to zero during T2. Label T1, T2, peak VA, slopes, and indicate clock gating intervals.

**Key relationship (mandatory mention for full marks):**
$$V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Vref = 10.000 V
- T1 = 20 ms = 20 × 10⁻³ s
- T2 = 13.6 ms = 13.6 × 10⁻³ s
- True value = 6.75 V
- n = 12 bits, VFS = 10.000 V

**(i) Unknown input voltage (3 marks):**

$$V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 10.000 \times \frac{13.6 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$V_{in} = 10.000 \times 0.680 = \mathbf{6.800 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Percentage error (2 marks):**

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{V_{measured} - V_{true}}{V_{true}} \times 100 = \frac{6.800 - 6.750}{6.750} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{0.050}{6.750} \times 100 = +\mathbf{0.741\%}$$

**Positive sign** indicates the meter reads **high** (over-reading). Often within class 1.0 accuracy.

**(iii) Quantization error (2 marks):**

$$R = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{10.000}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{10.000}{4095} = 2.4421 \text{ mV}$$

$$Q_{max} = \pm \frac{R}{2} = \pm \mathbf{1.221 \text{ mV}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B — Digital Energy Meter (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **digital (static) energy meter**. Discuss the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A static energy meter is installed at a residential consumer. The voltage and current waveforms sampled by the meter's ADC for one AC cycle (20 ms) are recorded as:

| Sample k | v(k) in V | i(k) in A |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | 325.0 | 12.5 |
| 2 | 311.0 | 9.8 |
| 3 | 215.0 | 5.6 |
| 4 | 110.0 | 1.8 |
| 5 | -110.0 | -1.8 |
| 6 | -215.0 | -5.6 |
| 7 | -311.0 | -9.8 |
| 8 | -325.0 | -12.5 |

Sampling interval Δt = 2.5 ms. Compute: (i) the energy consumed in this cycle, (ii) the average power, (iii) the energy in kWh if the load runs continuously for 24 hours at the same average power. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram of Digital Energy Meter (7 marks):**

Refer to the diagram in Section 4.3 — describe each block as follows:

| Block | Function (allocate ~1 mark each) |
|-------|---------------------------------|
| **Voltage Sensor (PT / V-Divider)** | Steps down line voltage to safe, low-level signal proportional to v(t) |
| **Current Sensor (CT / Shunt / Hall)** | Produces low-level signal proportional to i(t), galvanically isolated from line |
| **ADC (Dual Channel)** | Digitizes both v(t) and i(t) at high sampling rate (≥ 4 kHz for harmonics) |
| **Microcontroller / DSP** | Executes the algorithm: $p(k) = v(k) \cdot i(k)$; integrates over time |
| **Multiplier (algorithm)** | Computes instantaneous power — could also be analog TDM multiplier in older designs |
| **V/f Converter / Accumulator** | Converts power to pulse rate; counter accumulates pulses → energy |
| **Display (LCD)** | Shows cumulative kWh to consumer |
| **Non-volatile Memory (EEPROM)** | Stores readings across power outages |
| **Calibration LED & Communication** | LED pulses for testing; RF/PLC module for AMR/AMI remote reading |

**Brief Working Narrative (remaining marks):**
The voltage and current are sensed, digitized, and multiplied sample-by-sample. The product is summed and scaled to obtain the running total of energy in kWh. The value is latched in non-volatile memory and continuously displayed. Tamper detection algorithms monitor for bypass or reverse connections, and a communication port (optical/RS-232/RF) allows the utility to read the meter remotely.

---
