**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** 8 samples, Δt = 2.5 ms, total cycle T = 20 ms

**(i) Energy in one cycle (3 marks):**

Compute instantaneous power p(k) = v(k)·i(k) at each sample:

| k | v(k) | i(k) | p(k) = v·i (W) |
|---|------|------|----------------|
| 1 | 325.0 | 12.5 | 4062.5 |
| 2 | 311.0 | 9.8 | 3047.8 |
| 3 | 215.0 | 5.6 | 1204.0 |
| 4 | 110.0 | 1.8 | 198.0 |
| 5 | -110.0 | -1.8 | 198.0 |
| 6 | -215.0 | -5.6 | 1204.0 |
| 7 | -311.0 | -9.8 | 3047.8 |
| 8 | -325.0 | -12.5 | 4062.5 |

$$\sum p(k) = 2 \times (4062.5 + 3047.8 + 1204.0 + 198.0) = 2 \times 8512.3 = 17024.6 \text{ W}$$

Energy per cycle (numerical integration using rectangular rule):

$$E_{cycle} = \sum p(k) \cdot \Delta t = 17024.6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = \mathbf{42.5615 \text{ J}}$$

**(ii) Average power (2 marks):**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_{cycle}}{T} = \frac{42.5615}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = \mathbf{2128.1 \text{ W} \approx 2.128 \text{ kW}}$$

*(Alternative: $P_{avg} = \frac{1}{N}\sum p(k) = \frac{17024.6}{8} = 2128.1$ W — same result.)*

**(iii) Energy in 24 hours (2 marks):**

$$T_{total} = 24 \text{ h}$$

$$E_{24h} = P_{avg} \times T_{total} = 2.1281 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h} = \mathbf{51.07 \text{ kWh}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose NO Marks Here:**
> 1. **Forgetting to label the waveform** in ramp/dual-slope DVM — examiners *demand* a labelled sketch (T1, T2, VA, slopes) for full 7 marks on the descriptive part. A textual answer alone fetches only 3-4 marks.
> 2. **Sign convention on percentage error** — always state whether the meter reads high (positive) or low (negative). Simply writing "0.741%" without sign loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. **In energy-meter numericals**, students often forget to **multiply by Δt** to get Joules, or skip the **N samples → average power** step. Show the rectangular integration sum explicitly.
> 4. **Quantization error sign** — write **±** with the value; examiners mark the formula and unit (mV or V) carefully.
> 5. **Block diagrams without arrows** showing signal flow are penalized. Use the standard input → process → output top-to-bottom or left-to-right convention.
> 6. **Do not write "DVM is more accurate"** as an advantage without quantifying — say "10 µV resolution at 200 mV range" or "accuracy 0.05% of reading."

---

### 🔑 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **DVM Definition:** Converts analog voltage → digital display using ADC + reference + counter.
- **Three DVM types in syllabus:** Ramp-type (obsolete, waveform-based), Dual-Slope (industry standard, noise-immune), SAR (fast, binary search).
- **Dual-slope formula (MUST memorize):** $V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot (T_2 / T_1)$ — independent of R, C, and clock.
- **Resolution formula:** $R = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$. **Quantization error:** $Q = \pm R/2 = \pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB.
- **DVM advantages:** High input impedance (10 MΩ), no parallax, direct interface to computers/PLCs, autoranging.
- **Static Energy Meter:** Solid-state, no moving parts, uses V & I sensors + multiplier + V/f converter + counter.
- **Active Power AC:** $P = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi$. **Energy:** $E = P \cdot t$ (in kWh for billing).
- **Digital energy computation algorithm:** $E = \sum v(k) \cdot i(k) \cdot \Delta t$ (numerical integration).
- **Accuracy classes (KTU fact):** Class 2.0 / 1.0 / 0.5 / 0.2 / 0.1 — lower number = higher accuracy.
- **Modern features expected in answers:** AMR/AMI, tamper detection, net-metering (solar), time-of-use tariffs, TOU display.
- **Common KTU mistake to avoid:** Never confuse **V-F converter** (in old static meters) with **ADC** (in modern digital meters). Both exist; mention which one your block diagram shows.
- **Waveform label rule:** Always mark T1 (run-up, constant), T2 (run-down, variable), VA (peak amplitude), and the slopes $(V_{in}/RC)$ and $(V_{ref}/RC)$ on your dual-slope output plot.
- **Unit vigilance:** Resolution → **V** (or mV, µV); Energy → **J in physics, kWh in billing**; Power → **W or kW**; % Error → **dimensionless with ± sign**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TOD Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT411 - ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENT AND MEASURING INSTRUMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TOD Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **TOD (Time of Of Day) Meter** — also known as a **Time-of-Use (TOU) Meter** — is an advanced multi-tariff electronic energy meter that records and stores the consumption of electrical energy (active, reactive, or apparent) segregated into **different time slots** of a 24-hour day. The tariff rates applied by utilities (electricity boards) differ across these time bands, thereby incentivizing consumers to shift heavy loads away from peak demand periods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** A TOD meter is essentially a multi-register energy meter whose register is automatically switched by an internal real-time clock (RTC) and timer circuit in synchronism with pre-programmed time zones, enabling differential energy accounting.

The three standard time zones used by most State Electricity Regulatory Commissions (SERCs) in India are:

| Time Zone | Typical Hours (India) | Tariff |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak Hours** | 06:00 – 10:00 & 18:00 – 22:00 | Highest |
| **Normal Hours** | 10:00 – 18:00 | Medium |
| **Off-Peak Hours** | 22:00 – 06:00 | Lowest |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍕

> [!NOTE]
> **Pizza Delivery Analogy:** Imagine ordering pizza at midnight vs. at 8 PM on a Saturday. The same pizza costs **₹250 at 2 AM** but **₹450 at 8 PM** because the kitchen and delivery boys are busier at peak dinner time. A TOD meter works **exactly like this** — it charges different "rates" for the *same unit* of electricity depending on **when** you consume it. Your washing machine at 11 PM is the *same electrical energy* as your geyser at 7 AM, but the bill is computed differently because the utility's stress on the grid was different at those two moments.

## 1.3 Why Was It Invented?

The Indian power grid historically suffers from a **"Duck Curve"** problem — a steep peak in the morning (06:00–10:00) and evening (18:00–22:00). Conventional flat-rate meters gave consumers **no economic incentive** to shift loads (e.g., EV charging, water heaters, industrial pumps) to off-peak hours. The TOD meter solves this by **financially penalizing peak-hour consumption** and **rewarding off-peak usage**, flattening the load curve and deferring costly generation capacity addition.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Physical Reference:** The "base time" is maintained by a **quartz crystal oscillator** (typical frequency **32.768 kHz**), which divides down to a precise 1-second tick for the RTC. This is the same crystal used in digital wristwatches.

## 1.4 KTU-Mandated Core Components

- **Measurement Unit:** Voltage sensor (potential divider) + Current sensor (shunt/CT) → multiplying ADC
- **Microcontroller / DSP Engine:** Calculates kWh per time slot
- **Real-Time Clock (RTC) with Backup Battery:** Retains time even during power failure (typically a **CR2032 Lithium cell**, **3 V**, ~**3 years** life)
- **Multi-Register Memory:** Separate energy counters for each tariff zone (usually 2, 4, or 8 registers)
- **LCD / LED Display:** Cyclically displays cumulative energy in each zone
- **Communication Port (Optional):** Optical port / RS-232 / RF for AMR (Automated Meter Reading)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The TOD meter does **not** measure a new physical quantity. Instead, it is a **conventional kWh meter augmented with three extra functional blocks**:

1. **Energy Sensing Stage:** Samples voltage $v(t)$ and current $i(t)$; the instantaneous power is
$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$

2. **Integration Stage:** Active energy over a billing interval $T$ is the integral of real power
$$E = \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \cdot i(t) \cdot \cos\phi \; dt \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

3. **Time-Slot Routing Stage:** The microcontroller continuously monitors the RTC and adds the measured energy to the **register corresponding to the currently active tariff zone**.

4. **Storage & Display Stage:** Each register's cumulative energy is retained in non-volatile memory (EEPROM/Flash).

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" behind each block:** The sensing stage converts analog to digital. The integration stage obeys the IEEE/IEC definition of active energy. The routing stage implements the *temporal differentiation*. The storage stage ensures data survives power outage — critical because the meter is revenue-grade.

## 2.2 Mathematical Model of Multi-Zone Billing

Let there be $n$ tariff zones indexed by $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$. Each zone has:
- A duration $T_k$ (hours per day)
- A tariff rate $R_k$ (₹/kWh or any currency unit)

The total bill for one billing cycle of $D$ days is then
$$\text{Bill} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left( R_k \cdot E_k \right)$$

where $E_k$ is the total energy consumed in zone $k$ over the $D$-day period:
$$E_k = D \cdot \int_{t \in \text{Zone}_k} v(t) i(t) \cos\phi \; dt$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$ | Instantaneous power | W |
| 2 | $P = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\phi$ | Average real power | W |
| 3 | $E = \int P \, dt$ | Energy consumed | Wh |
| 4 | $\text{Bill} = \sum_{k} R_k E_k$ | Total TOD-bill | ₹ |
| 5 | $\Delta f_{\text{xtal}} = f_0 \cdot \Delta T / T_0$ | RTC drift from quartz | Hz |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{load factor}} = E_{\text{avg}} / E_{\text{peak}}$ | Load flattening metric | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} T_k = 24$ | Sum of all zone durations | hours |
| 8 | $\text{PF} = \cos\phi = P / S$ | Power factor | p.u. or % |
| 9 | $E_{\text{reactive}} = \int V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \sin\phi \, dt$ | Reactive energy | VARh |
| 10 | $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | Apparent power | VA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** For a valid TOD tariff structure, the time zones must be **mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive** over 24 hours, i.e., $\sum T_k = 24$ hours and no time instant belongs to two zones.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 🏭

- **Industrial Consumers:** Steel mills, cement plants shift induction-furnace operation to off-peak hours, saving **15–30%** on energy bills.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Charging Stations:** Smart-charging algorithms use TOD signals to schedule charging between **23:00 and 05:00**.
- **Demand-Side Management (DSM):** Discoms use aggregated TOD data to predict the **duck curve** and schedule generation.
- **Renewable Integration:** Solar generation peaks at noon; TOD pricing nudges loads to absorb solar energy, reducing curtailment.
- **Smart Grid Backbone:** TOD meters are foundational IEDs (Intelligent Electronic Devices) feeding AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Energy from Multi-Zone Consumption

**Problem Setup:** A small industry consumes electrical energy over 30 days as follows:
- Peak zone ($T_1 = 8$ hr/day): Average load $P_1 = 50$ kW
- Normal zone ($T_2 = 8$ hr/day): Average load $P_2 = 30$ kW
- Off-peak zone ($T_3 = 8$ hr/day): Average load $P_3 = 20$ kW

Determine the total energy in each register and the total monthly bill if tariff rates are $R_1 = ₹9.50$/kWh, $R_2 = ₹7.20$/kWh, $R_3 = ₹4.80$/kWh.

### Solution Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Energy per zone per day**

For each zone, energy in one day is power multiplied by hours-of-use:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1^{(\text{day})} &= P_1 \cdot T_1 \\
&= 50 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ hr} \\
&= 400 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_2^{(\text{day})} &= P_2 \cdot T_2 \\
&= 30 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ hr} \\
&= 240 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_3^{(\text{day})} &= P_3 \cdot T_3 \\
&= 20 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ hr} \\
&= 160 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify time-zone completeness**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{k=1}^{3} T_k &= 8 + 8 + 8 \\
&= 24 \text{ hr} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The zone durations sum to 24 hours, satisfying the boundary condition from Section 2.3.

**Step 3 — Energy per zone over 30 days**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1^{(30)} &= 400 \times 30 = 12000 \text{ kWh} \\
E_2^{(30)} &= 240 \times 30 = 7200 \text{ kWh} \\
E_3^{(30)} &= 160 \times 30 = 4800 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total monthly bill using multi-tariff summation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bill} &= \sum_{k=1}^{3} R_k E_k^{(30)} \\
&= (9.50)(12000) + (7.20)(7200) + (4.80)(4800) \\
&= 114000 + 51840 + 23040 \\
&= ₹188{,}880
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with flat-rate billing**

Had the consumer been billed at a flat rate of, say, $R_{\text{flat}} = ₹7.00$/kWh:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= 12000 + 7200 + 4800 = 24000 \text{ kWh} \\
\text{Bill}_{\text{flat}} &= 7.00 \times 24000 = ₹168{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Savings analysis (what if industry shifts 30% of peak load to off-peak)**

Shifted peak load = $0.30 \times 50 = 15$ kW moved to off-peak zone.
New off-peak power = $20 + 15 = 35$ kW.
New peak power = $50 - 15 = 35$ kW.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1^{\text{new}} &= 35 \times 8 \times 30 = 8400 \text{ kWh} \\
E_3^{\text{new}} &= 35 \times 8 \times 30 = 8400 \text{ kWh} \\
\text{Bill}_{\text{new}} &= (9.50)(8400) + (7.20)(7200) + (4.80)(8400) \\
&= 79800 + 51840 + 40320 \\
&= ₹171{,}960
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Savings} = 188880 - 171960 = ₹16920 \;\; (\approx 8.96\%)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RTC Drift Compensation (Examiner-Favorite Topic)

**Problem:** A TOD meter uses a quartz crystal with nominal frequency $f_0 = 32768$ Hz. Over 30 days, it drifts by $\Delta f = +2$ Hz due to temperature. Compute the cumulative time error.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total oscillations (ideal)} &= f_0 \times t = 32768 \times (30 \times 86400) \\
&= 32768 \times 2592000 \\
&= 8.4947 \times 10^{10} \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual frequency} &= f_0 + \Delta f = 32770 \text{ Hz} \\
\text{Actual elapsed time} &= \frac{f_0 \times t}{f_0 + \Delta f} \\
&= \frac{8.4947 \times 10^{10}}{32770} \\
&= 2.59207 \times 10^{6} \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time error} &= t_{\text{actual}} - t_{\text{ideal}} \\
&= 2592070 - 2592000 = 70 \text{ seconds fast in 30 days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implication:** Even a tiny 2 Hz drift causes the meter to believe the day has 24 hours + 70 s. After 1 year this can exceed **14 minutes**. IS 14697 / IEC 62053-21 specify the meter RTC drift must be **< 0.5 s/day** for Class 1 accuracy. Hence high-stability DTCXO crystals are used in premium TOD meters.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TOD vs. Other Meters

| Feature | Conventional Meter | TOD Meter | Smart Meter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time differentiation | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Real-time clock | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ (synced via GSM/GPRS) |
| Multiple registers | Single | 2–8 | 8–48 (interval data) |
| Tariff complexity | Flat | Step-tariff | ToU + Critical-peak + Real-time |
| Communication | None | Optional optical | Mandatory bidirectional |
| Data granularity | Cumulative | Zonal cumulative | 15-min interval data |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TOD Meter

The TOD meter cannot be adequately represented as a physical schematic; instead, we map its functional architecture to clearly show the **flow of measurement, timing, and tariff logic**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VSense[Voltage Sensor\nPotential Divider] --> ADC1[ADC Channel 1]
    ISense[Current Sensor\nShunt or CT] --> ADC2[ADC Channel 2]
    ADC1 --> MUL[Digital Multiplier\nand Integrator]
    ADC2 --> MUL
    MUL --> ENERGY[Active Energy Engine\nComputes kWh]
    ENERGY --> MCU[Microcontroller MCU]
    XOSC[Quartz Crystal\n32768 Hz] --> RTC[Real Time Clock RTC]
    BAT[Backup Battery CR2032 3V] --> RTC
    RTC --> MCU
    TBL[Tariff Time Zone Table\nStored in EEPROM] --> MCU
    MCU --> REG1[Register 1 Peak Hours]
    MCU --> REG2[Register 2 Normal Hours]
    MCU --> REG3[Register 3 Off Peak Hours]
    MCU --> REG4[Optional Register 4\nReactive Energy]
    REG1 --> LCD[LCD Display Cyclic]
    REG2 --> LCD
    REG3 --> LCD
    REG4 --> LCD
    MCU --> OPT[Optical Port 62056 21]
    MCU --> RSOC[RS232 or RF Optional]
    MCU --> EEPR[EEPROM Non Volatile Memory]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **left side** is the analog front-end (sensing). The **center** is the digital computation and decision engine. The **right side** is the data storage and human-machine interface. The **RTC block** is what distinguishes a TOD meter from a plain electronic energy meter.

## 4.2 Tariff Switching State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OffPeak
    OffPeak --> Normal: Time crosses 06 00
    Normal --> Peak: Time crosses 10 00
    Peak --> Normal: Time crosses 18 00 wait wrong
    Normal --> Peak: Time crosses 18 00
    Peak --> OffPeak: Time crosses 22 00
    OffPeak --> Normal: Time crosses 06 00 next day
    note right of OffPeak: Tariff R3 low rate
    note right of Normal: Tariff R2 medium rate
    note right of Peak: Tariff R1 high rate
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Logic:** The MCU continually polls the RTC. On every **state transition**, it closes the current register, opens the new one, and increments an internal **transition counter** (for audit purposes). Each transition is logged with a timestamp in EEPROM.

## 4.3 Data Flow Through Registers (Cycle Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Measurement] --> B{Current Time Slot}
    B --> C1[Add to Peak Register]
    B --> C2[Add to Normal Register]
    B --> C3[Add to Off Peak Register]
    C1 --> D1[EEPROM Store Peak]
    C2 --> D2[EEPROM Store Normal]
    C3 --> D3[EEPROM Store Off Peak]
    D1 --> E[LCD Cyclic Display]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Utility Billing Engine]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a TOD meter. Mention any two advantages of TOD metering from the consumer's perspective.

**Model Answer:**

A **Time-of-Day (TOD) meter** is an electronic multi-register energy meter that records the consumption of electrical energy in **separate registers** corresponding to **different time slots of the day**, using an internal real-time clock and pre-programmed tariff zone table.

**Two advantages to the consumer:**

1. **Monetary Savings:** Consumers can shift discretionary loads (e.g., washing machines, water heaters, EV charging) to **off-peak hours** where tariffs are 30–50% lower, directly reducing the monthly bill.
2. **Improved Awareness:** The cyclic display of separate register readings creates conscious awareness of *when* energy is being used, encouraging disciplined energy behavior.

*(Examiner's note: Students often write "reduces bill" without explaining the mechanism. Always specify HOW — i.e., load shifting to cheaper time zones.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List the essential functional blocks of a TOD meter. Why is a backup battery provided in a TOD meter?

**Model Answer:**

**Essential functional blocks:**

1. **Voltage and current sensing** (potential divider + shunt/CT)
2. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** for signal sampling
3. **Multiplier and integrator** (computes instantaneous power and energy)
4. **Microcontroller** (system brain)
5. **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** with quartz crystal oscillator
6. **Multiple energy registers** (one per tariff zone, stored in EEPROM)
7. **Display unit** (LCD with cyclic register readout)

**Purpose of backup battery:**

The **CR2032 lithium cell (3 V)** maintains the RTC and register memory during power outages. Without it, the meter would **lose track of time**, causing **tariff misclassification** (e.g., billing off-peak consumption at peak rates). It also ensures cumulative register readings are not erased.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of a TOD meter. Highlight the role of the real-time clock.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An industrial consumer records the following average loads:
- Peak zone (06:00–10:00 and 18:00–22:00, total **8 hr/day**): 75 kW
- Normal zone (10:00–18:00, **8 hr/day**): 40 kW
- Off-peak zone (22:00–06:00, **8 hr/day**): 25 kW

Tariffs: Peak = ₹10.00/kWh, Normal = ₹7.50/kWh, Off-peak = ₹5.00/kWh.
Compute the **monthly bill (30 days)**. If 20% of peak load is shifted to off-peak, calculate the **new bill** and the **percentage savings**.

---

### ✅ Solution to Question A

#### (a) Working Principle (7 marks)

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The working steps are:

1. **Sensing:** The line voltage is stepped down by a potential divider; line current is sensed by a shunt resistor or CT. Both signals are conditioned and fed to the ADC.
2. **Computation:** The MCU computes instantaneous power $p(t) = v(t)\,i(t)$ and integrates over time to obtain active energy.
3. **Time-slot determination:** The **RTC** (driven by a **32.768 kHz quartz crystal**) generates a continuous 1-second tick. The MCU reads the time and matches it against the pre-stored tariff zone table.
4. **Register routing:** The computed energy is added **only to the register corresponding to the currently active zone**.
5. **Storage:** All three registers are updated every billing cycle in EEPROM (non-volatile memory).
6. **Display:** A pushbutton or auto-cyclic LCD shows **Register 1 / 2 / 3** readings in turn.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of RTC:** The RTC is the "heart" that differentiates a TOD meter from a flat-rate meter. It provides **absolute time reference** and ensures that even if power fails, the time and tariff classification remain accurate. Without RTC, energy could not be apportioned across time slots.

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Energy per zone per day**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= 75 \times 8 = 600 \text{ kWh/day} \\
E_2 &= 40 \times 8 = 320 \text{ kWh/day} \\
E_3 &= 25 \times 8 = 200 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy per zone for 30 days**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1^{(30)} &= 600 \times 30 = 18000 \text{ kWh} \\
E_2^{(30)} &= 320 \times 30 = 9600 \text{ kWh} \\
E_3^{(30)} &= 200 \times 30 = 6000 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Original monthly bill**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bill}_{\text{old}} &= (10.00)(18000) + (7.50)(9600) + (5.00)(6000) \\
&= 180000 + 72000 + 30000 \\
&= ₹282{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — After 20% load shift from peak to off-peak**

Shifted peak power = $0.20 \times 75 = 15$ kW.
New peak power = $75 - 15 = 60$ kW.
New off-peak power = $25 + 15 = 40$ kW.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1^{\text{new}} &= 60 \times 8 \times 30 = 14400 \text{ kWh} \\
E_3^{\text{new}} &= 40 \times 8 \times 30 = 9600 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — New monthly bill**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bill}_{\text{new}} &= (10.00)(14400) + (7.50)(9600) + (5.00)(9600) \\
&= 144000 + 72000 + 48000 \\
&= ₹264{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Percentage savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= 282000 - 264000 = ₹18000 \\
\%\text{ savings} &= \frac{18000}{282000} \times 100 \approx 6.38\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare conventional electronic energy meters and TOD meters in terms of (i) number of registers, (ii) tariff structure, (iii) real-time clock requirement, (iv) cost, and (v) consumer benefits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A domestic consumer uses the following loads:
- Morning peak (07:00–10:00): 2 kW geyser for 1 hr and 0.5 kW appliances for 2 hr
- Evening peak (18:00–22:00): 1 kW AC for 3 hr and 0.3 kW lighting for 4 hr
- Off-peak (22:00–07:00): 0.2 kW refrigerator continuous (estimate 9 hr as additional contribution above baseline)

Tariffs: Peak = ₹8.00/kWh, Normal = ₹6.00/kWh, Off-peak = ₹4.50/kWh.
Calculate the **daily energy cost** and the **savings** if the geyser is shifted to off-peak hours.

---

### ✅ Solution to Question B

#### (a) Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | Conventional Meter | TOD Meter |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Registers | Single cumulative register | Multiple registers (2–8) |
| (ii) Tariff | Flat single-rate | Time-differentiated multi-tariff |
| (iii) Real-time clock | Not required | Mandatory, with battery backup |
| (iv) Cost | Low (₹500–₹1500) | Higher (₹2500–₹6000) due to RTC + memory |
| (v) Consumer benefit | None beyond measurement | Bill reduction through load shifting |

**Examiner Tip:** Mention **load curve flattening** and **grid stability** as *societal* benefits beyond individual savings.

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Peak morning energy**

Geyser energy: $2 \text{ kW} \times 1 \text{ hr} = 2 \text{ kWh}$.
Other appliances: $0.5 \text{ kW} \times 2 \text{ hr} = 1 \text{ kWh}$.
$E_{\text{peak-morning}} = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ kWh}$.

**Step 2 — Peak evening energy**

AC energy: $1 \text{ kW} \times 3 \text{ hr} = 3 \text{ kWh}$.
Lighting: $0.3 \text{ kW} \times 4 \text{ hr} = 1.2 \text{ kWh}$.
$E_{\text{peak-evening}} = 3 + 1.2 = 4.2 \text{ kWh}$.

**Step 3 — Total peak energy**

$$
E_{\text{peak}} = 3 + 4.2 = 7.2 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Off-peak contribution**

Refrigerator: $0.2 \text{ kW} \times 9 \text{ hr} = 1.8 \text{ kWh}$.
$E_{\text{off-peak}} = 1.8 \text{ kWh}$.

**Step 5 — Original daily cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{old}} &= (8.00)(7.2) + (4.50)(1.8) \\
&= 57.60 + 8.10 = ₹65.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — After shifting geyser to off-peak**

Geyser is now in off-peak: $2 \text{ kWh}$ shifts from peak to off-peak.
New peak: $7.2 - 2 = 5.2 \text{ kWh}$.
New off-peak: $1.8 + 2 = 3.8 \text{ kWh}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{new}} &= (8.00)(5.2) + (4.50)(3.8) \\
&= 41.60 + 17.10 = ₹58.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Daily savings**

$$
\text{Savings} = 65.70 - 58.70 = ₹7.00 \text{ per day}
$$

Annualized: $7.00 \times 365 = ₹2555$ per year.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 30 days:** Many students compute daily energy and forget to scale to monthly energy. This costs **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Mixing tariff rates:** Peak rate must NEVER be applied to off-peak energy, even if the consumer says "it's all electricity." The TOD concept is precisely about *separating* these.
> 3. **Not mentioning the RTC:** Any explanation of TOD meter that omits the **Real-Time Clock** will lose at least 2 marks — it is the *defining* component.
> 4. **Unit errors:** Writing kW·h as kWh/hr or mixing kW and kWh confuses the examiner. Always write units explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the zone-completeness check:** Always verify $\sum T_k = 24$ hr; examiners reward this.
> 6. **Treating it as a power meter:** TOD meters record **energy** (kWh), not power (kW). Power × time = energy.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **TOD = Time of Day** metering; the same kWh is billed differently at different times.
- ✅ **Three core zones:** Peak, Normal, Off-peak (Indian discom convention).
- ✅ **RTC at 32.768 kHz** with **CR2032 backup battery** is the heart of a TOD meter.
- ✅ **Multiple registers** (typically 2–8) store zonal energy cumulatively in **EEPROM**.
- ✅ **Boundary condition:** $\sum T_k = 24$ hours (zones are mutually exclusive and exhaustive).
- ✅ **Bill formula:** $\text{Bill} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k \cdot E_k$ where $R_k$ is tariff and $E_k$ is energy in zone $k$.
- ✅ **Standards:** IS 14697 (India), IEC 62053-21 / 62053-22 / 62053-23 for static meters.
- ✅ **RTC drift limit:** < **0.5 s/day** for Class 1 accuracy.
- ✅ **Load factor improvement** is the prime grid-level benefit of TOD deployment.
- ✅ **Smart meter ≠ TOD meter:** Smart meters are a superset; TOD is a *feature* of smart meters.
- ✅ **Tariff signal** is the primary **Demand-Side Management (DSM)** tool for utilities.
- ✅ **Exam mantra:** When asked to explain TOD, **always** start with "RTC + multiple registers + differential tariff" — these three phrases cover ~80% of the marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Metering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT411 - ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENT AND MEASURING INSTRUMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Metering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SMART METERING (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Smart Metering is an **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)** system that employs electronic measurement, processing, two-way communication, and data management technologies to record energy (or other utility) consumption in **real-time or near real-time intervals**, enabling automated, bidirectional information flow between the consumer's end-device (the smart meter) and the utility's central management system.

A *Smart Meter* is the end-point electronic device that, in addition to performing the basic function of an energy meter (measuring active, reactive, and apparent energy), also supports:
- Time-of-Use (TOU) tariff handling
- Remote connect/disconnect
- Tamper detection and event logging
- Power quality monitoring (voltage sag/swell, harmonics)
- Two-way communication (uplink + downlink)
- Firmware upgrades over-the-air (OTA)

**Key International Standards** governing smart metering:
- **IEC 62056** – COSEM (Companion Specification for Energy Metering) / DLMS (Device Language Message Specification)
- **IEEE 1701 / 1702 / 1703** – LAN/WAN communication protocols
- **IS 16444 (Part 1 & 2)** – Indian Standard for AC Static Direct Connected Smart Energy Meters (BIS)
- **IS 15959 (Part 1–3)** – Data exchange protocol for smart meters in India

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Digital Postman + Cashier"

Imagine your old electromechanical energy meter is a **silent clerk** that simply keeps stacking up "energy tokens" in a hidden box. Once a month, a human meter reader physically visits, peeks into the box, copies down the total, and leaves. The process is slow, error-prone, and gives you *no idea* what happened during the month.

A **Smart Meter is like a modern digital bank teller + postman rolled into one**:
- It *measures* every kilowatt-hour used, *timestamped* exactly when it happened.
- It *talks* to the utility's central computer over a wireless or wired link (like a digital postman).
- It *receives* instructions — new tariffs, disconnect orders, firmware updates (like a cashier accepting remote instructions).
- It *reports* anomalies — like someone trying to bypass it (tamper detection).
- It shows *you*, the consumer, exactly how much you are spending this very minute on a display or app.

So, **Smart Metering = Smart Meter (hardware) + Two-way Communication Network + Central Data Management System (software)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU 2024:** India's National Smart Meter Programme (NSMP) targets the deployment of **250+ million** smart meters by 2025 under the Revamped Distribution Sector Scheme (RDSS). Every electrical engineer is expected to understand AMI architecture as a high-impact contemporary topic.

### 1.3 Smart Meter vs Conventional Meter – Quick Comparison

| Feature | Conventional (Electromechanical / Static) Meter | Smart Meter |
|---|---|---|
| Reading method | Manual, on-site | Automatic, remote (AMR/AMI) |
| Data granularity | Cumulative only | Interval data (15-min / 30-min) |
| Communication | None | RF / PLC / Cellular / ZigBee |
| TOU Tariff | Not supported | Native support |
| Tamper detection | Mechanical seal only | Electronic event logs with alarms |
| Remote connect/disconnect | Not possible | Possible via relay |
| Power quality monitoring | No | Yes (sag, swell, THD) |
| Outage detection | No | Last-gasp signalling |

> [!NOTE]
> **AMR (Automated Meter Reading)** is the *one-way* read-only version (utility → consumer), while **AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure)** is the *two-way* interactive version — Smart Metering specifically implies AMI.

### 1.4 Key Physical/Regulatory Constants for Smart Metering in India

- **Operating Voltage:** 240 V (phase to neutral), 415 V (three-phase)
- **Base Current (Ib):** 5 A / 10 A (direct connected); 1 A / 5 A (CT operated)
- **Maximum Current (Imax):** 60 A / 100 A typical
- **Accuracy Class:** Class 1.0 or Class 0.5 (active), Class 2.0 (reactive)
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** **27 °C ± 2 °C**
- **Standard Reference Frequency:** **50 Hz ± 5%**
- **Data interval:** 15 minutes (typical as per IS 16444)
- **Memory retention without power:** **> 10 years** (BIS mandate)
- **Communication:** RF mesh (865–867 MHz) or PLC (CENELEC A/B/C/D bands)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Smart Metering Architecture

A complete Smart Metering system (the **AMI stack**) is built on four integrated layers:

**Pillar 1 — Smart Meter End Device (Field Layer)**
- Microcontroller/MCU + metering IC (e.g., ADE7758, ADE9000 family)
- Voltage sensor (resistive divider) + Current sensor (CT / Shunt / Rogowski)
- Real-Time Clock (RTC) with battery backup
- Communication module (RF / PLC / Cellular)
- Local display (LCD) + Optical port (IEC 62056-21)
- Load control switch (relay / contactor) for remote connect/disconnect

**Pillar 2 — Communication Network (Transport Layer)**
The smart meter is the "node" and the network is the "highway." Common options:

| Technology | Medium | Range | Data Rate | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PLC** (Power Line Comm.) | LV/MV power line | 1–3 km on LV | 5–500 kbps | Urban dense areas |
| **RF Mesh (ZigBee/Wi-SUN)** | 865–867 MHz RF | 100 m–1 km/hop | 50–300 kbps | Multi-hop dense urban |
| **Cellular (GPRS/4G/NB-IoT)** | Telecom towers | Several km | 50 kbps–10 Mbps | Rural / sparse |
| **LoRaWAN** | Sub-GHz RF (868/915 MHz) | 2–10 km | 0.3–50 kbps | Long-range low-rate |
| **Wi-Fi** | 2.4 GHz | 30–50 m | 11–300 Mbps | Indoor HAN/BAN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAN (Home Area Network)** = Inside the home (smart meter ↔ appliances).
> **NAN (Neighbourhood Area Network)** = Aggregator to meters.
> **WAN (Wide Area Network)** = Utility DC to NAN gateway.

**Pillar 3 — Head-End System (HES)**
- Server software at the utility Data Centre
- Manages millions of meters
- Performs data collection, command dispatch, OTA firmware pushes
- Validates and stores interval data in a relational database (typically SQL)
- Generates alarms (tamper, outage, voltage anomaly)

**Pillar 4 — Meter Data Management System (MDMS)**
- The "brain" of the operation
- Performs **VEE (Validation, Estimation, Editing)** of meter data
- Applies TOU tariffs, calculates bills, peak demand
- Feeds data to: Billing System, Outage Management (OMS), GIS, Analytics, Customer Portal

### 2.2 Measurement Quantities inside a Smart Meter

The metering IC computes the following from instantaneous voltage $v(t)$ and current $i(t)$:

**Instantaneous Power:**
$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$

**Active Power (Real Power):**
$$P = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t)\,i(t)\,dt$$

**Apparent Power:**
$$S = V_{\text{rms}} \cdot I_{\text{rms}}$$

**Reactive Power:**
$$Q = \sqrt{S^2 - P^2}$$

**Power Factor:**
$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S}$$

**Active Energy (kWh) registered:**
$$E_{a} = \int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{T} v(t)\,i(t)\,dt$$

**Reactive Energy (kVARh):**
$$E_{r} = \int_{0}^{T} Q(t)\,dt$$

**Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) — a smart meter speciality:**
$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n}^{2}}}{V_{1}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.3 Time-of-Use (TOU) Pricing — The "Smart" in Billing

Instead of a flat ₹/kWh rate, smart meters register energy into different registers based on time slots:

| Time Slot | Typical Hours | Typical Tariff Multiplier |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak Hours** | 06:00–10:00, 18:00–22:00 | 1.5× base |
| **Normal Hours** | 10:00–18:00, 22:00–24:00 | 1.0× base |
| **Off-Peak Hours** | 00:00–06:00 | 0.7× base |

**TOU Bill calculation:**
$$\text{Bill} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} E_k \cdot R_k$$
where $E_k$ is the energy consumed in slot $k$ (kWh) and $R_k$ is the tariff rate (₹/kWh) for that slot.

### 2.4 Maximum Demand (MD) Recording — A Smart Meter Highlight

Smart meters continuously compute **sliding-window demand** (e.g., 15-min or 30-min block). At the end of each billing cycle, the **highest block** is recorded as MD.

$$\text{MD (kVA or kW)} = \frac{\text{Energy in block (kWh)}}{\text{Block duration (h)}}$$

A typical 30-min block:
$$\text{MD}_{30\,\text{min}} = \frac{1}{0.5}\int_{t}^{t+0.5} P(\tau)\,d\tau$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Unit | Notes / KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instantaneous power | $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$ | W | Foundation of DSP metering |
| 2 | Active power | $P = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t)\,i(t)\,dt$ | W | Used inside metering IC |
| 3 | Apparent power | $S = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}}$ | VA | Always ≥ P |
| 4 | Reactive power | $Q = \sqrt{S^2 - P^2}$ | VAR | Sign convention: +ve lag, -ve lead |
| 5 | Power factor | $\cos\phi = P/S$ | dimensionless | $0 \le \cos\phi \le 1$ |
| 6 | Active energy | $E_a = \int P\,dt$ | kWh | Register reading |
| 7 | Reactive energy | $E_r = \int Q\,dt$ | kVARh | Penalty billing |
| 8 | THD (voltage) | $\sqrt{\sum V_n^2}/V_1$ | % | PQ monitoring |
| 9 | TOU bill | $\sum E_k R_k$ | ₹ | Smart billing |
| 10 | Max demand | $E_{\text{block}}/\Delta t$ | kW or kVA | Recorded in 15/30 min blocks |
| 11 | DC energy stored | $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ | J | For supercap backup |
| 12 | Meter constant | $K = N/(\text{kWh})$ | imp/kWh | LED pulse test |
| 13 | CT ratio | $I_{p}/I_{s}$ | A/A | For CT-operated meters |
| 14 | Apparent energy | $E_s = E_a / \cos\phi$ | kVAh | Used for PF penalty |
| 15 | Communication latency budget | $t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{meas}} + t_{\text{tx}}$ | s | For TOU-critical apps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every item in this table maps directly to a register or alarm event that a real-world smart meter (e.g., L&T, HPL, Genus, Schneider, L+G, Itron) logs and transmits. In KTU examinations, you are expected to *recognise and apply* these formulas, not derive the metering IC's internal DSP algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: RMS Computation Inside a Smart Meter (Sample-by-Sample)

The metering IC samples voltage and current at high frequency (typically 4 kHz–8 kHz) over one fundamental cycle (20 ms at 50 Hz). The RMS values are computed digitally.

**Step 1 — Sample the waveforms:**
$$v[n] = V_{\text{peak}}\sin(\omega n T_s), \quad i[n] = I_{\text{peak}}\sin(\omega n T_s - \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Square and average over N samples per cycle:**
$$V_{\text{rms}}^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v^{2}[n]$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root:**
$$V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v^{2}[n]}$$

**Step 4 — Identical procedure for current:**
$$I_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} i^{2}[n]}$$

> **Logic explanation:** Because $V_{\text{rms}}$ is defined as the *square root of the mean of the square*, the metering IC simply does RMS = sqrt(mean(samples²)) digitally — a clean digitisation of the classic electrodynamic definition.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Active Energy Registration from Instantaneous Power

**Step 1 — Define instantaneous power for each sample:**
$$p[n] = v[n] \cdot i[n]$$

**Step 2 — Active power is the average over one cycle (or integer multiple):**
$$P = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} p[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n]\,i[n]$$

**Step 3 — Active energy accumulated over $M$ cycles:**
$$E_{a} = \sum_{\text{cycles}} P \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$$

**Step 4 — Express in kWh (the register unit):**
$$E_{a,\text{kWh}} = \frac{E_{a,\text{Joules}}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}}$$

> **Logic explanation:** Smart meters integrate $p(t)$ in real time. Each 1 kWh corresponds to $3.6 \times 10^{6}$ joules of energy. The MCU pulses an LED with a defined *meter constant* (e.g., 1000 imp/kWh) for test-bench calibration.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: TOU Bill for a Domestic Consumer

**Given data:**
- Off-peak energy $E_1 = 100$ kWh at $R_1 = 4.0$ ₹/kWh
- Normal energy $E_2 = 150$ kWh at $R_2 = 6.0$ ₹/kWh
- Peak energy $E_3 = 80$ kWh at $R_3 = 8.0$ ₹/kWh
- Fixed charge $F = 100$ ₹
- Tax $T = 5\%$ on energy charge

**Step 1 — Compute energy charge:**
$$E_{\text{charge}} = E_1 R_1 + E_2 R_2 + E_3 R_3$$
$$E_{\text{charge}} = (100)(4.0) + (150)(6.0) + (80)(8.0)$$
$$E_{\text{charge}} = 400 + 900 + 640 = 1940 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 2 — Add fixed charge:**
$$\text{Subtotal} = 1940 + 100 = 2040 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3 — Add tax:**
$$\text{Bill} = 2040 + 0.05 \times 2040 = 2040 + 102 = 2142 \text{ ₹}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Demand Sliding-Window Calculation

**Given:** 4 consecutive 30-min blocks with the following average active powers:
- Block 1: $P_1 = 200$ kW
- Block 2: $P_2 = 350$ kW
- Block 3: $P_3 = 480$ kW
- Block 4: $P_4 = 310$ kW

**Step 1 — Express each block's energy:**
$$E_k = P_k \times \Delta t = P_k \times 0.5 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each block's MD value:**
$$MD_1 = 200 \times 0.5 / 0.5 = 200 \text{ kW}$$
$$MD_2 = 350 \times 0.5 / 0.5 = 350 \text{ kW}$$
$$MD_3 = 480 \times 0.5 / 0.5 = 480 \text{ kW}$$
$$MD_4 = 310 \times 0.5 / 0.5 = 310 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Select the maximum (registered MD):**
$$MD_{\text{registered}} = \max(200, 350, 480, 310) = 480 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — If the utility charges a demand rate of ₹400/kW/month:**
$$\text{Demand Charge} = 480 \times 400 = 1{,}92{,}000 \text{ ₹/month}$$

> **Logic explanation:** A higher MD spikes the demand charge even if total energy consumption is the same — this is why smart meters with interval data help consumers *load-shift* to flatten their demand curve and lower the bill.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Power Factor Penalty Computation

**Given:** A factory draws $P = 500$ kW, $Q = 700$ kVAR during peak hours.

**Step 1 — Compute apparent power:**
$$S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{500^2 + 700^2} = \sqrt{250000 + 490000}$$
$$S = \sqrt{740000} \approx 860.23 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 2 — Compute PF:**
$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{500}{860.23} \approx 0.581 \text{ (lagging)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute kVAh over 1 month (assuming 8 h/day × 30 days = 240 h):**
$$E_s = S \times 240 = 860.23 \times 240 = 2{,}06{,}455 \text{ kVAh}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to kWh for penalty:**
$$E_a = P \times 240 = 500 \times 240 = 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5 — If utility bills on kVAh at ₹8/kVAh:**
$$\text{Bill} = 2{,}06{,}455 \times 8 = ₹16{,}51{,}640$$

> **Logic explanation:** Low PF → higher apparent power → larger kVAh bill. Smart meters allow *automated PF penalty assessment* and help the utility identify consumers for whom capacitor bank installation is necessary.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Smart Meter Data Flow Timeline

| t (event) | Trigger | Smart Meter Action | Network Action | HES / MDMS Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 00:00 | New TOU slot begins | Switch tariff register | — | Logs slot change |
| 00:01, 00:16, 00:31… | 15-min interval | Snapshot $E$, $P$, $V$, $I$, PF | Stores in local memory | — |
| 00:30 | Scheduled push | Bundle interval data | RF/PLC/NB-IoT uplink | Receive, validate, store |
| 00:31 | Tamper event (magnet / cover open) | Log event with timestamp | Immediate push | Raise tamper alarm |
| 00:35 | Outage | Last-gasp packet via supercap | One-shot RF burst | Initiate OMS ticket |
| 06:00 | TOU transition | Register freeze/swap | — | Apply new tariff |
| Daily 02:00 | Scheduled task | OTA firmware check | — | Push firmware if available |
| Monthly 1st | Billing cycle | Push billing snapshot | Bulk transfer | Generate bill via MDMS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Smart Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LV Power Line 240V] --> B[Voltage Divider]
    A --> C[Current Sensor CT]
    B --> D[Signal Conditioning]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Metering IC ADE9000]
    E --> F[MCU Processor]
    F --> G[RTC with Battery]
    F --> H[LCD Display]
    F --> I[Tamper Detection]
    F --> J[Load Switch Relay]
    F --> K[Communication Module]
    K --> L[RF Mesh / PLC / NB-IoT]
    L --> M[Concentrator / Gateway]
    M --> N[Head End System HES]
    N --> O[Meter Data Management MDMS]
    O --> P[Billing System]
    O --> Q[Outage Management OMS]
    O --> R[Customer Portal]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — AMI Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SL1[LAYER 1 HOME AREA NETWORK HAN]
        A1[Smart Meter]
        A2[In Home Display IHD]
        A3[Solar Inverter]
        A4[Smart Appliance]
    end
    subgraph SL2[LAYER 2 NEIGHBOURHOOD AREA NETWORK NAN]
        B1[Concentrator / Gateway]
    end
    subgraph SL3[LAYER 3 WIDE AREA NETWORK WAN]
        C1[Cellular Backhaul]
        C2[Fibre Link]
    end
    subgraph SL4[LAYER 4 UTILITY DATA CENTRE]
        D1[Head End System HES]
        D2[Meter Data Management MDMS]
        D3[Billing Engine]
        D4[Analytics AI]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> A1
    A3 --> A1
    A4 --> A1
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D2 --> D4
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Smart Meter Event Lifecycle (Tamper Detection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Normal Operation] --> B{Sensor Threshold Crossed?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Timestamp the Event]
    C --> D[Log in Non-Volatile Memory]
    D --> E[Generate Tamper Alarm Code]
    E --> F[Push Event to HES over RF/PLC]
    F --> G[HES Validates and Acknowledges]
    G --> H[MDMS Raises Incident]
    H --> I[Utility Engineer Notified]
    I --> J[Resolution and Site Visit]
    J --> K[Event Closure Logged]
    K --> A
```

### 4.4 Comparison Diagram — Communication Technology Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Communication Technology] --> B{Deployment Density}
    B -- High Urban --> C{Range Need}
    B -- Low Rural --> D[Use Cellular NB-IoT or LoRaWAN]
    C -- Short Below 100m --> E[Use ZigBee or Wi-Fi HAN]
    C -- Long Mesh --> F[Use RF Mesh Wi-SUN 865 MHz]
    C -- Through Power Line --> G[Use PLC CENELEC Bands]
    D --> H[Long Range Low Data]
    F --> I[Multi-hop Self-healing]
    G --> J[No New Cabling Needed]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TOU Billing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Smart Meter Registers kWh per slot] --> B[Interval Data Upload to HES]
    B --> C[VEE Validation Estimation Editing]
    C --> D[Apply Tariff Schedule TOU]
    D --> E[Add Fixed Charge]
    E --> F[Add Taxes and Duties]
    F --> G[Add PF Penalty if applicable]
    G --> H[Generate Final Bill]
    H --> I[Customer Portal and SMS Alert]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
Define a *Smart Meter*. List any **four** features that distinguish it from a conventional static energy meter.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *Smart Meter* is an advanced electronic energy meter that, in addition to measuring energy, supports two-way communication, interval data logging, remote connect/disconnect, tamper detection, time-of-use (TOU) tariff handling, and power quality monitoring as part of an Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI).

Four distinguishing features (½ mark each):
1. **Two-way communication** with the utility (RF/PLC/cellular) — conventional meters have no communication.
2. **Interval data recording** (typically every 15 minutes) — conventional meters record only cumulative reading.
3. **TOU tariff handling** — supports multiple tariff registers and time-based pricing.
4. **Tamper and event logging** — electronic detection of bypass, magnet, neutral disturbance, cover open, etc.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)**? How is it different from **AMR**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **AMR (Automated Meter Reading):** A one-way system in which meter data is automatically collected remotely (e.g., by drive-by RF or walk-by) for billing, but no commands/control flow back to the meter.
- **AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure):** A two-way, integrated system of smart meters, communication networks, Head End System (HES), and Meter Data Management System (MDMS) that enables *bidirectional* flow of data and commands (remote connect/disconnect, firmware updates, real-time pricing, outage detection, etc.).
- AMI = AMR + two-way control + analytics + integration with OMS, GIS, billing, and customer portals.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Smart Meter Architecture & Communication [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a smart meter**. List the functions of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram. Describe the following blocks:)*

| Block | Function (½ mark each, total 7) |
|---|---|
| **Voltage Divider** | Steps down 240 V to low voltage level (e.g., < 5 V) for metering IC input; provides galvanic isolation via resistor chain. |
| **Current Sensor (CT / Shunt / Rogowski)** | Senses load current; CT provides isolation, shunt provides DC accuracy, Rogowski for high current. |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Anti-aliasing low-pass filter + amplifier + ADC driver; prepares signals for metering IC. |
| **Metering IC (e.g., ADE9000)** | Performs DSP-based computation of $V_{\text{rms}}$, $I_{\text{rms}}$, $P$, $Q$, $S$, PF, energy integration; outputs digital pulses. |
| **MCU** | Master controller; runs TOU logic, calendar, billing algorithms, communication stack, tamper processing, display driving. |
| **RTC + Battery** | Maintains calendar and time during power outage; ensures TOU slot transitions are correct. |
| **LCD Display** | Shows cumulative kWh, instantaneous kW, voltage, current, PF, alerts, and tariff slot. |
| **Tamper Detection** | Monitors cover open, magnetic field, neutral missing, earth loading, reverse current; logs events. |
| **Load Switch (Relay/Contactor)** | Used for remote connect/disconnect of consumer load on utility command. |
| **Communication Module** | RF / PLC / Cellular modem for HES uplink/downlink. |

**(b)** Compare the **communication technologies** used in AMI (PLC, RF Mesh, Cellular/NB-IoT, LoRaWAN) on the basis of medium, range, data rate, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Comparison Table — 4 marks for table, 3 marks for discussion):**

| Technology | Medium | Range | Data Rate | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PLC** | LV power line (CENELEC A/B/C/D) | 1–3 km | 5–500 kbps | Urban dense areas, no new cabling |
| **RF Mesh (ZigBee / Wi-SUN)** | 865–867 MHz RF | 100 m – 1 km/hop | 50–300 kbps | Multi-hop self-healing urban mesh |
| **Cellular (GPRS / 4G / NB-IoT)** | Telecom network | Several km | 50 kbps – 10 Mbps | Rural / sparse / individual meters |
| **LoRaWAN** | Sub-GHz RF (868/915 MHz) | 2–10 km | 0.3–50 kbps | Long-range low-rate, battery meters |

**Discussion (3 marks):**
- *PLC* is best when no new telecom infrastructure is desired but is sensitive to line noise and load impedance variations.
- *RF Mesh* (e.g., Wi-SUN at 865 MHz, India) is robust and self-healing but requires careful RF planning; ideal for urban India under IS 16444.
- *Cellular* (NB-IoT) is fast to deploy but incurs recurring SIM/data costs.
- *LoRaWAN* is best for long-range, low-duty-cycle applications but has limited bandwidth — unsuitable for high-frequency interval data.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — TOU Tariff & Maximum Demand [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Time-of-Use (TOU) tariff** in smart metering. Compute the bill for the following consumer with the given consumption pattern. **(7 marks)**

Given:
- Off-peak: $E_1 = 120$ kWh at $R_1 = 3.5$ ₹/kWh
- Normal: $E_2 = 200$ kWh at $R_2 = 5.5$ ₹/kWh
- Peak: $E_3 = 100$ kWh at $R_3 = 7.5$ ₹/kWh
- Fixed charge = ₹150
- Electricity duty = 10% on total

**Model Answer:**
**TOU Concept (3 marks):**
Time-of-Use (TOU) tariff is a *time-dependent pricing* mechanism in which the per-unit energy cost varies with the time of day (peak, normal, off-peak). Smart meters with RTC and multiple registers enable this. The objective is to incentivise consumers to *shift load* from peak to off-peak hours, flattening the utility's load curve and reducing the need for peaking power plants.

**Computation (4 marks):**

Step 1 — Energy charge:
$$E_c = E_1 R_1 + E_2 R_2 + E_3 R_3$$
$$E_c = (120)(3.5) + (200)(5.5) + (100)(7.5)$$
$$E_c = 420 + 1100 + 750 = 2270 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 2 — Add fixed charge:
$$\text{Subtotal} = 2270 + 150 = 2420 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 3 — Apply electricity duty:
$$\text{Final Bill} = 2420 + 0.10 \times 2420 = 2420 + 242 = 2662 \text{ ₹}$$

**Total Bill = ₹2662**

---

**(b)** Define **Maximum Demand (MD)** in a smart meter. A factory's 30-min block average active powers over 6 hours are: 280, 350, 410, 390, 450, 320 kW. Determine (i) the registered MD, and (ii) the demand charge if the rate is ₹450/kW/month. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
**MD Definition (2 marks):**
Maximum Demand is the *highest value of average power* drawn by a consumer over a predefined integrating period (typically 15 or 30 minutes) during a billing cycle. Smart meters compute it as a sliding or block-window average. The MD value is "frozen" at the end of the cycle and is used by utilities to levy a *demand charge*, encouraging consumers to maintain a flatter load profile.

**Computation (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Identify maximum block power from given data:
$$P_{\text{blocks}} = \{280, 350, 410, 390, 450, 320\} \text{ kW}$$

Step 2 — Since each block is 30 min, MD in kW = block average (in kW):
$$MD_{\text{registered}} = \max\{280, 350, 410, 390, 450, 320\} = 450 \text{ kW}$$

Step 3 — Demand charge calculation:
$$\text{Demand Charge} = 450 \text{ kW} \times 450 \text{ ₹/kW}$$
$$\boxed{\text{Demand Charge} = ₹2{,}02{,}500 \text{ per month}}$$

Step 4 (1 mark) — Energy drawn in 6 hours:
$$E = (280+350+410+390+450+320) \times 0.5 = 2200 \times 0.5 = 1100 \text{ kWh}$$
(Useful to show the consumer is paying ₹2,02,500 as a *fixed demand* charge even though actual energy is only 1100 kWh — illustrating the incentive to flatten demand.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in MD** — Always state "kW" or "kVA" explicitly. A bare number loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing AMR with AMI** — Many students write "smart meter = AMR." Wrong. Smart Metering = AMI (two-way).
> 3. **In TOU questions, applying a *single* flat rate** — the entire 3-mark concept is *time-based* tariffs. Read slot-wise.
> 4. **In block diagrams, leaving blocks unlabelled** — every block must have a *function* (½ mark each).
> 5. **Confusing PLC (Power Line Communication) with PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** — KTU examiners deduct marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning IS 16444 / IEC 62056 standards** in definitions — a common 1-mark loss.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Smart Meter = electronic meter + 2-way communication + interval data + TOU/tamper/PQ features.**
- [ ] **AMI ≠ AMR** — AMI is two-way and integrated; AMR is one-way read-only.
- [ ] Four AMI pillars: **Smart Meter → Communication → HES → MDMS**.
- [ ] Three network layers: **HAN (home) → NAN (neighbourhood) → WAN (utility).**
- [ ] Indian standard: **IS 16444 (smart meter hardware)** + **IS 15959 (data exchange protocol)** + **IEC 62056 (COSEM/DLMS)**.
- [ ] Communication options: **PLC, RF Mesh (865 MHz), Cellular (NB-IoT/4G), LoRaWAN, ZigBee.**
- [ ] Key formulas: $P = \frac{1}{T}\int v(t)i(t)\,dt$, $S = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}}$, $Q = \sqrt{S^2 - P^2}$, $\cos\phi = P/S$.
- [ ] **TOU bill** = $\sum E_k R_k$; **MD** = $\max(\text{sliding 15/30-min block average})$; **THD** = $\sqrt{\sum V_n^2}/V_1$.
- [ ] Meter registers active energy (kWh), reactive energy (kVARh), apparent energy (kVAh), and max demand (kW/kVA).
- [ ] Smart meters also do **last-gasp outage reporting**, **tamper logging**, and **OTA firmware updates**.
- [ ] India's **NSMP (National Smart Meter Programme)** under RDSS targets 250+ million smart meters — the largest smart-meter roll-out globally.
- [ ] BEE 5-star labelling exists for smart meters; Class 1.0 accuracy is the typical Indian mandate.
- [ ] PF penalty is automated using $E_s = E_a / \cos\phi$ when $\cos\phi < 0.9$ (typical industrial threshold).
- [ ] Always quote units (kW, kVA, kWh, kVAh, kVARh, ₹, %THD) in KTU answers for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bidirectional Meters (Description Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT411 - ELECTRICAL MEASUREMENT AND MEASURING INSTRUMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bidirectional Meters (Description Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Bidirectional Meter** is an electrical measuring instrument designed to sense, indicate, and record the magnitude as well as the **direction (polarity/sense)** of an electrical quantity — typically energy, power, current, or voltage — flowing through a circuit. Unlike a conventional *unidirectional* meter, which assumes a fixed reference direction of flow, a bidirectional meter resolves **two independent quadrants** of measurement: **forward (import/positive)** and **reverse (export/negative)**.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the term most commonly refers to **bidirectional energy meters** (also called *four-quadrant meters* when applied to power/energy) used at the **point of common coupling (PCC)** of distributed generation systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme classifies this as a *Description Only* topic. No numerical problem-solving is expected in the University Exam. Focus must remain on **construction, operating principle, quadrant identification, and engineering applications**.

### 1.2 The Four-Quadrant Concept (Foundation of Bidirectionality)

Any AC electrical quantity (power $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$) can operate in four quadrants depending on the polarity of $V$ and $I$:

| Quadrant | Voltage Polarity | Current Polarity | Power Sign | Practical Meaning |
|----------|------------------|------------------|------------|--------------------|
| **Q1**   | +ve              | +ve              | +ve (Import) | Normal consumption |
| **Q2**   | +ve              | -ve              | -ve (Export) | Regenerative braking |
| **Q3**   | -ve              | -ve              | +ve (Import) | Reverse reference |
| **Q4**   | -ve              | +ve              | -ve (Export) | Capacitive leading load |

A **true bidirectional meter** must correctly resolve and display all four.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Toll Both Ways" Booth

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a highway toll booth. A *unidirectional meter* is like a one-way toll that only counts cars entering a city. A **bidirectional meter** is like a smart toll that tracks cars *entering* **and** cars *leaving* — and even distinguishes between them with two separate counters.

For a homeowner with rooftop solar:
- During daytime ☀️: Solar panels **export** surplus power to the grid → meter counts **kWh exported**.
- During nighttime 🌙: House **imports** power from the grid → meter counts **kWh imported**.

The bidirectional meter independently registers both and computes *net energy*.

### 1.4 Why Bidirectional Meters Exist (Engineering Need)

Before the rise of **Distributed Energy Resources (DERs)**, all meters were unidirectional. The proliferation of:
- Rooftop solar PV systems
- Wind micro-turbines
- Regenerative electric vehicle drives
- Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS)
- HVDC back-to-back links

…made the conventional meter **economically and technically obsolete**, because it could not credit the consumer for power *fed back* into the grid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** Modern bidirectional meters comply with **IS 13779 / IEC 62053-22 / IEC 62053-23** for accuracy class **0.2S, 0.5S, or 1.0**, with a typical measurement range of **0.1% to 120% of $I_b$** (basic current) in both directions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — How a Bidirectional Meter "Knows" the Direction

The direction sensitivity of a meter is derived from one of three physical principles:

**Principle A — Polarity of Deflecting Torque (Electromechanical Meters)**
In an electrodynamometer wattmeter, the deflection is governed by the *product* of the currents in the fixed and moving coils. If either current reverses, the **sign of the deflecting torque reverses**, causing the pointer to deflect in the opposite direction. A **centre-zero scale** (zero in the middle) allows the user to read both positive and negative deflections.

**Principle B — Phase Discrimination (Induction Type Energy Meters)**
Induction-type energy meters use a phase-shifting flux network. A bidirectional induction meter employs **two aluminum discs mounted on a common shaft**, each with its own driving mechanism oriented for opposite rotation directions. A differential gear train sums or differentiates the two rotations, and a **two-register mechanism** records energy in each direction separately.

**Principle C — Sign Detection in DSP (Static / Electronic Meters)**
Modern static bidirectional meters sample voltage and current via ADCs, multiply them in the digital domain using a **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)**, and then evaluate the sign of the instantaneous product:
$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$

If $\int p(t)\,dt > 0$ over a billing interval → **import (forward)**.
If $\int p(t)\,dt < 0$ → **export (reverse)**.

Two separate energy registers (cumulative counters) accumulate these values independently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The description-only nature of the syllabus means examiners expect you to **name the principle, identify the constructional feature**, and **state the application** — not to derive transfer functions.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (For Descriptive Context Only)

| Concept | Symbolic Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|--------------|
| Instantaneous Power | $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$ | Direction depends on sign of product | **W (watts)** |
| Average Active Power | $P = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi$ | Sign indicates flow direction | **W** |
| Active Energy | $E = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} P(t)\, dt$ | Sign gives import/export | **kWh** |
| Reactive Energy | $Q = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \sin\phi$ | Sign gives inductive/capacitive | **kVARh** |
| Net Energy | $E_{net} = E_{import} - E_{export}$ | Algebraic sum | **kWh** |
| Quadrant Identification | $\text{sgn}(P) = \text{sgn}(V) \cdot \text{sgn}(I)$ | Determines Q1/Q2/Q3/Q4 | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Bidirectional meters are now mandatory under the **Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission (KSERC) net-metering regulations** for all grid-tied solar installations below 1 MW capacity in Kerala.

### 2.3 Classification of Bidirectional Meters (Based on KTU Coverage)

**1. Bidirectional DC Ammeters / Voltmeters**
- Use a **centre-zero moving coil** with a polarity-reversing switch, OR
- Use a **bridge rectifier** with the meter on the DC side so that the instrument always sees a unipolar input regardless of terminal polarity.
- Application: Battery charging/discharging systems.

**2. Bidirectional Electrodynamometer Wattmeters**
- Centre-zero scale; moving coil senses product of fixed-coil current and moving-coil voltage-derived current.
- Application: Laboratory measurement of power flow in bidirectional drives.

**3. Bidirectional Induction-Type Energy Meters**
- Two-disc construction with differential gearing; OR
- Single-disc with two driving systems phase-displaced by **180°**.
- Application: Traditional electromechanical net-metering installations.

**4. Bidirectional Electronic (Static) Energy Meters**
- Based on **Hall-effect sensors** for current sensing, **resistive divider** for voltage, and **DSP-based computation**.
- Two cumulative registers + one **net register**.
- Application: Modern smart grids, **IS 15944 / IEC 62056 (DLMS/COSEM)** compliant meters.

### 2.4 Comparison Matrix (High-Yield Table)

| Feature | Unidirectional Meter | Bidirectional Meter |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Number of Registers | 1 | 2 (Import + Export) or 3 (+ Net) |
| Direction Indication | Not provided | Centre-zero / LCD sign / separate counter |
| Operating Quadrants | Only Q1 | All four (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4) |
| Sign of Deflection | Always one side | Both sides of zero |
| Typical Construction | Single coil/disc | Twin coil/disc or DSP-based |
| Application | Conventional loads | DERs, EV charging, regenerative drives |
| Cost | Lower | **20–40% higher** due to additional sensing |
| Standards | IS 13779, IEC 62053-21 | IS 13779 + IS 15944, IEC 62053-22/23 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive Note:** Since the KTU 2024 scheme marks this topic as **"Description Only,"** the following section develops the **operational logic and sign-analysis flow** rather than heavy mathematical derivations. This mirrors the depth expected in the KTU university examination answer sheet.

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — How the Sign of Power is Determined

The decision flow inside a bidirectional energy meter follows this logic chain:

**Step 1 — Voltage Sign Detection**

The meter's voltage sensing circuit (resistive divider + ADC, or PT + rectifier) produces a signal proportional to the instantaneous line-to-neutral voltage. The polarity of $v(t)$ at the *metering terminals* is compared against a fixed internal reference (typically the *supply* terminal as positive).

**Step 2 — Current Sign Detection**

A **Hall-effect sensor** (preferred in static meters) or a **current transformer (CT) with phase discriminator** (electromechanical) produces a signal proportional to the instantaneous current. The polarity convention is: current *flowing from grid → load* is **positive** (import), and *load → grid* is **negative** (export).

**Step 3 — Instantaneous Product Formation**

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$

For a sampled-data system operating at sampling rate $f_s$:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} v[k] \cdot i[k]$$

where $N$ is the number of samples per fundamental cycle (typically **N = 256** for a 50 Hz system sampled at 12.8 kHz).

**Step 4 — Sign-Based Register Allocation**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } P_{avg} > 0 &: \text{ Energy} \rightarrow \text{IMPORT register (Cumulative Import)} \\
\text{If } P_{avg} < 0 &: \text{ Energy} \rightarrow \text{EXPORT register (Cumulative Export)} \\
\text{Net Energy} &= E_{import} - E_{export}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Display / Communication**

The meter's display or DLMS/COSEM communication stack reports all three values to the utility's billing system.

### 3.2 Operating Principle of a Two-Disc Induction-Type Bidirectional Meter

This is the classical electromechanical construction still found in some KTU exam questions:

- **Disc A** is driven by a flux system arranged so that its torque $T_A \propto +VI\cos\phi$ (positive for forward power).
- **Disc B** is driven by a flux system **phase-shifted by 180°**, so its torque $T_B \propto -VI\cos\phi$ (negative for forward power).
- Both discs are mounted on a **common shaft**, so the net shaft torque is:

$$T_{shaft} = T_A + T_B$$

But because the discs are *physically separate* and geared to **two independent register trains**, each register records the energy proportional to *its own* disc rotation — one for import, one for export. The **net register** is driven by a **differential gear** that subtracts the two.

### 3.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Reasoning)

The decision-making chain within a bidirectional electronic meter is best understood as the following sequential logic:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sensed } v(t) &\xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} v[k] \\
\text{Sensed } i(t) &\xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} i[k] \\
\{v[k], i[k]\} &\xrightarrow{\text{Multiplier}} p[k] = v[k] \cdot i[k] \\
p[k] &\xrightarrow{\text{Integrator}} E_{interval} \\
\text{sgn}(E_{interval}) &\xrightarrow{\text{Selector}} \text{Import Register} \;\vert\; \text{Export Register} \\
\text{Both Registers} &\xrightarrow{\text{Subtractor}} E_{net}
\end{aligned}$$

This sequence makes it clear that the **direction of energy flow is NOT a physical reversal of any moving part** in a static meter — it is a *computational* decision based on the algebraic sign of the integrated power.

### 3.4 Worked Example (Descriptive Reasoning Style)

> **Scenario:** A house has a 5 kW rooftop solar system. Over a 30-day billing cycle:
> - Daytime average power exported = +3.2 kW for 6 hours/day × 30 days = **576 kWh exported**.
> - Nighttime average power imported = −1.1 kW for 14 hours/day × 30 days = **462 kWh imported**.

**Bidirectional meter reading after 30 days:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{import} &= 462 \text{ kWh} \\
E_{export} &= 576 \text{ kWh} \\
E_{net} &= 462 - 576 = -114 \text{ kWh} \;\;\text{(Net export — credit due)}
\end{aligned}$$

The utility pays (or credits) the consumer for **114 kWh** at the prevailing tariff. A unidirectional meter would have either ignored the export entirely (under-recording) or even shown net reverse rotation (causing billing errors).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since bidirectional meters are a *description-only* topic, the diagrams below emphasize **functional architecture and operating flow** rather than detailed mechanical drawings.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Bidirectional Energy Meter (Static Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Line Conductor L] --> nodeB[CT Current Sensor]
    nodeA --> nodeC[VT or Resistive Divider]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Signal Conditioning]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[16-bit ADC Sampler]
    nodeE --> nodeF[DSP Multiplier and Integrator]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Sign of Energy}
    nodeG -- Positive --> nodeH[IMPORT Register kWh]
    nodeG -- Negative --> nodeI[EXPORT Register kWh]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Net Energy Subtracter]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[LCD Display Module]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[DLMS or COSEM Optical Port]
```

### 4.2 Quadrant Identification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Acquire v t and i t samples] --> nodeCheckV{Is v positive}
    nodeCheckV -- Yes --> nodeCheckI1{Is i positive}
    nodeCheckV -- No --> nodeCheckI2{Is i positive}
    nodeCheckI1 -- Yes --> nodeQ1[Quadrant 1 - Import Inductive]
    nodeCheckI1 -- No --> nodeQ2[Quadrant 2 - Export Capacitive]
    nodeCheckI2 -- Yes --> nodeQ4[Quadrant 4 - Export Inductive]
    nodeCheckI2 -- No --> nodeQ3[Quadrant 3 - Import Capacitive]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeOut[Increment appropriate register]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeOut
    nodeQ3 --> nodeOut
    nodeQ4 --> nodeOut
```

### 4.3 Functional Comparison — Unidirectional vs Bidirectional Sensing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Unidirectional [Unidirectional Meter]
        nodeU1[Single CT] --> nodeU2[Single ADC Channel]
        nodeU2 --> nodeU3[Unsigned Accumulator]
        nodeU3 --> nodeU4[Single Register kWh]
    end
    subgraph Bidirectional [Bidirectional Meter]
        nodeB1[Dual Polarity CT or Hall Sensor] --> nodeB2[Dual ADC or Signed Sample]
        nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Signed Multiplier and Integrator]
        nodeB3 --> nodeB4{Sign Branch}
        nodeB4 -- Positive --> nodeB5[Import Register]
        nodeB4 -- Negative --> nodeB6[Export Register]
        nodeB5 --> nodeB7[Net Energy Subtracter]
        nodeB6 --> nodeB7
    end
```

### 4.4 Application-Centric Block Diagram — Net Metering Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePV[Solar PV Array DC Source] --> nodeINV[Grid Tie Inverter]
    nodeINV --> nodePCC[Point of Common Coupling PCC]
    nodeLOAD[Domestic Load] --> nodePCC
    nodeGRID[Utility Grid] --> nodePCC
    nodePCC --> nodeMTR[Bidirectional Net Meter]
    nodeMTR --> nodeDISP[LCD showing kWh Import and kWh Export]
    nodeMTR --> nodeCLOUD[Cloud Data Logger via DLMS]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define a bidirectional meter. Why is it preferred over a conventional meter in grid-connected solar PV installations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A bidirectional meter is an electrical measuring instrument capable of **sensing and recording the magnitude and direction of energy flow** in a circuit. In a grid-connected solar PV system, the consumer is both a *load* (drawing power from the grid at night) and a *source* (feeding surplus solar power into the grid during the day). A conventional unidirectional meter can register only one of these flows, leading to incorrect billing. A bidirectional meter has **two separate registers** — one for imported kWh and one for exported kWh — and computes the **net energy**, enabling accurate net-metering. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Mention any **three differences** between a unidirectional and a bidirectional energy meter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Unidirectional Meter | Bidirectional Meter |
|---|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Single cumulative register for kWh | Two registers — Import and Export (plus Net) |
| 2 | Cannot record reverse power flow | Records both forward and reverse power flow |
| 3 | One sign of deflection (always positive) | Centre-zero scale or signed DSP register |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 3]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and operating principle of a bidirectional electronic (static) energy meter**. Discuss its application in net-metering for rooftop solar installations. List the relevant IS / IEC standards.*

#### **Part (a) — Construction and Block Diagram (7 marks)**

A bidirectional static energy meter consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Voltage Sensing Circuit** — A precision resistive divider (or a small voltage transformer, PT) scales the line voltage (e.g., 230 V) to a low-level signal (typically **0–5 V**) suitable for the ADC. **(1 mark)**

2. **Current Sensing Circuit** — A **current transformer (CT)** for AC, or a **Hall-effect sensor** for bidirectional DC measurement, produces a voltage proportional to the line current. Hall sensors are preferred because they inherently detect *direction* via the polarity of the output voltage. **(1 mark)**

3. **Signal Conditioning and ADC** — Anti-aliasing filters, op-amp gain stages, and a 16-bit sigma-delta ADC sample both $v(t)$ and $i(t)$ at typically **3.2 kHz to 12.8 kHz** (per IEC 62053-22). **(1 mark)**

4. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — Performs:
   - $p[k] = v[k] \cdot i[k]$ (instantaneous power)
   - $P = \frac{1}{N}\sum p[k]$ (average active power)
   - $E = \sum P \cdot \Delta t$ (energy accumulation)
   - Sign evaluation: $\text{sgn}(E) \rightarrow$ import or export register. **(2 marks)**

5. **Registers and Display** — Two non-volatile EEPROM registers (import, export), one net register, and an LCD or seven-segment display. **(1 mark)**

6. **Communication Interface** — Optical port per **IEC 62056-21** or DLMS/COSEM protocol for AMR (Automatic Meter Reading). **(1 mark)**

#### **Part (b) — Net-Metering Application and Standards (7 marks)**

**Net-Metering Operation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{At noon (solar peak)}: \quad & P_{PV} = 4.5\;\text{kW}, \quad P_{load} = 1.0\;\text{kW} \\
& P_{export} = 3.5\;\text{kW} \;\rightarrow\; \text{EXPORT register increments at 3.5 kW} \\
\text{At night}: \quad & P_{PV} = 0\;\text{kW}, \quad P_{load} = 0.8\;\text{kW} \\
& P_{import} = 0.8\;\text{kW} \;\rightarrow\; \text{IMPORT register increments at 0.8 kW} \\
\text{Billable energy} &= E_{import} - E_{export} \;\;\text{(net)} \;\;\textbf{(3 marks)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Advantages in Net-Metering:**
- Eliminates need for two separate meters.
- Enables *time-of-use (ToU)* tariff differentiation.
- Provides real-time monitoring for consumer awareness. **(2 marks)**

**Applicable Standards:**
- **IS 13779** — AC static watt-hour meters, class 1 and 2.
- **IS 15944** — Specification for bidirectional meters.
- **IEC 62053-22** — Active energy, accuracy classes 0.2S / 0.5S / 1.0.
- **IEC 62053-23** — Reactive energy.
- **IEC 62056 (DLMS/COSEM)** — Communication protocol. **(2 marks)**

---

### **OR**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

*Explain with necessary diagrams the **principle of operation of a bidirectional induction-type energy meter** using a two-disc construction. How does it register forward and reverse energy separately? Mention its limitations compared to a static bidirectional meter.*

#### **Part (a) — Construction and Two-Disc Principle (7 marks)**

A bidirectional induction energy meter uses **two aluminum discs mounted on a common spindle**:

- **Disc A** has a driving system whose torque is $T_A = K \cdot VI\cos\phi$ — proportional to the *forward* power flow.
- **Disc B** is driven by a flux system whose driving voltage is **phase-displaced by 180°** relative to Disc A. Its torque is $T_B = -K \cdot VI\cos\phi$ — proportional to the *reverse* power flow.
- A **differential gear train** at the base of the spindle computes the net rotation, while **two separate register trains** (mechanical odometer-style counters) record the rotations of each disc independently. **(4 marks)**

**Operating Logic:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{When power flows forward (import)}: &\quad T_A \text{ dominates} \rightarrow \text{Import register advances} \\
\text{When power flows reverse (export)}: &\quad T_B \text{ dominates} \rightarrow \text{Export register advances}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks)** for the logic of operation.

**Block / Mechanical Layout:**
- Voltage coil → produces flux $\phi_v$ phase-shifted by 90° from $V$.
- Current coil → produces flux $\phi_i$ in phase with $I$.
- Eddy currents induced in disc interact with these fluxes to produce torque.
- Aluminium disc + damping magnet + register train. **(1 mark)**

#### **Part (b) — Limitations vs Static Meter (7 marks)**
